File.Name: 7515b_7512-002.jpg
Notes: Tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hiding in a Chive (Allium schoenoprasum) flower in the garden. It's early morning, and this spider is not yet ready for business. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2007)

File.Name: 7516a_7512-022.jpg
Notes: Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a flower of non-native Thyme (Thymus vulgaris or kin in the Lamiaceae family) in the garden - and vice-versa! The bee is getting nectar from the flower, but you can see the flower depositing almost-white pollen on the bee's head from it's lavender stamens. The bee gets a meal, and the flower spreads its pollen. This is the kind of "mutual aid" economics that I admire and try to support. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2007)
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Thymus vulgaris or kin in the Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Thymus vulgaris or kin in the Lamiaceae
Families: Thymus vulgaris or kin in the Lamiaceae

File.Name: 7516b_7512-007.jpg
Notes:
This is the fanciful caterpillar of a Western Tussock Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Lymantriidae, genus Orgyia). They make me think of little clown choo-choo trains with those bright colors and toothbrush-like tufts! They look funny, but they do real damage to many plants in the woods. This one is munching a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the canyon. I've been finding the caterpillars everywhere lately, even in my car. The adult moths are nondescript, and the females are wingless. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2007)

I had trouble reducing this photo to fotolog size. Whatever I do, the bristles look pixelated, but the original crop is almost full frame, about 2500 pixels across. Up close, the bristles look like little rainbows - it's a refraction problem! I wonder if this helps confuse preditors as well as photographers.
CommonNames: Western Tussock Moth; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 7517a_7517-130.jpg
Notes:
Elegant flower of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) - a kind of upside-fown Mariposa Lily in the Liliaceae family. Put your hand together to make a cup. Then add one more hand to complete the circle. That's how these flowers are made. There are outward-pointing fringes on the petals, but there are inward-pointing hairs inside that make it easy for an insect to enter between the petals, but hard to leave without picking up lots of pollen. Nature is tricky like that! (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2007)

Last year we had dozens of these flowering plants on the dry native side of our garden, but in this drought year we only have a few plants with flowers. This will be a tough summer for our local critters and wildflowers. I'm ready to document what happens in this drought year.

This butterfly took off from the flower just as I took the photo. It's wings are moving so fast that it became a ghost image, but you can still make it out if you look close. It hardly matters, but this is a Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly working the non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) flowers in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2007)

File.Name: 7518b_7513-066.jpg
Notes:
A fly is a fly, of course, of course... but there are so many different kinds!

I guess this is a furry Bee Fly (order Diptera, family Bombyliidae). It's working the non-native Coreopsis (family Asteraceae) flowers in the garden. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2007)

File.Name: 7518c_7505-116.jpg
Notes:
I guess this is a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae, order Diptera) - maybe genus Gymnosoma or kin perched on the dais of a non-native flower bud of a Coreopsis flower (family Asteraceae) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2007)

These Coreopsis flowers in the garden have been attracting many insects from the woods in this drought year. Single flowers have petals on top clasped by sepals at the base. Composite flowers like these have heads of many tiny flowers, so they have sepal-like phyllaries to hold the flower head together. These composites have phyllaries in 2 series. The outer are thick and spreading, and the inner are membranous and cover the flowers like wrapping paper. The double phyllaries are distinctive. See my [Next] photo.
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Gymnosoma; Coreopsis; Asteraceae; phyllaries
associatedTaxa: Tachinidae; Diptera; Gymnosoma; Coreopsis; Asteraceae; phyllaries

This skipper butterfly is not heavy enough to bring down the stamens for pollination. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2007)
ScientificName: Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae; Lepidoptera; Hesperiinae; Hesperia

File.Name: 7519b_7519-047.jpg

File.Name: 7519c_7519-024.jpg
Notes: I believe this is a California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly working the new flowers of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rose (Rosaceae) family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2007)/

File.Name: 7520a_7519-054.jpg
Notes:
A choo-choo train of mating Buprestid (family Buprestidae in the order Coleoptera, maybe genus Acmaeodera) beetles the non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) flowers in the garden. It feels like spring, and the critters are frisky! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2007)

I'm very busy working on my school slide show, and I'm off on a San Francisco road trip tomorrow with our eighth grade graduating kids. Then comes the slide show and graduation. I'll do what I can for photos when I return. *Sigh* Almost summer!

File.Name: 7520b_7519-034.jpg
Notes: I guess this is a Dance Fly in the family Empididae of the order Diptera working a native flower of Sticky Cinquefoil (Potentilla glandulosa) in the in the Rosaceae family growing in the canyon. The stamens look like little Lolita lollipops! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2007)

File.Name: 7520c_7519-031.jpg
Notes: I'm pretty sure this is a Sawfly (family Tenthredinidae of the order Hymenoptera). It's snared by a spider web on a native flower of Sticky Cinquefoil (Potentilla glandulosa) in the Rosaceae family frowing near the creek. (That's a blackberry vine in the background). Sawflies are interesting wasps that do NOT have constricted abdomens. This might be one of the wasps that makes galls on the nearby willow trees. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2007)

File.Name: 7520d_7519-015.jpg

It's feeding on a Phacelia distans or maybe P. cicutaria in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family growing in the canyon. The adults look very different. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2007)

File.Name: 7520e_7519-011.jpg

File.Name: 7526a_7521-021.jpg
Notes:
Young Pacific Rattlesnake (Crotalus oreganus?) hiding on the bark of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). This is real "cryptic camouflage"! Don't wait to find the rattle of these venemous snakes. The diiamond shape of the head is distinctive. (Hidden Villa Hostel in Los Altos, California)

I had a busy week in the San Francisco region. Now I'm even more busy making our school slide show for next Thursday!

Count the antennae and divide by two!

These butterflies get frisky when they are so close together - are they fighting or flirting? See my [Previous] photo for a sharper shot of the same butterflies. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2007)

File.Name: 7527c_7527-038.jpg
Notes: Brilliant Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working a composite flower in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in the garden - maybe a horticultural Sneezeweed (genus Helenium). (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2007)

File.Name: 7528a_7527-048.jpg
Notes:
Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working non-natiive Fuchsia flowers (Onagraceae) in the garden. The butterfly seems to be cheating the flowers. Instead of going up through the throat of the flower to pick up pollen, it's working its proboscis "under the skirts" behind the petals to go right to the good stuff. Many insects know this trick for many flowers. But I watched this butterfly for a while, and I think it really was delivering pollen with it's large wings. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2007)

I thought we lost this garden fuchsia in the hard frost last winter, but we followed good advice and didn't trim it at all. After looking dead for two months, we started to see buds, and now it's full of flowers. Give nature a chance before taking drastic action!

File.Name: 7529a_7527-074.jpg
Notes: Uncommon male Northern White Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working a composite flower in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in the garden. Skippers are moth-like butterflies that always have that curved "apiculus" extension on the tip of their antennae. There are many black and orange skippers that look alike, but this one is distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2007)

File.Name: 7530a_7526-018.jpg
Notes: I believe this is a Gold Hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum) butterfly resting on a Prickley Pear (Opuntia sp.) cactus in the garden. I haven't noticed this little butterfly before. It rubs its wings like any hairstreak, but it has plain brown colors and very short tails. Interesting common name! (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2007)

File.Name: 7531a_7527-023.jpg
Notes:
Sylvan Hairstreak (Satyrium sylvinus) butterfly working the non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) flowers in the garden. Note the butterfly's long hindwing tails in the upper-left. Also note the missing chunk from its hindwing. I see many butterflies missing pieces of their wings with scissor-cut edges. I reckon these are scars of near escapes from birds. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2007)

See my [Next] photo for a close shot of this butterfly.

File.Name: 7601a_7527-029.jpg
Notes:
Looking very close at a little Sylvan Hairstreak (Satyrium sylvinus) butterfly working the non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) flowers in the garden. See my [Previous] photo for another shot of the whole butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2007)

I've been very busy with end-of-the-year school stuff for the last few weeks. We had our school slide show last night, and graduation today. I don't just photograph butterflies and flowers! I made a wonderful school slide show of 1,850 photos of mostly my photos in 22 segments with music. I organize the segments and then work with my video friend Nick to turn them into a 58 minute DVD using Final Cut Pro software. I wish I could share it all here. So many photos!

I'm exhausted. It will be a great pleasure to go for a quiet walk in the canyon with my camera tomorrow. It's summer!

File.Name: 7602a_7602-014.jpg
Notes:
Pretty Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in "tent position" with wings spread wide like a solar collector trying to warm up as the morning clouds burn off. I'm down low with my camera, with the sun in back. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2007)

I've been busy with school stuff, so it was a pleasure to walk in the woods with my camera today! My photos are all similar checkerspot and fritillary butterflies. This is a drought year with only 1/3 normal rainful as we head into our hot dry summer. Midsummer flowers like Toyon and milkweed are already appearing a month or more early. This will be an interesting summer! I'll do my best to document it with my camera.

The bold patterns and subtle colors are remarkable, like ancient artwork. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2007)

I never did get a look at the underside to see if it's silvered.

I took my walk in the morning, then spent the afternoon reading school lab books. I hope to be done with school stuff tomorrow. I'm ready for summer!

File.Name: 7605a_7604-072.jpg
Notes:
This is an intimate moment between this Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) and a new flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family growing on the dry side of our garden. See my [Previous] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2007)

This flower has been open for a while. This flower has been open for a while. The pale yellow male stamens are done releasing pollen, and the lower petal is no longer wound up like a spring that the bee has to hold down to get to the nectar. But look close to see that the violet female-receptive style has moved in to make gentle contact with the bee's abdomen to hopefully receive pollen from other flowers and other plants that the bee has visited. This is an ongoing sexual dance between flower and insect that is happening all the time if you pause to notice. Ain't nature grand!

Hah - I just noticed from my photos that this flower is a "righty" - the female style comes from the right side of the flower - but the [Previous] two flowers from the same plant are lefties. I'll look closer at this plant tomorrow!

File.Name: 7605b_7604-074.jpg
Notes: Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) pushing down the lower petal of a new fower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family growing on the dry side of our garden. The broad lower petal of the flower stands up in the new flowers, and the bee has to force it down with its legs to get to the sweet nectar! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2007)

File.Name: 7605c_7604-075.jpg
Notes:
Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) snuggling into a new flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family growing on the dry side of our garden. The broad lower petal of the flower stands up in the new flowers, and the bee has to force it down with its legs. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2007) This flower is an example of "protandry" - the male stamens produce pollen before the female stigma is receptive. If you look close, you can see the female part of the flower held out of the way (and out of focus) toward the camera, with a violet color below the bee's middle leg. Male-first protandry and female-first protogyny are common ways for plants to avoid self-pollination by separating anthers and stigmas by time or space. See my [Next] two photos.

I think this flower is built for a bigger bee!
Locality: and out of focus) toward the camera
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; protandry; protogyny
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; protandry; protogyny

I finally finished school grades and teacher stuff, but today was too windy for photos. I'm still smiling - tomorrow is another day. It's summer!

File.Name: 7606b_7602-010.jpg

This sweet little bird in the garden looks like a sparrow, but I'm pretty sure it's a fledgling Dark-eyed Junco (Junco hyemalis oreganus) - aka "Oregon Junco". If you look close, you'll see that its wings aren't fully formed, and other fledglings are developing a dark cap . So many adventures await this little bird, though it's tough to be born in a drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2007)

It looks like this little bird is having second thoughts.

It's been windy, but today we hads a breeze rather than a gale. It's summer!

File.Name: 7607b_7607-159.jpg
Notes: Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly reeling in its proboscis after working the non-natiive Fuchsia flowers (Onagraceae) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2007)

File.Name: 7607c_7607-132.jpg
Notes: Remarkable Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae or kin in the order Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. These wasps are awesome preditors that use their long ovipositor "stinger" to lay eggs in critters hidden in wood or under ground to nourish its babies. The wasps' eggs need protein to develop, but the adults need sweet nectar from flowers for energy. They look like flying threads in the air. Up close, it holds its abdomen in the air, like Dizzy's horn! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2007)

File.Name: 7607d_7607-124.jpg
Notes: A rave of little Beetles (order Coleoptera) on a flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rose (Rosaceae) family in the canyon. The flat filaments of the stamens remind me of those wide matches that restaurants used to give out. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2007)
