File.Name: 7607e_7607-168.jpg
Notes: Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a new flower of native Catalina Mariposa Lily (Calochortus catalinae) in the Lily (Liliaceae) family. There's no mystery how pollination happens - the flowers have hairy nectar glands at the base of their petals to attract pollinators. I am surprised to find them flowering this late in the season, but there are hundreds of flowers on this hillside a mile from home. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2007)
CommonNames: Honey Bee; Catalina Mariposa Lily; Lily

File.Name: 7607f_7607-145.jpg
Notes: New caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on its usual food plant - non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family. This is an example of nature-in-progress. The fennel is introduced, and a bad weed in the mountains. The native butterflies use other Apiaceae) species like like Sanicula and Lomatium and Tauschia, but they seem to prefer the invasive fennel. I always check for caterpillars before I pull these weeds. The caterpillars are remarkable - there are so many extra eyes and legs painted on! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2007)
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Apiaceae; Sanicula; Lomatium; Tauschia
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Apiaceae; Sanicula; Lomatium; Tauschia

File.Name: 7608a_7608-215.jpg
Notes:
Remarkable summer flowers of Scarlet Larkspur (Delpinum cardinale). Our climate is winter-wet and summer-dry. The mountains are extremely dry in this near-record drought year. It's the driest year on record in Los Angeles County not far south of us. I perched on a slippery steep rocky hillside to get this photo, that's where they grow. I was worried about getting back up! (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2007)

Today's photos are all interesting and beautiful new wildflowers in our summer-dry mountains that you will miss if you drive on the highway in your car!

File.Name: 7608b_7608-236.jpg
Notes:
Pretty new flowers of Yellow-throated Phacelia (Phacelia brachyloba) in the confusing Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. The flowers are coiled in a typical "scorpioid inflorescence" - like a scorpian's tail - so new flowers keep appearing as the coil unwinds. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2007)

This flower and several previous are "fire follower" plants growing in a place that was cleared as a firebreak last spring. This hillside was "masticated" by an enormous TimbCo T425 "Hydro-shredder" machine - aka "The Masticator" - see my February photos starting at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/18834450

Despite the monster machine that chewed up the soil to mulch the slash, the native shrubs are stump-sprouting, and "fire follower" plants like this are doing their stuff - at least on this hillside. I'm worried about invasive Yellow Star Thistle (Centaurea solstitialis) and other weeds. Time will tell. I'll be documenting this site all summer. I'm getting a head start on next year's science fair project with a real question!
CommonNames: Yellow-throated Phacelia; Waterleaf; Yellow Star Thistle

File.Name: 7608c_7608-211.jpg
Notes: The "scorpioid inflorescence" of a native Large-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) flower in the mountains. The flower stalk is coiled up like a scorpion's tail. It continues to uncurl as new flowers ripen and old flowers make seeds. This plant is another faux "fire follower" in a place that cleared as a firebreak last summer. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2007)

File.Name: 7608d_7608-239.jpg
Notes: I believe this is native San Luis Blazing Star aka Small-flowered Stickleaf (Mentzelia micranthaBlazing Star (Loasaceae) plant family. The leaves are instantly recognizable because they feel rough like sandpaper. I haven't see this plant on the Pass for years. It's growing as a faux "fire follower" plant in a place that cleared as a firebreak last summer. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2007)
CommonNames: San Luis Blazing Star; Small-flowered Stickleaf; Blazing Star

File.Name: 7608e_7608-191.jpg
Notes: Elegant native flower and seed pods of Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) in the Papaveraceae family. This poppy does NOT have a disk below the flower or seed pod like E. californica. I think this is our real California Poppy that doesn't come from a wildflower seed packet. This poppy is growing on a hillside a mile from home that was "masticated" for a fire break last summer. See my [Next] few photos. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2007)

File.Name: 7608f_7607-163.jpg
Notes: New flower of native Catalina Mariposa Lily (Calochortus catalinae) in the Lily (Liliaceae) family. It seems late for these pretty flowers, but there's hundreds of them on one hillside near home. Most summer flowers are early in this drought summer in our mountains, but these spring lilies are late. I just show what I see! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2007)

File.Name: 7609a_7609-013.jpg
Notes:
Mama deer and her spotted fawn munching native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) plant family in the woods near the river today. We humans don't get along with Poison Oak, but the deer like it just fine. After living here many years, I'm still not sure what kind of deer we have - are they mulies or black-tailed? Sure it's a lucky shot - the kind of luck you have have when you get out every day with your camera! (Santa Ynez River, 9 June 2007)

The deer are invading our garden at home this summer. We no longer have our favorite dog Mojo to chase them away. So far, their favorite snack is Evening Primrose - no problem - but they're also nibbling on the roses and tomatoes and species salvias. I guess we need a new dog to chase them away - or do you have another solution?

File.Name: 7609b_7609-006.jpg
Notes: New flower heads of native Leather Root (Hoita macrostachya) in the Pea Family (Fabaceae) growing on the dry flood plain of the river. This is my best shot at a sharp photo in the wind today. This head-high perennial has trifoliate clover-like leaves and flower heads that look like hops fruit or little pine cones. It's not common here. (Santa Ynez River, 9 June 2007)

File.Name: 7609c_7609-001.jpg
Notes: Native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family, with an Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) visiting. I know it's not quite sharp, but I wanted to record the moment. I went to the river a few miles away today, and the wind seemed to kick up as soon as I started walking. Last year there were hundreds of these flowering plants growing in this place. This year I found just three flowers in the same place. Life is tough in a drought year. (Santa Ynez River, 9 June 2007)

File.Name: 7609d_7607-142.jpg
Notes: Little Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly working flowers of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family in the woods. It's a Hairstreak (subfamily Theclinae) butterfly without a tail, but it still rubs its hindwings together just like its tailed cousins. It looks brown from a distance, but up close it looks more like a pointillist painting by Georges Seurat - many color swatches that combine to make an eye-catching brown. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2007)
CommonNames: Brown Elfin; Coffee Berry; Buckthorn; Hairstreak
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Rhamnus tomentella; Rhamnaceae; Theclinae; pointillist
associatedTaxa: Callophrys augustinus; Rhamnus tomentella; Rhamnaceae; Theclinae; pointillist

File.Name: 7609e_7607-178.jpg
Notes: Pretty Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly working a non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) flower in the garden. The woods are so dry in this drought summer, I don't think the butterflies will last long - I'll get them while I can! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2007)

File.Name: 7609f_7607-131.jpg
Notes: This Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae or kin in the order Hymenoptera) looks so alien, but here it is working new flowers of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. You have to imagine another photo off to the upper-right to include it's long ovipositor "stinger". These wasps are awesome preditors that use their long ovipositor to lay eggs in critters hidden in wood or under ground to nourish its babies. it holds its abdomen in the air, like Dizzy's horn! (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2007)

File.Name: 7610a_7607-186.jpg
Notes:
This male Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) is just stretching while perched on a wire in the garden today. I wonder if it has to hold on tight? This male hummer has a beatiful iridescent "helmet" that sometimes looks brilliant red, but here it mostly has beautiful broze colors. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2007)

Long day burning 25 different CD disks of personal photos for newly graduated kids at school. My old computer takes 20+ minutes per disk. Tedious, but meaningful. I couldn't wander far between changing disks, so I stayed in the garden with my long lens to see what I could capture. A day well spent!

File.Name: 7610b_7610-024.jpg
Notes:
Little female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) perched in the garden. Maybe she's wondering how to build up those skinny legs!

Nah, she's just getting ready for a good scratch. But with those tiny legs, there must be places that are very hard to reach! (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2007)

File.Name: 7610c_7610-015.jpg
Notes: Little crested Oak Titmouse (Baeolophus inoratus) bird - aka "Plain Titmouse" - perched on a branch in the garden. They seem able to control their crest from up to down position. This is in-between, everything's cool. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2007)

File.Name: 7610d_7610-029.jpg
Notes: Mystery bird in the garden today. It has dull colors and a fairly heavy beak. What's remarkable is that it seems to be eating a leaf from a California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) tree on the edge of the garden. Have you ever accidentally eaten a "Bay Leaf" in a stew? *Yuck* Our native bay trees are like that only more so. I wonder who this little bird is and what it's doing. Are there little critters on the leaf? (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2007)

File.Name: 7610e_7610-018.jpg
Notes: Brilliant House Finch (Carpodacus mexicanus) bird hanging on a cord in the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2007)

File.Name: 7610f_7610-032.jpg
Notes:
Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly working new flowers of Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. At least this is most of the butterfly... life and birds have taken their toll. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2007)

I'll bet this veteran butterfly has some stories to tell of close encounters in the field! Life is tough for butterflies. They are big and beautiful and fluttery, which also makes them perfect targets. Nature sometimes works that way.

File.Name: 7611a_7611-047.jpg
Notes:
Maybe this bee was caught in a royal trap trying to steal the crown jewels...
It's almost true!

This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) is trapped in a flower of native Broad-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias eriocarpa) in the Asclepiadaceae family. The golden jewels on her legs are the "pollinia" pollen sacks of the flower that stick to the bee's legs as she walks over the flower and slips between the hoods of the flower. Sometimes the bees leave a leg behind or get stuck for good. (Santa Ynez Valley, 11 June 2007)

Our native Milkweeds are flowering a month early in this drought year. They came up so early that many of them were cut down by the usual weed trimming along the road. These late bloomers usually come up after the highway trim. I'm not complaining - I think of cars in the dry summer as moving bombs ready to go off every day!

I have more info about milkweed pollination at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8108247

File.Name: 7611b_7611-045.jpg
Notes:
This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) is trapped in a flower of native Broad-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias eriocarpa) in the Asclepiadaceae family. Look how her hind leg is trapped between the horned hoods of the milkweed flower.

I know this photo is not quite sharp, but it's an action shot. I was holding the flower up into the light with one hand and snapping the photo (on tripod of course) with my other hand. This is along the busy highway - cars and wind don't make it easy!
Locality:
Asclepias eriocarpa) in the Asclepiadaceae family. Look how her hind leg is trapped between the horned hoods of the milkweed flower.

I know this photo is not quite sharp

File.Name: 7611c_7611-053.jpg
Notes: Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a new flowers of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis) in the Asclepiadaceae plant family. These native flowers set traps for the bees so they will carry their golden "pollinia" away to other flowers - "carrying Mother Nature's seed to a new home in the sun". This bee has already snagged pollen sacks on each middle leg. The bee will pull free from this small milkweed flower, but see my [Next] photos. (Santa Ynez Valley, 11 June 2007)

File.Name: 7611d_7611-063.jpg
Notes:
I guess this is an immature Large Milkweed Bug (order Hemiptera, family Lygaeidae, species Oncopeltus fasciatus or kin) working flowers of native Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis) in the Asclepiadaceae plant family. (Santa Ynez Valley, 11 June 2007)

The milkweeds are early in this drought year, and this is the only milkweed bug or beetle I saw today - though I did chase a Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) from flower to flower - and missed the photo.
CommonNames: Large Milkweed Bug; Narrow-leaved Milkweed; Monarch Butterfly
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Lygaeidae; Oncopeltus fasciatus; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae; Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Hemiptera; Lygaeidae; Oncopeltus fasciatus; Asclepias fascicularis; Asclepiadaceae; Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera

File.Name: 7611e_7611-067.jpg
Notes: Tiny humpbacked fly (order Diptera) working a new flower of native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. I had to deliver a package to school this morning, but I got a few photos along the road. I got this photo near Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 11 June 2007)

File.Name: 7612a_7612-100.jpg
Notes:
I believe this remarkable wasp-mimic fly is a Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae in the insect order Diptera). That extra appendage below the antennae is its stiff proboscis. This amazing fly flew into view while I was getting photos on a native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family in the canyon. I leaned my tripod forward on two legs and tried to balance to got this photo. Then it was gone. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2007)

See my [Previous] two photos for another remarkable fly on the same shrub!

File.Name: 7612b_7612-103.jpg
Notes:
A fly is a fly, of course, of course...

But this fly (Diptera) looks like it's made of shining gold! It's working new flowers of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2007)

See my [Previous] photo. Maybe it's a Long-legged Fly (Dolichopodidae)? Flies are more complicated then we usually think. See my [Next] photo!
CommonNames: fly; Coffee Berry; Buckthorn; Long-legged Fly

File.Name: 7612c_7612-113.jpg
Notes: I don't know what this remarkable golden fly (Diptera) is, but it sure is beautiful. It's working new flowers of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family in the canyon. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2007) It lives up to the name Long-legged Fly (Dolichopodidae), but I have no idea if that's right.
CommonNames: fly; Coffee Berry; Buckthorn; Long-legged Fly

File.Name: 7612d_7612-116.jpg
Notes: Thread-like Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae or kin in the order Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. They look so alien-like up close! They use that long white-tipped ovipositor "stinger" to lay eggs in critters hidden in wood or under ground to nourish its babies. The wasps' eggs need protein to develop, but the adults need sweet nectar from flowers for energy. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2007)

File.Name: 7612e_7612-076.jpg
Notes:
Little Grass-Skipper (order Lepidoptera - subfamily Hesperiinae - genus Hesperia) butterfly working a new summer flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the canyon near the creek. These flowers vary from white to lavender. The brilliant red species cousins come later in summer. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2007)

Follow the butterfly's proboscis - it's fishing in the flowers!
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Hesperiinae; Hesperia; Cirsium occidentale; californicum
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Hesperiinae; Hesperia; Cirsium occidentale; californicum

File.Name: 7612f_7612-133.jpg
Notes:
I believe this is a Diurnal Firefly (genus Ellychnia, of the family Lampyridae, in the order Coleoptera) beetle that I caught taking wing from a Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii) plant in the woods. I know, it's not quite sharp - it's an action shot, and the shadow is crisp! (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2007)

"Diurnal firefly" sounds like an oxymoron! Fireflies really are beetles, and this critter is related in the same family. As far as I know it doesn't make light, though it's hard to tell when the sun is shining! We do have bioluminescent Glowworms (genus Phengodidae) in the mountains, though I've never seen them in our canyon. Maybe I should get out more at night.
ScientificName: Ellychnia; Lampyridae; Coleoptera; Lilium humboldtii; Phengodidae
associatedTaxa: Ellychnia; Lampyridae; Coleoptera; Lilium humboldtii; Phengodidae

File.Name: 7613a_7613-147.jpg
Notes: This oldtimer Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly has been around, and I'm sure it has many stories to tell about every near-miss and conquest! Yet here it is working the new flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) (family Scrophulariaceae?) that have just started to bloom in the garden. I understand completely! (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2007)

File.Name: 7614a_7613-181.jpg
Notes:
It's a festival of new Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies working the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) (family Scrophulariaceae?) that has just started flowering in the garden. There were dozens - maybe hundreds - of these butterflies working the flowers at the same time. It was windy, and I was holding the flower with one hand. Nothing is quite sharp, but sharpness isn't everything! (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2007)

We're getting ready for a festival too! Our kids and friends are starting to assemble for the three-day Live Oak Music Festival starting tomorrow just down the road near Lake Cachuma in the Santa Ynez Valley. We already have a car in line to get our camp site in the morning.

Check out http://www.liveoakmusic.org/ to see what you're missing!
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Butterfly Bush; Live Oak Music Festival

File.Name: 7614b_7613-141.jpg
Notes: New Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies working the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) (family Scrophulariaceae?) that has just started to flower in the garden. There were dozens - maybe hundreds - of these butterflies working the flowers at the same time. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2007)

File.Name: 7618a_7616-009.jpg
Notes:
Magical Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree lit by stage lights at the Live Oak Music Festival last weekend. This is looking over the backstage area late at night after the stage music is done and the campground jams are just beginning. That's the real magic of this festival! (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 June 2007)

The scene was not as bright as it looks in this photo. I had my camera on tripod and pushed the button. Nothing happened for a long time, so I finally turned the camera off, and it saved the image before shutting down. The EXIF data says 30 seconds @ f/6.3 @ ISO 200 - but I think the exposure was even longer than that. It captures the moment.

File.Name: 7619a_7617-061.jpg
Notes:
Live Oak Music Festival 2007! It happens just down the road from home near Lake Cachuma. There was fine music on the festival stage last weekend, but my favorite music is always our late night campground jams. Here are some old and new friends jammin' at 2:00 am on Sunday night - people who come together for this event year after year! (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 June 2007)

Thank you Ewan, Wil, Richard, Josh, Cheryl, Roz, Julie, Brianna, Lorenzo, Mark, Amber, Tracy, Zack, Jacob, TJ, Spencer, and everyone who was part of the jam this weekend. These are the best of times!

Check http://www.liveoakmusic.org/ to see what you missed.

File.Name: 7619b_7617-070.jpg
Notes: Mandolin, fiddle, and guitar at our late night campground jam at the Live Oak Music Festival last weekend. The light source is a Coleman lantern with a yellow glass cover. I like it better without "white balance". This exposure is 5 seconds @ f/4.5 @ ISO 400 @ about 2:00 in the morning with hours to go! (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 June 2007)

File.Name: 7619c_7617-071.jpg
Notes:
Hot music jam in our campground at the Live Oak Music Festival last weekend. This is fiddle and guitar - look for faces - they look to be in flames! (Santa Ynez Valley, 17 June 2007)

I don't even try to photograph the festival - other people do that - but I do like to shoot a few photos of our late night campground music jams. The light is a Coleman lantern with a yellow glass cover. The exposure is 3 seconds @ f/4.8 @ ISO 400. Everyone is moving, including the photographer!

File.Name: 7620a_7620-102.jpg
Notes: Pretty Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly perched on a Clary Sage (Salvia sclarea) flower in the garden. These butterflies outnumber all other butterflies in the woods now by 10-to-1 if not more. It won't last. I'll capture them while I can! See my [Previous] four photos for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2007)

File.Name: 7620b_7620-107.jpg
Notes:
This mama Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly is ovipositing - laying her eggs - underneath a new flower of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae family in the canyon today. I didn't want to disturb her labor to get a photo, so I came back an hour later to get the [Previous] photo showing her new golden eggs under the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2007)

Look close and you can see a web-nest of tiny caterpillars behind her.

Now I'm really confused. Last year, the Checkerspot caterpillars all made webs, but this year they didn't until now. Why is this butterfly laying her eggs on a flower that will soon fall away? Why is she laying her eggs near an existing colony rather than starting a new nest? Are these Checkerspot webs communal with caterpillars from many different butterflies? Is there an evoluntionary advantage to this?

I tagged the flower. I'll go back to document what happens.

File.Name: 7620c_7620-131.jpg
Notes: I watched a mama Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly oviposit her golden eggs an hour before I took this photo. The flower is a new Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae family in the canyon. The mama butterfly laid her eggs under the flower, so I flipped it over when I came back to get this photo. Note the other tiny caterpillars - see my [Next] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2007)

File.Name: 7620d_7619-076.jpg
Notes: As near as I can tell from my books, these are both Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies working new flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) (family Scrophulariaceae?) in the garden. I guess they're not called "variable" for nothing! The dark butterfly on the right is the odd one, with a really different color pattern. If it's a new species, it should be named Euphydryas treebeardensis! (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2007)
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Buddleia davidii; Scrophulariaceae; Euphydryas treebeardensis
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Buddleia davidii; Scrophulariaceae; Euphydryas treebeardensis

File.Name: 7620e_7620-099.jpg
Notes:
Different strokes for different folks!

This flower is an Oriental Poppy (Papaver somniferum) in the Papaveraceae family growing in a dry corner of the garden. The Grass-Skipper (order Lepidoptera - subfamily Hesperiinae - genus Hesperia) butterfly is probing under the stamens, but the little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) is interested in the disk-like female stigma. There's room for lots of critters with different tastes. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2007)
ScientificName: Papaver somniferum; Papaveraceae; Lepidoptera; Hesperiinae; Hesperia; Apoidea; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Papaver somniferum; Papaveraceae; Lepidoptera; Hesperiinae; Hesperia; Apoidea; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 7620f_7620-110.jpg
Notes:
Pretty little Grass-Skipper (order Lepidoptera - subfamily Hesperiinae - genus Hesperia) butterfly working a new summer flower of (maybe) native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the canyon near the creek.

I'm confused by our native thistles. These flowers vary from white to lavender, and the brilliant red-flowered thistles come later in summer. Whatever species they are, here it is. Tough exposure decision between flower and skipper. I like this.
Locality:
Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the canyon near the creek.

I'm confused by our native thistles. These flowers vary from white to lavender
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Hesperiinae; Hesperia; Cirsium occidentale; californicum
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Hesperiinae; Hesperia; Cirsium occidentale; californicum

File.Name: 7621a_7621-199.jpg
Notes:
This remarkable wasp-mimic fly is a Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae in the insect order Diptera). Here it is working new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2007)

Today is the Solstice day - summer at home in the Santa Barbara mountains, but winter in Brasil. It's also my Fotolog anniversary day. I started this f'log on June 21 in 2003. This will be photo number 4,457 in Treebeard's Photos - not counting all the other group and school photos. I captured most of these photos within walking distance from home on San Marcos Pass in the Santa Barbara mountains. I'm proud of this collection!

I still love my very first Fotolog photo:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/251716

File.Name: 7621b_7621-149.jpg
Notes: Furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae of order Diptera) working a pollen-bearing stamen of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family growing on the dry side of our garden. The little fly looks so delicate, like it's caressing the flower that will feed it. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2007)

File.Name: 7621c_7621-133.jpg
Notes: There are so many interesting flies - other than the bad ones - I won't try to guess what this critter is. For sure it's a Fly (order Diptera) working the new non-native composite (Asteraceae) Goldenrod (Solidago) flowers starting to flower in the garden. This fly moves around very fast, but when it finds a flower it likes it sticks its butt up in the air! (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2007)

File.Name: 7621d_7621-182.jpg
Notes:
Little Leafcutter Bee (family Megachilidae, order Hymenoptera) working a summer flower of Yellow-throated Phacelia (Phacelia brachyloba) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. This plant is a "fire follower" growing in a place that was cleared as a firebreak last spring. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2007)

This hillside was "masticated" by an enormous TimbCo T425 "Hydro-shredder" machine - aka "The Masticator" - but it's coming back with interesting natives and not too many invasive plants like Yellow Star Thistle (Centaurea solstitialis). I'm keeping an eye on this site to document what happens.
CommonNames: Leafcutter Bee; Yellow-throated Phacelia; Waterleaf; Yellow Star Thistle
ScientificName: Megachilidae; Hymenoptera; Phacelia brachyloba; Hydrophyllaceae; Centaurea solstitialis
associatedTaxa: Megachilidae; Hymenoptera; Phacelia brachyloba; Hydrophyllaceae; Centaurea solstitialis

File.Name: 7621e_7620-119.jpg
Notes:
Tiny Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera working a new summer flower of (maybe) native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the canyon near the creek. I guess this bee is a tiny Leafcutter Bee or Mason Bee in the family Megachilidae. These bees collect pollen on the underside of their abdomen, rather than on their legs like other bees. In this photo, it looks like the bee is munching the female style of the flower and rubbing against the male pollen-bearing anthers. Confusing anatomy, but the bee seems to know what its doing! (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2007)

I think of large insects as the important pollinators - like the Grass-Skipper butterfly I posted yesterday working the same flower. But it might be these tiny insects that do the real work to keep the flowers coming year after year.
CommonNames: Wild Bee; California Thistle; Leafcutter Bee; Mason Bee; Grass-Skipper
ScientificName: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Megachilidae
associatedTaxa: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Megachilidae

File.Name: 7621f_7620-122.jpg
Notes:
Pretty little yellow Beetle (order Coleoptera) munching a flower of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2007)

I don't understand how these beetles have hairs growing through their hard elytra wing covers! What do they attach to?

File.Name: 7622a_7622-222.jpg
Notes: Like crows chasing a hawk - here's a little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) "bugging" a much larger Wasp (family Sphecidae, order Hymenoptera) away from the Dahlia flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2007)

File.Name: 7622b_7622-209.jpg
Notes: Tiny new web-spinning caterpillars of (I think) a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly enshrouding a flower head of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae family in the canyon. I watched a butterfly lay her eggs on a flower near this web two days ago. I guess this is a second brood, but maybe they will over-winter as pupae? These Checkerspot butterflies are everywhere. It will be lovely to see more. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2007)

File.Name: 7622c_7621-197.jpg
Notes: I believe this is a Square-spotted Blue (Euphilotes battoides) butterfly working new flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the canyon near the creek. I got the blues! We have many different species in the canyon, but I went to my books, and I believe this is right - at least tonight! (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2007)

File.Name: 7622d_7621-200.jpg
Notes: Another photo of this remarkable wasp-mimic Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae in the insect order Diptera). See my [Previous] photo. This angle shows its amazing wasp-like abdomen and intricate genitalia. The fly is working new flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family near our mailbox in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2007)

File.Name: 7623a_7623-016.jpg
Notes:
Impressive Great Golden Digger Wasp (Sphex ichneumoneus, family Sphecidae, order Hymenoptera). It's working new summer flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family. This large wasp is about an inch long (2.5 cm). It nests in the ground and provides its young with large Katydid nymphs. Yum! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2007)

I'm suddenly seeing a lot of these wasps. It's interesting how they all emerge at the same time. That's good for mating and finding prey, but I wonder what the environmental signal is?
CommonNames: Great Golden Digger Wasp; California Buckwheat; Buckwheat
ScientificName: Sphex ichneumoneus; Sphecidae; Hymenoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Sphex ichneumoneus; Sphecidae; Hymenoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: 7623b_7622-215.jpg
Notes: Remarkable large Robber Fly (family Asilidae in the order Diptera) perched between leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon. Nice whiskers! This interesting fly is about an inch (2-3 cm) long, with beautiful colors. These flies hunt their prey in the air, like dragonflies. They don't visit flowers, so it's sheer luck to catch one at rest. I only got one chance for a photo before it disappeared. I can't recall seeing it before. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2007)

File.Name: 7623c_7623-002.jpg
Notes: Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly showing her stuff on the well-named flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) (family Scrophulariaceae?) in the garden. The white-bordered "eyes" on the forewings are visible on top and bottom. The hindwing eyes are showing through with the sun behind. I've seen a few other Buckeyes in the woods, but this is the first I've managed to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2007)

File.Name: 7623d_7621-155.jpg
Notes: Pretty little Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae - probably genus Hesperia) butterfly working flowers of well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) (family Scrophulariaceae?) in the garden. It's perched in classic "jet-plane" posture, with hindwings wider open than forewings. There are many species that look alike, so it's easy to "skip her" - at least I don't worry about species names. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2007)

File.Name: 7623e_7622-221.jpg
Notes:
First new flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family, growing in the canyon. This is an uncommon summer flower in our summer-dry mountains - especially in this drought year. Did you spot the little Beetle (order Coleoptera)? (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2007)

Monardellas are an interesting local group. My flora lists 10 different species in Santa Barbara county. Some species have an intense peppermint smell, but this mountain native smells musty, strong but not pleasant.
CommonNames: Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Mint; Beetle

File.Name: 7623f_7623-020.jpg
Notes:
It's always a surprise to find the first native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) flower in the canyon. The large hand-sized flowers are so exquisite and vulnerable. Most lilies in our mountains don't flower as deer and grubs (and tourists!) take their toll. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2007)

These flowers are made for Swallowtail butterflies. See my photo series starting at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/14398898
and previous to that. Nothing else could pollinate them!

File.Name: 7624a_7624-040.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful Tailed Copper aka Arota Copper (Lycaena arota) butterfly resting on a leaf in the woods. I didn't recognize the butterfly when I started chasing it - I thought it was a strange Hairstreak because of the tails. When I checked my archive, I found a photo of the same butterfly last year on July 1 in almost the same place. My books say their food plant is Gooseberries (Ribes sp. in the Grossulariaceae family), and the host plants were growing nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2007)

Fun hike today Lynn - lets do it again!
CommonNames: Tailed Copper; Arota Copper; Hairstreak; Gooseberries

File.Name: 7624b_7623-005.jpg
Notes:
Remarkable scary face on the front wing of this Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly working the well-named flowers of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) (family Scrophulariaceae?) in the garden. The face disappears when the butterfly slides her wings together, and it's a shock when she suddenly opens them. What do you see? (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2007)

Other insects and spiders have faux scary faces to startle potential enemies. Check this spider photo if you dare!
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/15542639
Isn't it remarkable that this butterfly has evolved a face that can scare critters in many phyla, including us. People making horror movies - take notice!

File.Name: 7624c_7624-047.jpg
Notes: Exquisite summer flowers of a native succulent Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata or maybe D. cymosa?) in the Stonecrop (Crassulaceae) family. These flowers look like they were sculpted from porcelain or wax. I've been seeing the first California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) butterflies working these flowers, but I'm still trying to get the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2007)
ScientificName: Dudleya lanceolata; D. cymosa; Crassulaceae; Zerene eurydice
associatedTaxa: Dudleya lanceolata; D. cymosa; Crassulaceae; Zerene eurydice

File.Name: 7624d_7623-013.jpg
Notes: It's always a surprise to find real orchids in our summer-dry mountains, but here they are. These are the uncommon summer flowers of native Green Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata - or maybe P. michaelii?) flowering in the woods. The plants look more like asparagus stalks than the orchids you get from a florist, but they are real native orchids growing in the summer heat. Note the long spur and how the inferior ovary is twisted to present the flower upside down. We have more of these plants on the dry hillside at home, and I saw their large hand-sized basal leaves this spring, but they are not flowering. This plant by our mailbox has the only flowers I've seen. This is one of our driest years ever, and some summer plants just aren't bothering to make flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2007)

File.Name: 7624e_7623-032.jpg
Notes: Developing seeds and large leaves of a native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the Maple (Aceraceae) family. I haven't found native maples in our canyon - this one is growing along San Jose Creek a mile away. I should bring some of these seeds home when they ripen in the fall! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2007)

File.Name: 7624f_7621-189.jpg
Notes: Pretty new summer flowers of native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. These daisies - and many other interesting natives - are doing well in an area that was cleared for a fire break last spring. This hillside was "masticated" by an enormous TimbCo T425 "Hydro-shredder" machine - aka "The Masticator". (You have to be careful saying that in public!) It didn't burn, but I'm seeing many typical "fire-follower" plants, even in this drought summer. I'm keeping an eye on this site to document what happens. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2007)

File.Name: 7625a_7625-054.jpg
Notes:
Tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) thinking twice about the little Wasp (order Hymenoptera) on a flower head of a horticultural composite (Asteraceae) Goldenrod (Solidago) in the garden. If you know the flower, you'll appreciate how small these critters are. The little spider tightened, but it decided not to strike.

Good advice (From B. Kliban) - never eat anything bigger than your head!
Locality: Solidago) in the garden. If you know the flower

File.Name: 7625b_7625-051.jpg
Notes: Tiny humpbacked fly (order Diptera) working the first new florets of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. If you know the flowers, you can appreciate how tiny this fly is! (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2007)

File.Name: 7625c_7621-160.jpg
Notes: Clown-colored Western Tussock Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Lymantriidae, maybe Orgyia vetusta or kin) caterpillar on a leaf in the garden. These critters are everywhere. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2007)

File.Name: 7626a_7610-017.jpg
Notes:
(Baeolophus inoratus) on the right. I don't really approve of feeders - the birds get so aggressive - but this drought summer must be pretty harsh for the critters, as many native plants are either not showing at all or are setting few seeds. Watching the feeder is also great entertainment. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2007)

I'm heading off to Arizona soon for some family stuff. This will be a busy week, but I hope I can find some time to get into the desert. I'll be back on Monday, hopefully with some new photos.

File.Name: 7626b_7619-079.jpg
Notes: I believe this is a pair of Dark-eyed Juncos (Junco hyemalis oreganus) aka "Oregon Juncos". The one on the left is a juvenile. The dark hood shows up later. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2007)

File.Name: 7626c_7610-016.jpg
Notes: Oak Titmouse (Baeolophus inoratus) bird - aka "Plain Titmouse" - munching a sunflower seed on the bird feeded. Usually these birds have a littled pointed crest on the heads, but they can lay it down. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2007)

File.Name: 7702a_7702-035.jpg
Notes:
Brilliant little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched for prey on a horticultural Onion (Allium) flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2007)

I've been in Arizona for a week, about an hour drive from Phoenix. No photos. I was busy with meetings. The temperature was close to 115 F (46 C) every day, and it was hard to think about photos at the end of the day.

I got home this afternoon, and it was a pleasure to visit the garden with my camera. D'oh - the temperature was over 100 degrees at home today. I look forward to my morning walk.

File.Name: 7703a_7703-036.jpg
Notes:
Male California Dogface (Colias eurydice aka Zerene eurydice) butterfly working a composite Dahlia flower in the garden. We have the sprinkler on - if you look close, you can see a line of little water drops on his proboscis. These butterflies are just starting to appear. I saw both males and females in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2007)

This is a "Sulphur" butterfly - but it's nothing ordinary. The white spots are silver, and it has an iridescent violet "dog face" pattern on its upper wings. This is the official State Butterfly of California, but I reckon only lepidopterists know why. It always folds its wings up tight when visiting a flower to pose for a photo. Capturing it in-flight with wings open is my challenge.

See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/8477823

File.Name: 7703b_7703-069.jpg
Notes:
This is like the photos you've seen of the Loch Ness Monster or Sasquatch - not a good photo, but (maybe ;=) it shows an interesting beast!

The flower is a common Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Diplacus longiflorus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family in the canyon. The uncommon critter is visible at the very top of the photo. I'm sure this is a Giant Flower-loving Fly - order Diptera, family Apioceridae, genus Rhaphiomidas. These flies are not quite bee-size. They fly with a high-pitched buzz - it's easier to hear them than to see them. Note the very long proboscis that is MUCH longer than the fly's body! The fly will fold its proboscis under its body like a Hemiptera when it flies away. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2007)

I spent an hour trying to get a clear photo of these amazing flies before the wind kicked up. They visit one flower and then zoom around extremely fast for several minutes before visiting another. I got this photo as the fly was leaving. I also saw these flies working flowers of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. I know where they are. I'll keep trying to get a better photo.
CommonNames: Bush Monkey Flower; Figwort; Giant Flower-loving Fly; Woolly Blue Curls; Mint
ScientificName: Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Diptera; Apioceridae; Rhaphiomidas; Hemiptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Diptera; Apioceridae; Rhaphiomidas; Hemiptera; Trichostema lanatum; Lamiaceae

File.Name: 7703c_7703-057.jpg
Notes: Strange Snakefly (order Neuroptera, family Raphidiidae, genus Agulla) insect wiping its face clean after diving into flowers of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family in the canyon. These critters look strange because they have a real "neck" - along with the usual head, thorax, and abdomen. The neck is a elongated "pronotum" - the first segment of the thorax. That extra degree of freedom gives these critters a creepy robotic look. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2007)
ScientificName: Neuroptera; Raphidiidae; Agulla; Rhamnus tomentella; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Neuroptera; Raphidiidae; Agulla; Rhamnus tomentella; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: 7703d_7703-064.jpg
Notes:
If you are just learning insects like I am (year after year after year), then this critter is very confusing. It has two-wings like a fly (order Diptera), but everything else is wrong, and what gives with those long tails?

With help from CHiS and N@ndo last summer, I'm pretty sure this a male Mealy Bug. I believe it is order Homoptera, suborder Sternorryncha, superfamily Coccoidea, family Pseudococcidae, and maybe genus Pseudococcus. The males have just two wings like flies and "two conspicuous, long, white anal filaments or tails". The females are wingless and look very different. I still haven't found a photo of anthing similar. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2007)

See my photo from last year and more links at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/14580694
ScientificName: Diptera; Homoptera; Sternorryncha; Coccoidea; Pseudococcidae; Pseudococcus
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Homoptera; Sternorryncha; Coccoidea; Pseudococcidae; Pseudococcus

File.Name: 7703e_7703-066.jpg
Notes: Well-named Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae in the family Reduviidae of the order Hemiptera) slowly walking over a leaf of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Diplacus longiflorus). This walkingstick-like critter is a preditor, but I'm not sure what its prey is. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2007)
ScientificName: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus
associatedTaxa: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus

File.Name: 7703f_7703-088.jpg
Notes: The little yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the lower left got excited when this Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) moved close - you can see it reaching out to touch the bee with its legs. Then it had second thoughts and prudently backed off. The flowers are a horticultural Onion (Allium) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2007)

File.Name: 7704a_7704-097.jpg
Notes:
Who needs fireworks for Independence Day when there is a red, white, and blue butterfly like this in the woods!

This is an astounding Great Purple Hairstreak (Atlides halesus) butterfly "puddling" on wet ground near the garden. It let me get close. Why should any critter in nature look so beautiful to our human eyes? (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2007)

I'm listening to Peter Tosh - "Talking Revolution" - this is a good day to remember our American roots.

File.Name: 7704b_7704-112.jpg
Notes: Just the recurved petals of a native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) flower in the canyon. These flowers are always a surprise in the mountains, like a celebrity at a campground music jam. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2007)

File.Name: 7704c_7703-051.jpg
Notes:
Remarkable Snakefly (order Neuroptera, family Raphidiidae, genus Agulla) insect on flowers of native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family in the canyon. These critters look strange because they have a real "neck" - along with the usual head, thorax, and abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2007)

See my photo yesterday:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/22562823
ScientificName: Neuroptera; Raphidiidae; Agulla; Rhamnus tomentella; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Neuroptera; Raphidiidae; Agulla; Rhamnus tomentella; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: 7704d_7704-090.jpg
Notes: A pair of new California Dogface (Colias eurydice aka Zerene eurydice) butterflies working different composite (Asteraceae) flowers in the garden. That's a female on top and a male below. The lighting is different, but you can see some similarities and differences. You can see the hidden "dogface" on the male's upper wings with the sun behind. To me, it looks more like a cartoon chicken or penguin - like a "tough guy" high school sports logo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2007)

File.Name: 7705a_7705-143.jpg
Notes:
New caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly trying to look invisible on its usual food plant - non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family. These native butterflies have really been keeping the invasive fennel in control this year. What great magic - turning weeds into butterflies! (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2007)

It's hard for this striking caterpillar to look invisible! I poked it hoping to see its brilliant orange "osmetarium" - no luck, but see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/14098442

File.Name: 7705b_7705-123.jpg
Notes: This photo isn't quite sharp, but it's good enough to show that this is a new Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly in the woods today. Note the iridescent blue scales on the hindwing margin. Summer legumes (Fabaceae) like Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) are mostly finished in this drought summer, but these butterflies can also use California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family that is just starting to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2007)
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; Deerweed; California Buckwheat; Buckwheat
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Fabaceae; Lotus scoparius; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Fabaceae; Lotus scoparius; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: 7705c_7704-094.jpg
Notes: Remarkable Great Purple Hairstreak (Atlides halesus) butterfly "puddling" on wet ground near the garden. It let me get close. It also has iridescent blue scales covering the wings above, but the butterflies always fold their wings up when they land. I've only seen a flash of color in the air. According to my book, their larval foodplant is Mistletoe (family Viscaceae), especially on Cottonwoods - genus Populus in the Salicaceae family. Usually I find these at the end of summer, but everything is early in this drought summer. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2007)

File.Name: 7706a_7706-155.jpg
Notes:
Pretty blue Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) on a stem by the only section of the Santa Ynez River I know of that is still flowing in this drought summer. I reckon all of today's photos show Narrow-winged Damselflies (family Coenagrionidae). I really need a better book about these critters. I love those megapixel eyes! (Actually about 30,000 facets or ommatidia.) (Santa Ynez River, 6 July 2007)

Yesterday was my camera's birthday! I bought my Nikon D70 three years ago. (Check my archive for July 5, 2004.) This camera - maybe all digital cameras - counts shutter presses and records the number in the EXIF header of every photo. I went through my old photos today using the freeware program IExif. I discovered that the counter rolls over at 65,535. That makes sense - it's 2^16 or 16 bits in binary.

In the last three years, I've taken 88,231 photos with my camera, about 30,000 per year or 80 per day! Maybe half of those are school photos, and I only keep the very good "Treebeard" photos - somewhere between 1 in 4 and 1 in 20. Dang - if digital photos were slides, that would take a lot of shoeboxes!

File.Name: 7706b_7706-162.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful tan Damselfly (Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) resting on a stem at the only part of the Santa Ynez River that is actually flowing. I guess this is a female - like ducks, the male damselflies are usually the colorful ones. (Santa Ynez River, 6 July 2007)

We went to the river today for a swim and a bath. Wouldn't you know - yesterday was the hottest day of the year at 105 degrees Fahrenheit (41 C), and that was the day our water tank went dry. We have to replace the pump - it should arrive tomorrow. This is scary when it's so hot and dry, and our second summer wildfire is burning in the backcountry. At least I got these nice damselfly photos!

File.Name: 7706c_7706-164.jpg
Notes: Pretty little Damselfly (Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) resting on a flower head of Dahlia in the garden. I startled it away, but it kept coming back to the same roost so I could finally get the picture. That's a good photo trick for dragonflies and their kin. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2007)

File.Name: 7707a_7707-027.jpg
Notes:
Remarkable native summer flower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) in the Lily (Lilium) family. It's remarkable for its beauty and for being a real lily flowering in our summer dry mountains. We've had high temps of 100+ degrees (Fahrenheit) all week, but here is this beautiful lily flowering on a south-sun-facing slope despite the heat and drought. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2007)

Our pump is fixed. I had a shower. Life is good!

File.Name: 7707b_7707-023.jpg
Notes: This opening flower bud looks strange rather than beautiful - but check my [Next] photo to see what it becomes! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2007)

File.Name: 7707c_7707-006.jpg
Notes:
I guess this is native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) - though other local plants have lavender or red flowers. Note the cobweb-like hairs on the phyllaries. These summer thistles are really confusing. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2007)

Be sure you know what you're doing before you pull any thistles as weeds in the back country. The invading weedy Bull Thistle (Cirsium vulgare) can fool you. When in doubt, WAIT! But please DO remove the bad ones.
Locality: Cirsium vulgare) can fool you. When in doubt

File.Name: 7707d_7705-137.jpg
Notes: Strange nature - here are interesting native Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) tending *something* on a twig of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon. I'm not sure if this is a gall, or a scale insect, or a pupa. Whatever it is, the ants are interested - I reckon they're harvesting sweet honeydew. I'll keep an eye on this colony. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2007)

File.Name: 7707e_7705-120.jpg
Notes:
The little web-spinning caterpillars have woven a shroud over this flower head of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae family in the canyon. It's creepy - I wouldn't want to wake up like that! I don't think these are Tent Caterpillars (family Lasiocampidae). I watched a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly oviposit eggs nearby that became a similar nest. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2007)

Are these caterpillars planing to hatch this year? Summer flowers are already scarce in this drought year. There won't be much left by the time they emerge as adults. I guess this is next year's brood. One of my books says they "overwinter or spend extended dry periods as partially grown caterpillars in a special 'thicker' skin with a more dense covering of spines." See my photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/22175817
CommonNames: Climbing Penstemon; Tent Caterpillars; Variable Checkerspot
ScientificName: Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Lasiocampidae; Euphydryas chalcedona
associatedTaxa: Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Lasiocampidae; Euphydryas chalcedona

File.Name: 7707f_7707-014.jpg
Notes: Butterflies are getting scarce in the canyon, and it's no mystery why. Summer flowers are scarce in this drought year, and butterflies just use themselves up like this veteran perched on an oak leaf. Very hot triple-digit weather this week has taken a toll. It's hard to tell, but I guess from the markings that this is the last of the Tailed Copper aka Arota Copper (Lycaena arota) butterflies that were fresh a few weeks ago. Fly on little wing! (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2007)

File.Name: 7708a_7708-041.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly perched on a leaf in the woods today. His eyes are amazing! I don't think any of those dark eye-spots are pigment. I reckon they are all a diffraction pattern of light reflecting from the thousands of six-sided ommatidia or "simple eyes" that make up the butterfly's compound eyes like a retina. Note that there are circles of six spots around the central dark spot that maybe match the six-sided simple eyes. All insects with large compound eyes show the pupil-like central spot in photos. I wonder why these butterflies show the circles of small spots around it? I'm a middle-school science teacher - I should understand this, but I'm better at raising questions than answering them! (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2007)

We're off to L.A. tomorrow to help my Dad for a few days. I'm not sure when I'll be back with new photos.

File.Name: 7708b_7708-038.jpg
Notes: Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae - probably genus Hesperia) butterfly working remarkable flowers of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum), a native shrub in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family in the canyon. With those long-exerted stamens and style, I'm not sure what critters these flowers are designed for - they sure are missing this skipper. (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2007)

File.Name: 7708c_7705-142.jpg
Notes: This fat mature caterpillar that will become an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly is flipping over on a stem of its usual food plant - non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family growing along the road in the canyon. Like all insects, it has six true legs up front. Those other "legs" under the stem are called "prolegs". This fat caterpillar is hefting a lot of mass with all those real and faux legs! (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2007)

File.Name: 7708d_7704-101.jpg
Notes:
Elegant summer flowers of native Green Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata in the Orchidaceae family) flowering in the woods. These uncommon plants look more like asparagus stalks than the orchids you get from a florist, but they are real native orchids growing in the drought-summer heat. This plant by our mailbox is the only plant with flowers I've seen this summer. This is our third driest year ever, and some summer plants just aren't bothering to make flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2007)

The orchid flowers have a long spur that looks like it should hold nectar to attract insect pollinators with a very long proboscis - but I don't think I've ever seen any insect visit these flowers.

File.Name: 7708e_7706-158.jpg
Notes:
Mating Damselflies (suborder Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) at the river. At least this is part of mating. That's the darker male above clasping the female behind her head. But the male's genitalia are on the first segment at the other end of his abdomen - the are visible here oon the first segment behind the wings. To transfer genetic material, the female brings the tip of her abdomen to make contact with the male in "wheel" position. I guess this female is oviposting - laying eggs in the water - but I wonder who's calling the shots. Is he holding her down or is she making him wait? I suspect the latter. (Santa Ynez River, 6 July 2007)

Looking at my books, these damselflies sure look like Sooty Dancers (Argia lugens).

File.Name: 7708f_7707-021.jpg
Notes:
I can't resist one more photo of the remarkable native summer flowers of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) in the Lily (Lilium) family. They flower in mid-summer - even in this drought year - when most native flowers are long gone. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2007)

Everything about these flowers comes in three's. They have three sepal-like petals and three real petals, each of which has a swolled nectar gland at the base - one gland is visible at the bottom of the photo. There are six male stamens and a female style with three fertile stigmas on top. The flowers vary from mostly yellow to mostly white to mostly purple. Our local variety has squarish petals and abruptly pointed anthers. They are not common, but I notice them in exposed places when I look for them.

(Lynn - they are flowering across the trail from the Scarlet Larkspur, and there are more along the road about 1/4 mile further near the lookout place!)
Locality: Lynn - they are flowering across the trail from the Scarlet Larkspur

File.Name: 7713a_7713-056.jpg
Notes:
Little Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera) in attack position on a composite flower head of Yarrow (Achillea) in the garden. If you know the flower, you will appreciate how small this critter is. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2007)

These preditors are masters of camouflage. Note the yellow pollen on its face and legs that help it look like part of the flowers. See my [Previous] photo to see how it's done.

File.Name: 7713b_7713-051.jpg
Notes:
Little Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera) perched on a composite flower head of Yarrow (Achillea) in the garden. If you know the flower, you will appreciate how small this is. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2007)

Maybe I've been watching too many "conspiracy theory" videos on YouTube, but it sure looks like this preditor is rubbing the flower with its crab-like front leg to pick up yellow pollen for camouflage. See my [Next] photo.

File.Name: 7713c_7713-052.jpg
Notes: Like a "Bull and Bear" fight in old California - here is a yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) facing a tough little bee - maybe a Leafcutter or Mason bee (family Megachilidae, order Hymenoptera) on a flower head of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. The disk flowers look like they're making a ring for the fight. This little bee got away. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2007)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Leafcutter; Mason; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 7713d_7713-049.jpg
Notes: Busy hive of Honey Bees (Apis mellifera) in a cavity at the base of a Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree at my Dad's house. (Santa Monica Mountains, 13 July 2007)

File.Name: 7713e_7712-046.jpg
Notes:
Handsome pair of young deer on the edge of my Dad's garden in the Santa Monica Mountains. I guess this spike-horned male and female are yearlings. I called them Bonnie and Clyde - "They're young. They're in love. They eat gardens." (Santa Monica Mountains, 12 July 2007)

I'm confused by our local deer - are these mulies or black-tailed?

File.Name: 7714a_7714-002.jpg
Notes:
Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working the last of the non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) flowers in the garden. Look on top to follow its proboscis - like a dagger - heading into the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2007)

How does this butterfly control its proboscis aka tongue? The proboscis is actually formed of two separate parts zipped together, so it can`t be muscles. Something with hydraulics?

File.Name: 7714b_7714-016.jpg
Notes: Butterflies are scarce in the canyon, just like flowers in this drought summer season. I found this little Blue (genus Euphilotes) butterfly perched near flowers of White Sweet Clover (Melilotus albus) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. I guess it's a Dotted Blue (E. enoptes) or maybe a Square-spotted Blue (E. battoides). In this dry summer, it's a treat to capture any butterflies! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2007)
CommonNames: Blue; White Sweet Clover; Pea; Dotted Blue; Square-spotted Blue
ScientificName: Euphilotes; Melilotus albus; Fabaceae; E. enoptes; E. battoides
associatedTaxa: Euphilotes; Melilotus albus; Fabaceae; E. enoptes; E. battoides

File.Name: 7714c_7714-027.jpg
Notes:
Little oak galls on an old catkin of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods, with exit holes where the adults got away. These galls have been home for the larvae of tiny Gall Wasps (family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera) - maybe the Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiousus). (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2007)

Gall wasps insert their eggs and a dose of RNA to re-program the tree to produce the abnormal gall growth that nourishes the larvae within. These wasps have a complicated life cycle with alternating bisexual and unisexual generations forming different kinds of galls on different parts of the trees.

See my photo of the new galls last spring at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/19670865
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Gall Wasps; Two-horned Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Dryocosmus dubiousus
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Dryocosmus dubiousus

File.Name: 7714d_7714-004.jpg
Notes: Summer flowers of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Honeysuckle (Caprifoliaceae) family. Note the joined leaves on the stem above the flower cluster. All summer flowers are interesting in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2007)

File.Name: 7714e_7714-041.jpg
Notes: Little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working a Hollyhock relative (genus Alcea or kin in the Mallow (Malvaceae) family) in the garden. The deer like this plant, but it's coming back. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2007)

File.Name: 7714f_7714-038.jpg
Notes: Perfect Hummingbird nest - made of mosses, lichens, and oak catkins, and bound together with spider webs. I spotted this finished nest along a trail in the woods I walk every week or two. I'll bet I walked right past this nest several times when mama-hummer was sitting with her young, and I didn't notice. It's remarkable how much we miss that is right in front of us! (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2007)

File.Name: 7715a_7715-066.jpg
Notes:
All summer flowers are remarkable in our summer-dry desert climate - especially in this drought summer, our third driest year on record. But this native summer flower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) in the Lily (Lilium) family blows away my expectations. I got under this flower and looked up to the sky. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2007)

Lynn tells me this flower has been raised to a species as Calochortus fimbriata. Names change, but I will always look forward to finding these flowers in the dry summer mountains.
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; Lilium; Calochortus fimbriata
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; Lilium; Calochortus fimbriata

File.Name: 7715b_7715-074.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the in the Scrophulariaceae family. All summer flowers are remarkable in our summer-dry desert climate, but these summer penstemons really are special. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2007)

I didn't notice until I got this photo back home on my computer, but look at the base of the upper flower. Did you spot the little Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera) in attack position? Seems like a strange place, but I noticed the bees working the flowers were doing a cheat and entering the flowers at the base to get right to the nectar. The preditor is in the perfect place.

Isn't it remarkable that the bees learn a cheat - and the preditors learn that the bees learned the cheat! Is this nature or nurture?
Locality: family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera) in attack position? Seems like a strange place

File.Name: 7715c_7715-062.jpg
Notes:
Adult Ladybird Beetles aka "Ladybugs" (family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) making babies on a non-native shrub of Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. Maybe this is a Variegated Lady Beetle (Hippodamia variegata). See my [Previous] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2007)

Spanish Broom is a non-native invasive shrub that is following the roads into the mountains. Aphids weaken the plants; and the ladybugs eat the aphids; but the plants are invasive; so maybe the ladybugs are helping the invaders?
CommonNames: Ladybird Beetles; Ladybugs; Spanish Broom; Pea; Variegated Lady Beetle
ScientificName: Coccinellidae; Coleoptera) making babies on a non-native shrub of Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum; Fabaceae; Hippodamia variegata
associatedTaxa: Coccinellidae; Coleoptera) making babies on a non-native shrub of Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum; Fabaceae; Hippodamia variegata

File.Name: 7715d_7715-055.jpg
Notes:
Pupae of Ladybird Beetles aka "Ladybugs" (family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) on Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. Maybe this is a Variegated Lady Beetle (Hippodamia variegata). (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2007)

See my [Next] and [Previous] photos.
CommonNames: Ladybird Beetles; Ladybugs; Spanish Broom; Pea; Variegated Lady Beetle
ScientificName: Coccinellidae; Coleoptera) on Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum; Fabaceae; Hippodamia variegata

File.Name: 7715e_7715-054.jpg
Notes:
Larvae of a Ladybird Beetle aka "Ladybug" (family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera) on seed pod of Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. This non-native shrub is invasive, but it usually stays along the roads. Maybe this is a Variegated Lady Beetle (Hippodamia variegata)? (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2007)

Now it gets complicated: aphids weaken the plants; and the ladybugs eat the aphids; but the plants are invasive; so the ladybugs are helping the plants? D'oh - we need more selective pests!
CommonNames: Ladybird Beetle; Ladybug; Spanish Broom; Pea; Variegated Lady Beetle

File.Name: 7715f_7715-042.jpg
Notes: American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) flowers in the garden this morning. This pretty lady has two large eyespots on its hindwings - other lady butterflies have four eyes. They've all been scarce this summer. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2007)
ScientificName: Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii

File.Name: 7716a_7716-105.jpg
Notes: Butterflies are scarce in this drought summer, so it was a pleasure to find this new visitor in the garden today. This is a Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly - I love the name - working flowers of non-native Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed (Asclepiadaceae) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2007)
ScientificName: Calephelis nemesis californica; Asclepias tuberosa; Asclepiadaceae
associatedTaxa: Calephelis nemesis californica; Asclepias tuberosa; Asclepiadaceae

File.Name: 7716b_7716-094.jpg
Notes:
Male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working flowers of this Iris/Gladiolus relative Crocosmia masonorum (or kin) in the garden. The sun is behind, so you can see the "dog face" showing through - but you can't see the brilliant iridescent violet sheen that is only revealed in the air when the butterfly takes wing. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2007)

See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/8477823

File.Name: 7716c_7716-096.jpg
Notes: Here is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly "puddling" on wet ground near the garden. I never see these butterflies visit flowers - they usually fly high through the oak trees - but they always show up on the wet ground when we water. It's working in its own shadow. What a face - the photo needs a caption! (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2007)

File.Name: 7716d_6807-033.jpg
Notes: Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly perched in the summer-dry woods. These butterflies have California summer colors - golden dry grass and fallen leaves, not the poppies - they are part of the "golden rolling hills of California" that Kate Wolf sang about so eloquently. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2007)

File.Name: 7716e_7715-050.jpg
Notes: Looking into a native summer flower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - recently renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Lily (Lilium) family. This is like an engineer's "exploded diagram" that shows the working parts inside. Looking close, I see that the flower didn't actually lose a petal - it's just rolled up in the lower left, and the other petals will soon do the same. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2007)
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Lilium
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Lilium

File.Name: 7717a_7717-138.jpg
Notes:
Mama, the flower is eating the butterfly!

Nah - this golden Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly is going deep into a non-native Gladiolus flower (Iridaceae) in the garden. These large butterflies - I guess our largest? - have been scarce this year. They should be working Humboldt Lilies (Lilium humboldtii) in the canyon, but drought and parasites have knocked down the native lilies this summer. I hope more of these butterflies will visit the garden! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2007)

Compare the Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly in my [Previous] photo.
CommonNames: Western Tiger Swallowtail; Humboldt Lilies; Pale Swallowtail
ScientificName: Papilio rutulus; Gladiolus; Iridaceae; Lilium humboldtii; Papilio eurymedon
associatedTaxa: Papilio rutulus; Gladiolus; Iridaceae; Lilium humboldtii; Papilio eurymedon

File.Name: 7717b_7717-143.jpg
Notes:
Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly snuggling into a flower of Crocosmia masonorum (or maybe a hybrid) in the Iris (Iridaceae) family in the garden. These plants can become invasive - we collected it browing "wild" near the Humboldt lagoons - but I think we're too summer-dry for it to spread into the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2007)

This is our most common swallowtail butterfly. It does get some yellow color, but there's no way to confuse it with the critter in my [Next] photo.

File.Name: 7717c_7716-116.jpg
Notes:
I found this egg-sized empty silk cocoon of a native Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus) on the ground in the woods yesterday. There's the remains of a pupal case inside, but I'm not sure if the large moth got out or a preditor got in. I collected the cocoon and put it in my shirt pocket. D'oh - I forgot and scrunched it when I got home. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2007)

I usually find a few of these cocoons every year, usually on Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. They are often parasitized. The cocoon feels and sounds like touching the paper cone of a stereo speaker. It's a magical sound, and Native American Chumash shamans made rattles from these cocoons. The adult moths are scarce, and the cocoons are a rare find. I've been collecting them for years. I have a basket full, and I plan to make my own rattle when the time is right. The adult moths are rare nighttime visitors. I'll watch out for them.
Locality: Rhamnaceae) family. They are often parasitized. The cocoon feels and sounds like touching the paper cone of a stereo speaker. It's a magical sound
CommonNames: Ceanothus Silk Moth; Greenbark Ceanothus; Buckthorn

File.Name: 7717d_7706-150.jpg
Notes: Impressive Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera looking for prey on a flower head of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. Note its stiletto-like proboscis folded under it's timy head. Now that's a scary combination! (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2007)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 7717e_7715-080.jpg
Notes:
This slouching beast is an insect horror monster that adults warn their larvae about!

This is a little Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera) in attack position on a flower bud of Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Scrophulariaceae family. I have no doubt that it's totally aware, looking right at me considering options. (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2007)

These preditors are tiny - smaller than a pencil erasor on end - but they can attack prey much larger than they are. See my photos:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/14285834
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/10618571

File.Name: 7717f_7716-112.jpg
Notes:
This Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly - I love the name - working flowers of non-native Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed (Asclepiadaceae) in the garden. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 July 2007)

Author Vladimir Nabokov made his mark studying metalmark butterflies (and blues) as well as writing novels like Lolita and Pale Fire.
ScientificName: Calephelis nemesis californica; Asclepias tuberosa; Asclepiadaceae; Lolita; Pale Fire
