File.Name: 7718a_7718-157.jpg
Notes: Little Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) in our pond today. The ring in the water looks like a halo. This is not really a pond - just our old upstairs fiberglass bathtub after we cut away the shower stall stuff when we replaced it. Julie added water plants and a little pump to recycle the water. The best trick for getting photos every day is to create little habitats like this! (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2007)

File.Name: 7718b_7718-148.jpg
Notes: Remarkable wasp-mimic fly (order Diptera) on a Dahlia flower in the garden this morning. I guess it's a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae) - maybe genus Spilomyia. I know it sounds impressive to mutter these Latin names, but I know nothing about this critter except that I saw it this morning in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2007)

File.Name: 7718c_7718-149.jpg
Notes: Female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a flower of non-native Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed (Asclepiadaceae) in the garden. I know, I've been taking many photos of these butterflies - but how can I resist? (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2007)

File.Name: 7719a_7719-170.jpg
Notes:
Looking up at summer flowers of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Poppy (Papaveraceae) family with the sky behind. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2007)

This is cropped and resized, but otherwise straight from the camera. I tried playing with saturation and contrast, but I always lost the glow and backed off. It's got that classic Kodachrome/Velvia color-saturation look. Of course I LOVE old Technicolor movies like "Meet Me in St. Louie"!

File.Name: 7719b_7717-134.jpg
Notes: New flower of native Red Thistle (Cirsium occidentale - maybe var. venustum?) just starting to flower in the middle of our summer drought in the third-driest year on record in our Santa Barbara mountains. I'm actually finding more available flowers than insects to visit them in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2007)

File.Name: 7719c_7715-088.jpg
Notes: Brilliant feather-like seed plumes of native Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) in the Rose (Rosaceae) family catching the light with the sun behind. The plumes look dull when the sun is the other way. I guess that's a lesson for life - find the right way to look at the world or *everything* looks dingy! (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2007)

File.Name: 7720a_7720-219.jpg
Notes:
This little bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) is caught by a non-native flower of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed (Asclepiadaceae) in the garden. One of its legs is trapped between the horned hoods of the milkweed flower. The little fly was spinning around like a kite in too much wind. Hard to get a photo - but it finally pulled free without my help. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2007)

I have more info about milkweed traps and pollination at:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/8108247

File.Name: 7720b_7719-159.jpg
Notes:
Handsome Fly (order Diptera) on a leaf in the garden. Maybe "handsome fly" sounds like an oxymoron, but this is an interesting native critter, not one of those bad flies - in fact this fly does some good. I guess it's a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae, order Diptera) - maybe genus Gymnosoma or kin. Typical tachinids are bristly, but this one is different. All these flies are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2007)

It's important to understand: kill these flies along with the bad ones, and your pest situation will only get worse. And you will miss the beauty of these creatures visiting your garden. Unfortunately, that's what's happening in the world. It's disturbing to visit the city (any city) and see mostly pest insects because the rest have been poisoned or robbed of their habitat. Not very interesting for photos either. If you can't find handsome critters like this every day where you live, then you should wonder why and do something about it.
Locality: any city) and see mostly pest insects because the rest have been poisoned or robbed of their habitat. Not very interesting for photos either. If you can't find handsome critters like this every day where you live

File.Name: 7720c_7719-187.jpg
Notes:
Tiny humpbacked Fly (order Diptera) on late flowers of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon Traskiae) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) plant family growing in the mountains. It's not a mosquito, but I have no idea what kind of fly this is or what it does when it's not visiting flowers. With that stout proboscis and long legs, maybe it's Bombyliidae or Empididae or Dolichopodidae? (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2007)

Finding insects on flowers is really like casting a net into the sea. We may catch some critters, but we know nothing about how they live when they're not being caught!
ScientificName: Diptera; Eriodictyon Traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Bombyliidae; Empididae; Dolichopodidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Eriodictyon Traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Bombyliidae; Empididae; Dolichopodidae

File.Name: 7720d_7719-186.jpg
Notes:
This photo is "interesting" rather than beautiful. It's a flower head of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon Traskiae) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) plant family, related to all the Phacelias. Most of the shrubs are finished for the summer, but this one still has a few flower buds about to open at the bottom. (See my [Next] photo.)

What's interesting is that there's a fuzzy white insect gall in the upper part of the photo. The flowers are glandular and sticky, but the gall doesn't seem to be glandular. The gall has an opening on top with pink "lips" - that's unusual. I have several books on insect galls, but none mention any host plant in the Hydrophyllaceae, so this is interesting. I plan to go back and open a gall to see what critter is inside. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2007)

File.Name: 7720e_7720-212.jpg
Notes:
Here's the shed skin of a butterfly's larva and the empty chrysalis it left behind when it flew away in the glory of metamorphosis. I have books about butterflies and caterpillars, but not chrysalids. The plant is a native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family. There's still lots for naturalists to learn! (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2007)

In my experience, caterpillars crawl away from their host plant before pupating. I guess this emerged as a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly, even though they prefer different food plants in the Scrophulariaceae) family. Compare my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/20989217
CommonNames: California Fuschia; Evening Primrose; Variable Checkerspot
ScientificName: Epilobium canum; Onagraceae; Euphydryas chalcedona; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Epilobium canum; Onagraceae; Euphydryas chalcedona; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: 7720f_7720-203.jpg
Notes: Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus - or kin in the family Vespidae , order Hymenoptera) in the garden. They look scary, but they don't bother me. The wasps make these neat open-faced nests of six-sided cells made of something like paper, and they provision their nest with caterpillars and other garden pests. This nest is on a piece of plywood that got turned over during some garden cleanup yesterday. The wasps were perched over the cells fanning their wings like good parents to make a breeze in the hot sun. Of course I turned the board over and moved it to a shady place. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2007)

File.Name: 7721a_5924-021.jpg
Notes:
These are the mud nests of solitary Potter Wasps (order Hymenoptera, family Vespidea, genus Eumeninae) on a stem of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the canyon. The wasps might be genus Eumenes.

I haven't seen the adult wasp - at least I didn't recognize it - but if it has a hair on it's body, I will call it the "Hairy Potter Wasp"! I went looking for these mud nests in the woods today for a photo, but I couldn't find any. I found this photo in my archive from almost two years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2005)

I call these the "Hairy Potter Wasps" - see my [Previous] photos for the "Hairy Potter Bees"! You can tell I got just the last Harry Potter book from Amazon this morning. I've been waiting for it!
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Vespidea; Eumeninae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Eumenes
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Vespidea; Eumeninae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Eumenes

File.Name: 7721b_7721-011.jpg
Notes:
This is another Leafcutter or Mason bee - aka Potter Bee - (family Megachilidae in the order Hymenoptera) - working a flower of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. The bees are quite hairy, so I reckon this must be a Hairy Potter Bee! Maybe it's not in the genus Anthidium - I don't care! (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2007)

Like Goldilocks met the three bears, I found three different "Hairy Potter Bees" this afternoon. This is the papa-size one. It's the size of a honeybee, and aggressive like a hummingbird! All megachilid bees collect pollen under their abdomens, but they seem to harvest pollen with their legs and face and then transfer it to their abdomen later. See my [Previous] photos. You can compare the size of these bees in my photos using the star-like disk flowers as a ruler.

You can tell I got the last Harry Potter book from Amazon this morning. I've been waiting for it!
CommonNames: Leafcutter; Mason; Potter Bee; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 7721c_7721-038.jpg
Notes:
This is a Leafcutter or Mason bee - aka Potter Bee - (family Megachilidae in the order Hymenoptera) working a flower of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden. The bees are quite hairy, so I reckon this must be a Hairy Potter Bee! Maybe it's not in the genus Anthidium - I don't care! (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2007)

This is the the mama-size "Hairy Potter Bee" (see my [Previous] photos) transfering pollen from her legs to her abdomen. I watched these bees working the flowers this afternoon as I was trying to get photos in the wind. They don't drag their ass over the flowers (*pardon*) like it sounds like in the books, but they plow through the flowers collecting pollen on face and legs. Then they transfer to their abdomen like this - it takes a minute or more.

You can tell I got the last Harry Potter book from Amazon this morning. I've been waiting for it!
Locality: *pardon*) like it sounds like in the books
CommonNames: Leafcutter; Mason; Potter Bee; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

Like Goldilocks met the three bears, I found three different "Hairy Potter Bees" this afternoon. This is the baby-size one. All megachilid bees collect pollen under their abdomens, but they harvest pollen with their legs and face and then transfer it to their abdomen. See my [Next] photos.

File.Name: 7721f_7720-216.jpg
Notes:
Another photo of this little bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) caught by a non-native flower of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed (Asclepiadaceae) in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2007)

The fly did get free just as I was deciding to help it. (And wondering how!) See my [Previous] photo for more info.

File.Name: 7722a_7722-056.jpg
Notes:
Mystery Critter #1 of 6.

I found this mystery critter working summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family along East Camino Cielo - our mountain ridge road heading toward sunrise - near the high place between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro. I'll hit the books, and try to figure it out for my [Next] photo tomorrow! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 July 2007)

It's been getting harder and harder to find new photos in the woods in this drought year at the peak of our dry summer season. This critter is really tiny - mosquito size, though it sure is built differently. If you have any idea what this critter is, please let me know!

File.Name: 7723a_7722-088.jpg
Notes:
New summer flowers of Scarlet Beardtongue (Keckiella ternata in the Scrophulariaceae family) growing near the top of La Cumbre Peak, almost 4,000 feet above Santa Barbara. It's a classic "hummingbird flower" - though I didn't see any hummers visiting today. This straggly shrub has long flowering stalks with leaves of threes, and shorter more congested non-flowering stems with bright red alternate leaves. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 July 2007)

Today's photos are mostly interesting native plants that are still flowering in the heat of summer in this drought year near the top of our mountains. Today was not a good photo day. It was cloudy - we even heard some thunder, but no rain so far. I spent several hours this afternoon trying to identify that remarkable "mystery fly" I photographed yesterday. If you know about Diptera, please look at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/23134819
and the six photos [Previous] to it!

File.Name: 7723b_7722-046.jpg
Notes:
Are these little spiderlings or pollen grains on this flower?

This is looking very close at a new summer flower of California Fuschia (Epilobium canum - aka Zauschneria californica) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family. I see these flowers every summer - I'm sure those are little bundles of pollen, though they sure looks like they have legs! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 July 2007)

These are the native flowers that get the hummingbirds through the dry summer, but they are not doing well this year. It's a drought year, and all summer flowers are scarce - and the Forest Service is making up for years of neglect by trimming along the mountain roads this summer because of the fire hazard. I can't complain, but the fuschias are partial to exposed rocky place - like roadsides - and they've taken a hit from the trimming. We have at least five hummers (hard to count) emptying the feeder in our garden every day.

File.Name: 7723c_7722-102.jpg
Notes: All summer flowers are beautiful in a drought year! Here are new composite flowers of Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in the mountains. This is a small shrub that usually flowers later in the dry summer. I found these flowers near the top of La Cumbre Peak, the high point of our mountains. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 July 2007)

File.Name: 7723d_7722-081.jpg
Notes: Beautiful summer flowers of Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Scrophulariaceae family. These pretty flowers are not only flowering in the middle of our drought summer, they are growing in hard-packed soil along the mountain road! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 July 2007) Lynn, these flowers are still looking good about a mile east of the Arroyo Burro shooting place. I find them here every summer, also near the third crossing in Paradise - though I think the Forest Service has closed the road for the summer. The flowers are not common.

File.Name: 7723e_7714-019.jpg
Notes:
Unusual summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family. They are unusual because they are flowers in the middle of summer in a drought year when all flowers are scarce! Other monardella have a wonderful mint fragrance - better than mint! - but these have a musty smell that is not quite pleasant. This is the flower that the mystery fly in my pervious photos seems specially made for. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2007)

There are summer flowers - even in this very dry drought summer - but you have to hunt for them. These monardellas are remarkable because I find them in both our shady north-slope canyon and on exposed south-facing chaparral slopes.

File.Name: 7723f_7722-062.jpg
Notes:
I spent several hours today trying to identify this little fly that I found yesterday - with no luck. It's the most interesting little critter that I've found in a long time. Keep clicking [Previous] to see the six photos I posted yesterday! I thought it would be easy to identify with that huge proboscis and those amazing faux "eyes" on its back that perfectly mimic a jumping spider when the fly is butts-up diving into a flower!

Try clicking back and forth between these two photos:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/18732092
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/23105582

I still don't know what this little Fly (order Diptera) is. It's only a few millimeters long. Here it is perfectly camouflaged on a sandstone outcrop near the uncommon summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family that it was working. Fly and flower seem made for each other.

I got these photos a few miles from home on the high point between the Castle and Arroyo Burro. We have the same flowers at home, but I haven't noticed the insects. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 July 2007)

I don't really want to collect (aka kill) one of these critters if I don't have to. I guess the next step to send photos to sites like http://bugguide.net/ and http://www.whatsthatbug.com/ - and visit the local Natural History Museum and University to talk with real experts. If you have any idea what this critter is, please let me know!
Locality: aka kill) one of these critters if I don't have to.  I guess the next step to send photos to sites like http://bugguide.net/ and http://www.whatsthatbug.com/ - and visit the local Natural History Museum and University to talk with real experts.  If you have any idea what this critter is
CommonNames: Fly ; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Mint

File.Name: 7724a_7722-090.jpg
Notes:
This is a fat green acorn of native Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus). These beautiful not-quite-real-oak-trees in the Oak (Fagaceae) family have erect catkins and Chestnut-like bristles on the acorn cups. They remind me of something from the ocean. We have these trees in the canyon at home, but they only flower up high where I can't get close. I got this photo (and the [Next]) near the top of La Cumbre Peak looking down on Santa Barbara, near 4,000 feet elevation. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 July 2007)

Today's photos are all acorns from different kinds of native oaks - they are the seeds of life and food for the other life in our mountains. This acorn photo collection is still not complete - I need Canyon Oak and Valley Oak in our mountains, and Black Oak and Leather Oak in the backcountry. And many more interesting native oaks on the Channel Islands - where I'll be next weekend!

File.Name: 7724b_7722-092.jpg
Notes: New acorns of our native scrub form of Interior Live Oak (Q. wislizenii var. wislizenii). We have several different "scrub oaks" in our mountains that are confusing to tell apart even when you know what to look for. This scrub oak has leaves that vary from smooth to toothed, but they are always shiny green underneath. My books say the acorns take two years to mature, so maybe these are last year's crop? (Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 July 2007)

File.Name: 7724c_7719-181.jpg
Notes:
New acorns on an interesting native oak in our mountains. I believe it is a "Shreve Oak" - which might be Quercus shrevei; or Q. parvula; or Q. parvula var. shrevei; or maybe Q. wislizenii var. frutescens, or even Q. wislizenii. Either way, it's a local oak in the Fagaceae family. Let the botanists draw wands and fight it out!

Whatever it's called, this is a small tree with leaves that are shiny and hairless on top and bottom. It usually grows in colonies, and it looks like a tree-sized version of the scrub-sized Interior Live Oak (Q. wislizenii var. wislizenii). The smooth scaled acorns are very similar to the scrub oak. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2007)
ScientificName: Quercus shrevei; Q. parvula; Q. parvula; shrevei; Q. wislizenii; frutescens; Q. wislizenii; Fagaceae; Q. wislizenii; wislizenii
associatedTaxa: Quercus shrevei; Q. parvula; Q. parvula; shrevei; Q. wislizenii; frutescens; Q. wislizenii; Fagaceae; Q. wislizenii; wislizenii

File.Name: 7724d_7717-136.jpg
Notes: Fat new acorns of native Blue Oak Trees (Quercus douglasii - Fagaceae) growing on a dry flat along the summer-dry Santa Ynez River near Sage Hill. The leaves aren't really blue, but they have a distinctive blue-green tint that is more visible from a distance. These oaks aren't common in our mountains - they prefer the hotter inland mountains - but they sometimes form beautiful groves that remind me of abandoned orchards. (Santa Ynez River, 17 July 2007)

File.Name: 7724e_7718-150.jpg
Notes:
A plump trio of new acorns on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia - Fagaceae) in the woods. Even in this harsh drought summer, the scrub oaks are making acorns. That will be good news for wildlife come fall. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2007)

It interesting that we also have more apples on our two trees thjan we've ever had before. Julie finally propped up the branches this week so they wouldn't break from the weight. What is it about a drought year that encourages some life?

File.Name: 7724f_7714-026.jpg
Notes:
New acorns of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia - Fagaceae) in the woods. We live in an oak woodland, but the dominant large oak trees aren't making many acorns in this drought year. Fortunately for the wildlife, the scrub oaks and other oaks are doing well. Today's photos are all acorns from different kinds of oaks - seeds of life and food for all other life in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2007)

This photo is also a reminder that acorns begin as the female flowers of oak trees. You can see the Y-shaped pollen-receptive female stigma at the tip of each new acorn where conception begins.

File.Name: 7725a_7725-123.jpg
Notes:
Take care, take care - it's a wild world out there!

Adolescent Katydid (family Tettigoniidae in the order Orthoptera) - maybe a Fork-tailed Bush Katydid (Scudderia furcata) - on a leaf in the garden this morning. The tiny bejeweled nymphs have no green, and the large adults are all green. This critter is in-between - like being in middle school. Note the cute little "training wings!" (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2007)

File.Name: 7725b_7721-004.jpg
Notes: This "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) in the garden has 5-segmented antennae, so I guess it's a "Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae or kin. Most of these bugs don't stink at all - they smell like the herb cilantro! This bug seems to be working one of the ray flowers of this Dahlia in the garden with it's long proboscis. I don't think of these critters as pollinators, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2007)

File.Name: 7725c_7724-115.jpg
Notes: This beautiful Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly blew through the garden yesterday, stopping for a moment to visit the last flowers of our well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii). It's been windy this week. I got this photo with my long lens and high ISO setting. I've only seen a few of these golden swallowtails this summer. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2007)

File.Name: 7725d_7725-121.jpg
Notes:
You can tell this sister has been around!

This California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly is resting for a moment in the garden. This veteran butterfly must have many stories to tell of close encounters with birds in the field! Life is tough for butterflies. They are big and beautiful and fluttery, which also makes them perfect targets. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2007)

File.Name: 7725e_7722-045.jpg
Notes: Dark white-fringed Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly - or is it a Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis)? Both butterflies have the white fringe on their hind wings. The common names say a lot! They are not my favorite butterflies to photograph, but they probably are our most common butterflies working the flowers in this drought summer. It seems to have white spots on the lower wing, but the upper wing shows that they are really windows reflecting the orange color of the Zinnia flower below. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2007)

File.Name: 7725f_7722-098.jpg
Notes: Erect staminate catkins of the same native Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree that I show with an acorn in my [Previous] photo. This is not a "true oak" - though they are both in the Fagaceae family. The difference is that oaks in the genus Quercus have "pendent" catkins that dangle, but these tanoaks in the genus Lithocarpus stand up. I believe there are Quercus-oaks that have the same chestnut-like spines on the acorn cups. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 July 2007)

File.Name: 7726a_7725-132.jpg
Notes:
My photos must give the wrong impression. This is one of our driest years ever, and summer is the driest time. Driving over our mountains - even walking past - there's not much to see in this drought summer. So it's almost a shock to find these beautiful native flowers in an exposed place!

These are interesting native flowers of Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala). This is an unusual composite (family Asteraceae) plant with heads of two-lipped mint-like flowers that are unlike any other composite I know. (Compare my [Previous] photo - the petals of each little composite flower are arranged in symmetry.) Usually I find Sacapellote in June, but I didn't see any in this dry year. This is growing in a place that was cut to the ground as a firebreak last spring. Sometimes that seems to help plants flower late - maybe they store early moisture in their roots? (Santa Ynez Mountains, 25 July 2007) I brightened this photo a bit, but I didn't touch the color saturation. This is right from my camera.

File.Name: 7726b_7722-051.jpg
Notes: Pretty little Beetle (order Coleoptera) working a new flower head of Miss Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. There are still new summer flowers starting to bloom in the mountains to keep the insects alive in the middle of this harsh drought summer. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 July 2007)
CommonNames: Beetle; Miss Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis; Composite
ScientificName: Coleoptera) working a new flower head of Miss Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera) working a new flower head of Miss Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae

File.Name: 7726c_7725-127.jpg
Notes:
Golden tendrils and flowers of native Dodder (probably Cuscuta californica) wrapping around a stem of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the woods. Dodder is a true parasitic vine without chlorophyll that starts as a seedling, but loses it's connection with the ground and lives by sucking the juices of other plants. The bumps on some of the orange stems in the photo are the haustoria that insert themselves into the vascular system of the host plant.

Creepy - like vampire plants! But Dodder is a real flowering plant, and you can see the little white flowers in this photo. Sometimes they are placed in their own Cuscutaceae family, and sometimes they are included in the Morning Glory (Convolvulaceae) plant family. Even the botanists have trouble with this strange invader! (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2007)
ScientificName: Cuscuta californica; Keckiella cordifolia; haustoria; Cuscutaceae; Convolvulaceae
associatedTaxa: Cuscuta californica; Keckiella cordifolia; haustoria; Cuscutaceae; Convolvulaceae

File.Name: 7726d_7721-040.jpg
Notes: This is a large Bumble Bee (family Bombus in the order Hymenoptera) perched on a petal of Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) in the garden yesterday. It was the end of the day, and the sun was about to go down behind the trees. This bee just sat quietly in front of the flower - not gathering pollen or nectar, neither toil nor spin - as though savoring the moment and wondering what it's for. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2007)
CommonNames: Bumble Bee; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 7726e_7724-112.jpg
Notes:
Little immature Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) on a composite seed head in the garden. I was happy so see this critter because I've seen very few grasshoppers in the woods so far in this drought summer. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2007)

The Katydid in my [Previous] two photos is a Long-Horned Grasshopper (family Tettigoniidae). Sometimes size does matter!

File.Name: 7726f_7725-124.jpg
Notes:
I can't resist another photo of this adolescent Katydid (family Tettigoniidae in the order Orthoptera) - maybe a Fork-tailed Bush Katydid (Scudderia furcata) - on a leaf in the garden yesterday. See my [Previous] photo for more info. It looks like it's admiring its own shadow - like Narcissus - but it's really getting ready to hop away from the darn photographer that's "bugging" it! (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2007)

The antennae should be almost as long as the hind legs, but you can tell from the shadow that its right antenna is mostly missing. I've always wondered - do insects and spiders get back their missing body parts during the next molt?

File.Name: 7727a_7725-139.jpg
Notes:
Little Hummingbird sticking its tongue out in the garden with the sun behind. It has angel wings!

Sure there's a hummingbird feeder up above out of the frame. Summer flowers are scarce in this drought year, so I've been helping the hummers. I guess this is a female Black-chinned Hummingbird (Archilochus alexandri) - though I'm getting really confused! We have 6 or more hummers at a time working the feeder - Black-chinned, Anna's, and now a Rufous/Allen's - and immatures as well.

No time for photos today. We're packing up to leave before sunrise to catch the boat out to Santa Cruz Island 20+ miles across the channel. This is like going back in time. We'll be camping on the west end of the island to do some restoration work scouting and removing invasive weeds. I'll be busy, but I hope to get some interesting photos! We'll be back on Tuesday.

Take a look at: http://www.channelislandsrestoration.com/
Locality: Archilochus alexandri) - though I'm getting really confused! We have 6 or more hummers at a time working the feeder - Black-chinned

File.Name: 7731a_7728-051.jpg
Notes:
I believe this is a Western Tailed-blue (Everes amyntula) butterfly perched on an inflated seed pod of Southern California Locoweed (Astragalus trichopodus var. lonchus) in the Pea (Fabaceae) family. I found this pretty butterfly in remote Pozo Canyon in the southwest corner of Santa Cruz Island after a long hike. (Santa Cruz Island, 28 July 2007)

We spent the last four days on the west end of Santa Cruz Island doing some volunteer restoration work scouting and removing invasive weeds. Take a look at: http://www.channelislandsrestoration.com/
CommonNames: Western Tailed-blue; Southern California Locoweed; Pea
ScientificName: Everes amyntula; Astragalus trichopodus; lonchus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Everes amyntula; Astragalus trichopodus; lonchus; Fabaceae

File.Name: 7731b_7728-038.jpg
Notes: Pretty composite flower head of Cliff Dandelion (Malacothrix saxatilis var. implicata) growing on the west end of Santa Cruz Island near Christy Beach. Other forms of this island endemic grow on the mainland near home. I would need my books and a magnifying glass to tell them apart. (Santa Cruz Island, 28 July 2007)

File.Name: 7731c_7729-101.jpg
Notes: Native succulent Dudleya in the Stonecrop (Crassulaceae) family growing near Christy Ranch on the west end of Santa Cruz Island. I guess this is the island endemic "Greene's Dudleya" (Dudleya greenei), but compare D. candelabrum). (Santa Cruz Island, 29 July 2007)
ScientificName: Dudleya; Crassulaceae; Dudleya greenei; D. candelabrum
associatedTaxa: Dudleya; Crassulaceae; Dudleya greenei; D. candelabrum

File.Name: 7731d_7728-046.jpg
Notes:
Pretty flowers of Island Paintbrush (Castilleja lanata ssp. hololeuca) growing along the dry creek in Pozo Canyon on the south-west corner of Santa Cruz Island. Despite the description in the flora, this is a real woody shrub that grows several feet high. (Santa Cruz Island, 28 July 2007)

It's odd that this endemic almost-shrub is considered a subspecies of a plant that only grows in northern Mexico and Texas. Go figure!

File.Name: 7731e_7728-059.jpg
Notes:
Clown-colored pea-flowers of Island Deerweed (Lotus dendroideus var. dendroideus). This island endemic is more shrub-like than our similar Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) at home across the channel. It doesn't have green stems, and it keeps its leaves in summer. (Santa Cruz Island, 28 July 2007)

This deerweed only grows on the Channel Islands - mainland plants are different. All my plant photos today show flowers that are endemic to the islands.

File.Name: 7731f_7727-009.jpg
Notes:
Thar she blows!

My [Previous] photo is a little hummingbird. This photo is as different as can be!

On our way to Santa Cruz Island across the Santa Barbara Channel last Friday, we came upon a pod of four giant Blue Whales (Balaenoptera musculus), and of course we slowed down and circled for a close look. These whales are believed to be the largest animals that ever lived on Earth. They grow as long as 33 metres (110 ft) in length and 200 tons or more in weight. You can see this whale blow, and a bit of its right pectoral fin out of the water as it rolls. That's the east end of Santa Cruz Island in the background. (Santa Cruz Island, 27 July 2007)

We spent the last four days on the west end of the island doing some volunteer restoration work scouting and removing invasive weeds. Take a look at: http://www.channelislandsrestoration.com/

File.Name: 7801a_7729-108.jpg
Notes:
Remarkable flower of native Fringed Indian Pink (Silene laciniata ssp. major) in the Pink (Caryophyllaceae) family growing near the Christy Ranch on the west end of Santa Cruz Island. The species name "laciniata" sounds like "lacerate" - irregularly slashed and jagged as if torn. Freddy Krueger comes to mind, but I'd rather think of Henri Matisse. I can see a whimsical little cut-out fish with open mouth on the right! (Santa Cruz Island, 29 July 2007)

My photos today are all RED - more or less. The red flowers are all classic hummingbird flowers with long tubes keeping the nectar out of reach from other pollinators, and exerted stamens and pistils to transfer pollen via the hummers' heads. All of these plant species are also flowering in the canyon at home on the mainland.

File.Name: 7801b_7729-118.jpg
Notes: Brilliant red flowers of Scarlet Monkeyflower (Mimulus cardinalis) in the Scrophulariaceae family growing along the creek near the Christy Ranch on the west end of Santa Cruz Island. We have the same summer flowers in our bathtub-pond at home on the mainland. (Santa Cruz Island, 29 July 2007)

File.Name: 7801c_7729-106.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful summer flowers of California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family growing near the Christy Ranch on the west end of Santa Cruz Island. I got this photo early in the morning fog. These classic hummingbird flowers are almost too red! (Santa Cruz Island, 29 July 2007)

See my [Previous] photo for very different "flowers' on the fuschia plants.

File.Name: 7801d_7728-074.jpg
Notes:
At first I thought this was a non-native Australian Saltbush (Atriplex semibaccata) because of the skinny leaves and red "berries". Then I did a double-take and realized this is a California Fuschia (Epilobium canum) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family, which has very different flowers - see my [Next] photo. (Santa Cruz Island, 28 July 2007)

The red bud-like swellings are interesting insect galls. Galls are a specialized kind of reaction to wounding caused by insects - usually fly midges or tiny wasps who insert their eggs. Neither of my books on galls - or a web search - mentions this one, though there is a reference to an Epilobium stem gall caused by a Mompha micro-moth. These galls are different. They are detachable with a hard dry center. Now here's a real science fair project - hatch these critters and find out what they are. Please let me know if you find out!
CommonNames: Australian Saltbush; California Fuschia; Evening Primrose

File.Name: 7801e_7728-083.jpg
Notes: Ripe fruit of native Lemonade Berry (Rhus integrifolia) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) family. The ripe berries are pleasantly sour at first, but they soon turn bitter. It's a "suck and spit" fruit - a great design for seed propagation! (Santa Cruz Island, 28 July 2007)

File.Name: 7801f_7730-195.jpg
Notes:
Breakfast on Santa Cruz Island featuring pancakes with fresh elderberry syrup!

Julie picked the berries from a huge native Blue Elderberry (Sambucus mexicana) tree in the Honeysuckle (Caprifoliaceae) family - now moved to the Adoxaceae family - growing in remote Pozo Canyon in the southwest corner of Santa Cruz Island. Julie boiled the berries down for several hours with some sugar - not too much - and the result was perfect, tart and tasty, maybe the best pancake topping ever! (Santa Cruz Island, 30 July 2007)

The breakfast plate is sitting on one of our coolers. The dirty smudges are footprints of a little housecat-sized Island Fox (Urocyon littoralis santacruzae) who visited during the night. The endemic foxes almost disappeared in the last decade, but they are coming back after major efforts to remove golden eagles and wild pigs from the island. There's more info here:
   http://www.nps.gov/chis/naturescience/island-fox.htm
Locality: Urocyon littoralis santacruzae) who visited during the night. The endemic foxes almost disappeared in the last decade
ScientificName: Sambucus mexicana; Caprifoliaceae; Adoxaceae; Urocyon littoralis santacruzae
associatedTaxa: Sambucus mexicana; Caprifoliaceae; Adoxaceae; Urocyon littoralis santacruzae

File.Name: 7802a_7729-154.jpg
Notes:
I found some time for photos on the west end of Santa Cruz Island last week, but this was primarily a work trip to eradicate a nasty invader from several canyons. The plant is Tamarisk aka Saltcedar (Tamarix ramosissima) in the Tamaricaceae family. These non-native shrubs or small trees have been invading western rivers for decades. I remember floating for miles along the Green River in Utah with no place to land our raft because the Tamarisk had completely taken over the river banks. According to my flora, the plant was first collected on Santa Cruz Island in 1977. There's still time to control it - at least on the islands - if we act now.

Tamarisk has these pretty purple flowers and cedar-like foliage. We were using GPS waypoints to track down plants in the canyons and remove them - like geocaching with a purpose! We pulled hundreds of seedlings, but mature plants like this are more difficult. We cut the stems. Then we bag the flowers and seeds to transport away from the island. Then we squirt the cut stems on the ground with a shot of the herbicide Garlon to hopefully kill the root. It was a productive weekend! (Santa Cruz Island, 29 July 2007)

I'm sure you can find your own way to help the environment at home. We were doing volunteer restoration work with the "Channel Islands Restoration" non-profit group. Take a look at:
   http://www.channelislandsrestoration.com/

File.Name: 7802b_7729-115.jpg
Notes:
Summer Asters - even in a drought year on a desert island!

I got this photo early on a foggy morning - the regular fog drip surely helps the natives near the west end of Santa Cruz Island. This is a native California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) flower in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. The current (and aging) Jepson Manual renamed the Corethrogyne genus and simplified it. But this island form is surely different from our summer asters at home on the mainland! (Santa Cruz Island, 29 July 2007)

File.Name: 7802c_7729-120.jpg
Notes: Pretty flowers of Western Vervain (Verbena lasiostachys var. scabrida) in the Verbenaceae family. The ring of flowers works up the stem as old ones drop and new ones open - just like life! This plant is growing in wet soil by the flowing creek at Christy Beach on the west end of Santa Cruz Island. We have the species in the mountains at home, but our plants are more purple and less luscious. (Santa Cruz Island, 29 July 2007)

File.Name: 7802d_7727-017.jpg
Notes: Common Monkey Flower (Mimulus guttatus) growing along the running creek near Christy Beach on the west end of Santa Cruz Island. This native herb with pretty flowers grows wherever there is flowing water - which makes it scarce in this near-record drought year in Southern California. (Santa Cruz Island, 27 July 2007)

File.Name: 7802e_7727-019.jpg
Notes: Pretty yellow flowers of native Beach Evening Primrose (Camissonia cheiranthifolia ssp. cheiranthifolia) growing above the tide line on the dunes at Christy Beach on the west end of Santa Cruz Island. They grow on protected beaches on the mainland, but it's much more interesting to find them on the islands! (Santa Cruz Island, 27 July 2007)

File.Name: 7802f_7729-110.jpg
Notes:
Bright yellow flowers of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii var. vernonioides) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. These native shrubs are widely scattered on the beach and up into the hills. This plant looks unusually pubescent in the photo. (Santa Cruz Island, 29 July 2007)

Yesterday was all red. Today is yellow and violet. These are all photos from our trip to the west end of Santa Cruz Island, 20+ miles off the coast from home in the Santa Barbara Mountains.

File.Name: 7803a_7803-052.jpg
Notes:
Oh shit...

Fire on the mountain and it's heading our way. I got this photo from Stagecoach Road near the bridge at about 10:30 tonight - a one second exposure using my 200 mm lens on a tripod. The fire is getting that close. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2007)

This is the Zaca Wildfire that started a month ago 20+ miles away. It has been burning for weeks through the backcountry wilderness. By now it has burned 44,000 acres and it's heading towards our mountains, and my home. The Paradise area of the Santa Ynez River - where I get many of these summer photos - has been evacuated. Up here on San Marcos Pass, we are under an evacuation warning. Highway 154 is closed. Next behind us is the city of Santa Barbara. Yipes.

Wish us well. Tomorrow will be a busy day.
There's current fire info at http://www.inciweb.org/incident/770/

File.Name: 7804a_7804-062.jpg
Notes:
We're still home - the fire situation is looking better tonight, but I don't want to jinx it!

The Zaca Wildfire is still burning out of control, but the action seems to be moving east away from us into the Dick Smith Wilderness area behind Little Pine Mountain. That's a huge rugged wilderness area, and the Forest Service doesn't expect containment until September. Damn - that's when our fire season really starts! (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2007)

We had an interesting day expecting evacuation orders at any moment. It's a good exercise to look at all your stuff and decide what's REALLY important: of course my camera; my 500 GB external hardrive with all my photos and writing and music; my vintage guitar and grandpa's fiddle; a box of important papers and family photos; Julie has her own stuff; a travel cage ready for our cat; some clothes and sleeping bags and bathroom stuff... With time to kill, I added a dozen old natural history books I wouldn't know how to replace, and I still haven't finished the last Harry Potter book. Our important stuff is all ready to go piled up on the porch. I'm sure it won't fill the pickup truck.

The white cloud-tops of the smoke plumes raise some stumpers. Why are they shaped like nuclear bomb mushroom clouds? Why do the plumes of dingy smoke become pure white cumulus thunderhead clouds on top? There should be a name for these clouds - I call them FIREHEADS.

File.Name: 7804b_7804-019.jpg
Notes:
We're still home - the fire situation is looking better, but I don't want to jinx it!

This is the view early this morning from the same place I got my [Previous] photo last night. The ridge in the foreground is Fremont Ridge that leads up to our house on San Marcos Pass. Beyond that (hidden) is the Santa Ynez River and Paradise Road. The fire is burning in the Santa Cruz Creek drainage beyond. It looked like the fire might run down to the river and up to us, but the wind was helpful today and pushed the fire east (right) and away from us. It's still smoldering here, and a new wind tonight could change everything. (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2007)

Note the white cloud-tops of the smoke plumes. See my [Next] photo.

File.Name: 7805a_7804-042.jpg
Notes:
This brilliant Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly looks like it just emerges from its chrysalis. It's working new non-native Agapanthus flowers in the garden. These "Lily of the Nile" plants are common landscape plants in Santa Barbara. They are kind of boring en masse, but our single plant fits nicely in the garden, and the butterflies like it. (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2007)

This "yellow-form 'Black' Swallowtail" looks something like the Western Tiger Swallowtail (P. rutulus) in my [Previous] photo, but it is smaller and has much more black along the forewing.

The name "Anise Swallowtail" is really a misnomer. These butterflies are native, and they have a taste for the introduced Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family that grows as a weed in our mountains. They use other Apiaceae) species like like Sanicula and Lomatium and Tauschia as larval host plants, but they seem to prefer the invasive fennel. Good! They have potential as a natural biological control of this bad weed. There's enough fennel for gazillions of butterflies!

I have many photos of the large caterpillars, e.g.
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/21755229
Locality: Apiaceae) family that grows as a weed in our mountains. They use other Apiaceae) species like like Sanicula and Lomatium and Tauschia as larval host plants
CommonNames: Anise Swallowtail; Western Tiger Swallowtail; Fennel; Parsley

File.Name: 7805b_7804-031.jpg
Notes: Even with smoke in the air, there are new butterflies in the garden! This Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly is working an unkown perennial herb. If you recognize the flowers, please let me know! (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2007)

File.Name: 7805c_7803-040.jpg
Notes: Healing Purple Coneflower (Echinaceae purpurea) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family growing in the garden. I took this photo early Friday morning before we knew that the Zaca wildfire was starting to move towards us. The red sun shining through the smoke adds to the color. (San Marcos Pass, 3 August 2007)

File.Name: 7805d_7804-052.jpg
Notes:
This is the thunderhead cloud-top of the same fire plume in my [Previous] photo. This is the view from our porch yesterday afternoon. The Zaca wildfire seems to be mostly moving past us through the backcountry to the east. But as I type this, a fire crew is hand cutting a fuel break on the ridge above our house. I guess they are still worried, so I am too. (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2007)

I call these wildfire clouds "fireheads". I did some research last night and discovered they are properly called Pyrocumulonimbus (PyroCb) clouds. The clouds in our wildfire have been reaching 25,000 - 35,000 feet. They can produce lightning and rain, but the rain probably won't make it to the ground.

Fire and water seem like opposites, but they have interesting connections that produce these clouds:

* It's an old middle school science teacher's trick to hold a dry glass over a candle flame. In a moment, water will condense inside the glass. The chemical "fire equation" is something like this: CnHm + O2 --> CO2 + H20. Fire really does produce water vapor as a product of combustion!

* Fire evaporates more water from the ground and burning plants.

* Hot smoke and water vapor rise by convection into an area of lower atmospheric pressure. In time the smoke cools and gets pushed out of the way by more hot air rising from below. That creates the mushroom cloud shape. (Nuclear bombs work the same way.)

* As water vapor cools in lower pressure, it condenses into droplets, and the smoke provides plenty of particles to act as nucleation sites - like cloud seeding!

The result is that wildfires can make their own pyrocumulonimbus rainclouds! I'm curious why these fireheads only form sometimes. I'm sure they require a very intense firestorm on the ground to generate strong convection. Maybe atmospheric conditions make a difference too?

After I took this photo, I set up my camera on a tripod and started taking a photo every 15 seconds for more than an hour. I ended up taking almost 300 photos until my battery gave out. I've only looked at them as a very fast slideshow, and the motion in the cloud is amazing and beautiful. I'll figure out how to make a proper movie and post it on youtube when I find time.
Locality:
Nuclear bombs work the same way.)

* As water vapor cools in lower pressure

File.Name: 7806a_7806-117.jpg
Notes:
Perfect Allen's Hummingbird (Selasphorus sasin) or maybe a Rufous Hummingbird (S. rufus) in the garden today. It's a female or an immature of one of these two species, but I don't know which. These pretty hummers aren't residents in our mountains, they just travel by and stay for a few day as they migrate between north and south. I believe they winter along the Pacific coast of central Mexico. Let me know if you see this pretty bird in Mexico! (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2007)

Note the little blood-red teardrop shape on the hummer's throat. It's an iridescent patch that changes color with the light. It looks like a badge or emblem.

It's interesting that there is a race of Allen's Hummingbird (S.s. sedentarius) that is a permanent resident on the Channel Islands off the Santa Barbara coast. I think I saw them on our trip to Santa Cruz Island last week.
CommonNames: Allen's Hummingbird; Rufous Hummingbird; Allen's Hummingbird
ScientificName: Selasphorus sasin; S. rufus; S.s. sedentarius
associatedTaxa: Selasphorus sasin; S. rufus; S.s. sedentarius

File.Name: 7806b_7731-010.jpg
Notes:
Pretty little Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly spread over non-native summer flowers of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed (Asclepiadaceae) in the garden. It looks like it's posing to be a museum specimen! (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2007)

There's no pins or poison jars near our garden! I'm happy collecting butterflies with my camera, and I don't mind saying "I don't know" when I don't know.

File.Name: 7806c_7806-135.jpg
Notes: Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly - I love the name - working a non-native Black-Eyed Susan aka Gloriosa Daisy (Rudbeckia hirta) flower in the garden. The stamens of the disk flowers look like little hot crossed buns, but the butterfly is probing down below for nectar. Only small butterflies like this can reach the good stuff. (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2007)
CommonNames: Fatal Metalmark; Black-Eyed Susan; Gloriosa Daisy

File.Name: 7806d_7731-015.jpg
Notes: Pretty little "cruciform" Plume Moth (order Lepidoptera, family Pterophoridae) perched on a perennial flower in the garden that I don't know. This little moth has a mimic-face on its back to scare preditors, but you have to look close to see it in this 500 pixel image. Look at the moth's thorax - between the wings - I see eyes and mouth and a fancy hat! See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 July 2007)

File.Name: 7806e_7804-041.jpg
Notes:
This Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly working a non-native Agapanthus flower in the garden is also a remarkable mimic! You can see its head in the upper left, but tilt your head and look up from the eye-spots and tails in the lower right - I see a scary face with red eyes and blue eyebrows and fangs or claws!

This mimicry only works because these butterflies have the remarkable trick of hovering with just their forewings while keeping their hindwings still. Most moths (in the same order Lepidoptera) have a "frenulum" velcro-like structure to hold both wings together as they fly. Most butterflies lack this structure, but the Anise Swallowtails are the only butterfljies I've seen that separate their fore and hind wings like this. (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2007)

File.Name: 7806f_7804-040.jpg
Notes: Fresh Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly snuggling into a non-native Agapanthus flower in the garden. It looks so content with its legs hanging out over the edge of the flower! (San Marcos Pass, 4 August 2007)

File.Name: 7807a_7807-146.jpg
Notes: Beautiful - or scary? This Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) is curled up on a composite flower of native Common Aster (Aster chilensis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in the garden. The spider has nifty-looking orange bands around it's knees that look like plastic! That little red thing on the right is a scale that fell from an oak tree above, but the spider seems to be holding it like a bait. I wonder what it's fishing for? (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2007)
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Common Aster; Sunflower
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Aster chilensis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Aster chilensis; Asteraceae

File.Name: 7807b_7806-123.jpg
Notes:
Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) collecting pollen from the Sweet Corn (Zea mays var. rugosa) tassles in the garden. I didn't know they did that - but I can hear the buzz of the bees walking by our small corn patch. (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2007)

Just by chance, I happened to read an article in "Science News" (vol. 172, no. 4) today about disappearing bees - what's called "colony-collapse disorder" (CCD). One possibility is that honey bees are being effected by genetically modified crops that contain the Bt toxin derived from the bacterium Bacillus thuringiensis. I quote:

"The toxin is intended to target the caterpillars of moths and butterflies rather than bees, which belong to a different order. In any case, one of the major Bt crops, corn, relies on wind for pollination and isn't a top pollen choice for foraging bees."

Hah!

File.Name: 7807c_7807-153.jpg
Notes: This bee-sized critter looks like a shiny black wasp at first, but it's clearly a Fly (order Diptera) when you look close. I'm pretty sure this is a Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens) resting on a leaf in the garden. The "windows" are the pair of translucent areas that you can see at the base of the abdomen in this photo. What amazing eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2007)
CommonNames: Fly ; Window Fly; Black Soldier Fly

File.Name: 7807d_7805-079.jpg
Notes:
Awesome firestorm growing over Little Pine Mountain on the other side of the Santa Ynez Valley. It's hard to appreciate the immensity of this smoke plume. I can't imagine what it's like on the ground. I've climbed Little Pine - at 4506 feet, it feels huge. At the very bottom of the photo, you can see a bit of Fremont Ridge that leads up to our house. I took this photo on Sunday afternoon. The blue skies are a sign that the wind is blowing away from us. Since then, we've had cooler days and morning fog. But tonight the wind is shifting and the air is smoky. I'm nervous. (San Marcos Pass, 5 August 2007)

At the very top of the smoke plume, you can see the firehead - the smoke has become a pure white Pyrocumulonimbus cloud. For more info about these fire clouds, see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/23544901

File.Name: 7807e_7805-118.jpg
Notes: Beautiful hummingbird perched in the Ceanothus shrub above the garden. It's a female or an immature of either Allen's Hummingbird (Selasphorus sasin) or Rufous Hummingbird (S. rufus), but I don't know which. These species are very hard to tell apart, and they're both welcome in the garden! See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 August 2007)

File.Name: 7808a_7808-160.jpg
Notes:
This perfect little Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) climbed up right in front of me on a Coreopsis leaf in the garden. I was lucky to get my [Previous] photo before it disappeared under the leaves. I waited for a good five minutes before I noticed it looking right at me almost too close to photograph with my macro lens! I got this photo almost full-frame at 1.1 magnification. The frog is not much larger than my thumbnail. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2007)

We're threatened by the Zaca Wildfire right now. I've been posting photos of the natural life in our canyon on San Marcos Pass in the Santa Barbara Mountains for 4+ years now in this fotolog. I know exactly what will be lost if the winds change and the fire comes our way. I hope it doesn't happen, but I know that life - like the phoenix - comes back after fire. Life is tough, we go on.
ScientificName: Pseudacris regilla; Hyla regilla) climbed up right in front of me on a Coreopsis
associatedTaxa: Pseudacris regilla; Hyla regilla) climbed up right in front of me on a Coreopsis

File.Name: 7808b_7808-156.jpg
Notes:
I had my camera and tripod set up close to the ground in the garden today trying to get a photo of some little wasp-like critters that I think are flies resting on the Coreopsis leaves. This perfect little Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) climbed up right in front of me. I only had a moment for one photo before it disappeared. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2007)

Of course I waited quietly for the frog to come back. See my [Next] photo!

File.Name: 7808c_7808-164.jpg
Notes:
I drive out every morning to see what the Zaca Wildfire is doing since we can't see much from our home in the canyon. Usually I go to a place on Stagecoach Road near the bridge and lean on the "bob-wire" fence to get a view. Today I noticed this pretty caterpillar near the top of one of the metal fence stakes. Look close and you can see it's spinning webs to anchor its cocoon. The caterpillar is about five feet high on the stake - a big crawl for this little guy. I'll be back tomorrow morning to see what's happening - with the fire and the caterpillar! (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2007)

As I was setting up my tripod to get this photo, I heard a buzz. Next moment I was in a loud swarm of bees flying past around me! A moment later they were gone. Whatever happens with this wildfire, I will remember the bees!

File.Name: 7808d_7807-152.jpg
Notes: Even with smoke in the air from the approaching Zaca Wildfire, I've been spotting new butterflies in the woods. This is a female Common Checkered-Skipper (Pyrgus communis) butterfly working flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Buckwheat (Polygonaceae) family growing by the mailbox. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2007)

File.Name: 7808e_7808-170.jpg
Notes:
All of Cold Springs Tavern has been wrapped with a fire-resistant material that looks like aluminum foil - like a giant baked potato. This is the restaurant "under wraps". If the fire moves close, fire crews will spray a gel over the foil. This strategy has worked to save several historic cabins in the backcountry. If the Zaca wildfire gets this far, the power lines are already gone. I guess that's what that orange "swimming pool" of water is for. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2007)

We live a couple of miles away from the tavern. We're getting jittery seeing the fire crews working so hard close to home. We spent the day going through our stuff again - adding to the pile by the front door. There are plans in the works for a massive evacuation of Santa Barbara. The wildfire has been quiet, but everything depends on the winds now.

File.Name: 7808f_7808-172.jpg
Notes:
Cold Springs Tavern has been wrapped with a fire-resistant material that looks like aluminum foil - like a giant baked potato. The tavern is a historic stagecoach stop with buildings dating back to the mid-1800's. It's still a great place to hear local music. I had two thoughts at about the same time:

   * Cool - like a Christo art project!

   * Darn - this is only a couple of miles from home, are the fire crews THAT worried??

I have another photo of the tavern from almost the same view at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/8799569

It's hard to look at this photo and imagine Tom and Kenny or my friend Wil Ridge jamming blues under the old maple trees. (San Marcos Pass, 8 August 2007)

File.Name: 7809a_7809-188.jpg
Notes: Adolescent Katydid (family Tettigoniidae in the order Orthoptera) - maybe a Fork-tailed Bush Katydid (Scudderia furcata) - on a Purple Coneflower (Echinaceae purpurea) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family in the garden this morning. What a strong color combination in the morning light! (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2007) Can you spot the little Crab Spider (Thomisidae)? (Hint: the katydid is pointing at it!)

File.Name: 7809b_7802-034.jpg
Notes: Pretty as it is, this new flower of Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family is already fading in the noontide sun. It's a local endemic species that is common in our mountains. I don't know when these morning flowers open, but the fresh petals are violet. They lose their color and melt away before noon. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2007)
CommonNames: Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Sunflower

File.Name: 7809c_7809-195.jpg
Notes: Beautiful red flowers of Scarlet Monkeyflower (Mimulus cardinalis) in the Scrophulariaceae family growing in our little water garden at home. It's just an old fiberglass tub that we put to good use. This will have to do until the Zaca Wildfire is done or moved on, so that Paradise Road to the Santa Ynez River is open again. (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2007)

File.Name: 7809d_7807-142.jpg
Notes: Worn California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly on her high perch in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia - Fagaceae) tree in the canyon. These butterflies are aggresive. They perch in the trees and chase every other butterfly away - this one is checking me out! I'm not sure what they're trying to accomplish. (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2007)

File.Name: 7809e_7809-190.jpg
Notes:
Yesterday's caterpillar is today's chrysalis - literally! I spotted the caterpillar yesterday near the top of a metal fence stake supporting a "bob-wire" fence along Stagecoach Road near the bridge where I go every morning to see what the Zaca Wildfire is up too. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/23647440

Today it has made its cocoon, and the miracle of transformation - caterpillar to butterfly - has begun. Look close, and you can see the silken harness holding the chrysalis against the post. It's interesting that in yesterday's photo, the caterpillar is facing the post. But in this photo, it seems to be facing away. How does it turn around?? (San Marcos Pass, 9 August 2007)

File.Name: 7809f_7807-148.jpg
Notes:
Mama deer and her adolescent fawn under the native Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri) not far from home. One good thing about the Zaca Wildfire (for us) is that the highway has been closed all week. Even with all the fire trucks in the mountains, the deer are coming out. I spotted this pair along the road, switched to my long lens as fast as I could, and took the photo leaning against my truck. D'oh, my camera was still set at f/22 from my last flower shots. This photo is not quite sharp, but it makes up for it with sweetness! (San Marcos Pass, 7 August 2007)

I've lived here for many years, and I'm still confused by our local deer - are they mulies or black-tailed? The local species lists that I trust (eg the UCSB Sedgwick Reserve Mammel List) call them Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus) without qualification. The huge ears look right, but they sure are small compared to Eastern Sierra mule deer.

I probably won't see these deer again soon - except maybe in the garden if I get up early enough. The decision was made to reopen Highway 154 tonight at 8:00. I guess that's encouraging news about the fire, though the sky is still full of smoke, and there are rumors about dangerous winds developing. I have to chuckle seeing different agencies battle over their domains. As I showed in my photos yesterday, Cold Springs Tavern was just "wrapped" with fire-resistant material. Will they now unwrap it for business as usual this weekend?? I'll bet someone is pissed!
Locality: Odocoileus hemionus) without qualification. The huge ears look right

File.Name: 7810a_7810-225.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly holding on to garden Phlox flowers in the wind today. I backed off and used my long lens. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2007)

The wind is bad for photos, and the Zaca Wildfire is still growling in the backcountry. The wind was from the east today, which is good for us, and it quieted down tonight. The wildfire has now burned 80,000 acres - 125+ square miles. Now there is talk of setting fire to the rest of the Santa Barbara backcountry on the edges to contain the wildfire in the middle. We're still waiting.

File.Name: 7810b_7809-196.jpg
Notes: Butterflies get all the glory, but there are many more Moths in the order Lepidoptera. I have no idea what this little micro-moth is, but it's diurnal - working the day shift - on a native flower of Common Aster (Aster chilensis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family growing in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2007)

File.Name: 7810c_7810-231.jpg
Notes: Little Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae in the order Hemiptera) in attack position on a Purple Coneflower (Echinaceae purpurea) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family in the garden. Look how it's holding the disk flower as bait. This critter could sit on a pencil erasor, but I've seen them bring down honeybees and skipper butterflies! (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2007)

File.Name: 7810d_7810-234.jpg
Notes: Nasty looking Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae in the order Hymenoptera) taking a drink from a leaf in the garden. This wasp is large - maybe 3 or 4+ cm long. They twitch their wings constantly. Most Pompilids have metallic colors that depend on the light. You can see a bit of metallic blue on this wasp. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2007)

File.Name: 7810e_7810-210.jpg
Notes: Our bird feeder is like "the days of the old school yard" - pretty nasty as I recall! That's an immature Black-headed Grosbeak (Pheucticus melanocephalus) bird on the left and a cowering Oak Titmouse (Baeolophus inoratus) bird - aka "Plain Titmouse" - on the right. Titmouse birds ("titmice"?) usually have a crest, but this bird is lying low. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2007)

File.Name: 7811a_7811-002.jpg
Notes:
What amazing psychedelic rainbow eyes! I'm sure this is a Fruit Fly in the family Tephritidae of the order Diptera. It's long brown rigid ovipositor - visible in the upper-right - is pretty amazing too. There are 300+ species in North America, and many fruit flies are important pest species. I found this one on an artichoke flower in the school garden today. They are not all bad. At least two species have been introduced from Europe to control invasive Yellow Star Thistle and Spotted Knapweed. (Santa Ynez Valley, 11 August 2007)

The Zaca Wildfire continues to burn, but the expected winds never happened today. There is more talk about "prepping for burnout" - starting backfires that would add another 100,000+ acres of burned forest mostly in the remote Dick Smith Wilderness Area to fight fire with fire. Wow, that's a ballsy plan!

At least the action has moved away from us. The highway is open, and so is Cold Springs Tavern. These are good signs. We're hoping to leave for Oregon in a few days. It all depends on the weather...

File.Name: 7811b_7801-019.jpg
Notes: I believe these little metallic-green flies are Long-legged Flies (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera). It's hanging out on a Sweet Corn (Zea mays var. rugosa) leaf in the garden, though I see them on many different plants. See my [Previous] photo for a copper-colored version on the same leaf. At first I thought the color might be a gender difference, but the flies look different up close - eg the copper fly has dark wing spots. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2007)

File.Name: 7811c_7802-023.jpg
Notes: I believe these little copper-colored metallic flies are Long-legged Flies (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera). It's hanging out on a Sweet Corn (Zea mays var. rugosa) leaf in the garden, though I see them on many different plants. See my [Next] photo for a metallic green version on the same leaf. My book says they are preditors of tiny soft-bodied insects, but I never see them go for prey. They just seem to move from leaf to leaf and pose. Hah - I guess some people do that too! (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2007)

File.Name: 7811d_7811-018.jpg
Notes: Furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae of the order Diptera) working a non-native Aster in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 11 August 2007)

File.Name: 7811e_7811-014.jpg
Notes:
Female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a non-native Fuschia flower in the garden. Instead of going up through the throat of the flower, it's working its proboscis "under the skirts" behind the petals to go right to the good stuff. Many insects know this trick for many different flowers.

These are one of our most common butterflies here in the mountains on San Marcos Pass. I started seeing them in mid-June, and here they are in mid-August. Most butterfly books say that their exclusive food plant is the native shrub False Indigo (Amorpha californica) which I haven't found in our canyon - though I have found it on the other side of the highway a mile or two away. To be so common, these butterflies must be utilizing other legumes.
Locality: Amorpha californica) which I haven't found in our canyon - though I have found it on the other side of the highway a mile or two away. To be so common

File.Name: 7811f_7810-218.jpg
Notes:
Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working Phlox flowers in the garden. It was windy, and the butterfly is hanging on by using its forewings as stabilizers. I thought only Anise Swallowtails (Papilio zelicaon) did that in our mountains, but I was wrong. (San Marcos Pass, 10 August 2007)

See my Anise Swallowtail photo and caption at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/23580939

File.Name: 7812a_7812-035.jpg
Notes:
I captured this beautiful metallic wasp in the garden this morning. I believe this is a Cuckoo Wasp aka Jewel Wasp in the Chrysididae family of the Hymenoptera order. Their body surface is very hard and armored with coarse pits and dimples. Many of the larvae are parasitic on other bees and wasps - maybe they don't consume the larvae, but they eat their stored food cache of other paralyzed insects. Wow, nature is beautiful and nasty! (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2007)

The Zaca Wildfire is still burning, but the action is getting further away from home. One consequence is that my usual summer photo places at the river and the high mountains are all closed. Summer insects and flowers are disappearing from our woods in this record drought year. I'm looking closer at the garden, and finding interesting critters that I wouldn't otherwise notice!

The wildfire has now burned 86,000+ acres. The very good news is that the Paradise Road evacuation order was just lifted tonight. This is the community along the Santa Ynez River between the fire and our home. With Cold Springs Tavern, Highway 154, and now Paradise all open, I feel better about heading up to Oregon later this week!

File.Name: 7812b_7812-040.jpg
Notes: Another pretty metallic Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera). I posted photos of two more of these handsome flies yesterday. Click [Previous] a few times to compare. It's remarkable how this fly's head is scooped out between the compound eyes. Its three ocelli - the simple eyes between the big compound eyes - are almost in a straight line rather than the usual triangle. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2007)

File.Name: 7812c_7812-027.jpg
Notes: Little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working summer flowers of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed (Asclepiadaceae) in the garden. It looks like a wasp, but notice the hairs on its hindleg that are designed to be a "pollen basket" to hold collected pollen. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2007)

File.Name: 7812d_7812-042.jpg
Notes: Little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working a morning flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. The plant is a local endemic species that is common in our mountains. If that little insect doesn't look like a bee, look at the legs. You can see it's front leg going down, and its middle leg kicking up. It's hind leg is carrying that yellow mass of pollen visible under the abdomen. These little black bees are probably the most commen visible insects the our mountains (and garden) right now. Large showy insects are really scarce as we move further into this drought summer. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2007)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Sunflower

File.Name: 7813a_7812-064.jpg
Notes:
Dead blue Woodlouse on a board in the garden. These critters are terrestrial crustaceans in the suborder Oniscidea of the order Isopoda of the subphylum Crustacea. (Whew!) They are also known as pill bugs, sow bugs, roly-polys and many more common names.

Usually these common critters are gray - so why is this one colored such a brilliant blue? Probably it was infected with a fatal Iridovirus that changes their color as it kills them. I've also heard stories about internal parasites that change the color and behavior of woodlice so that they seek sunlight and develop bright colors so that birds will eat them. The birds then get the parasite and pass it back to the woodlice through their droppings. Ain't nature strange! (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2007)

All I know for sure is that this dead woodlouse in the garden has a really beautiful color!

File.Name: 7813b_7813-076.jpg
Notes:
Caught in the act!

This doe is nibbling the green apples in our garden. She's been nibbling in the garden all summer. It's cool, she mostly just nibbles the tops of plants - like pinching them off - and the garden comes back stronger. Well, it's not like we can do much! I guess she is a Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus), though our mulies are much smaller than the deer in eastern California and Oregon. (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2007)

We've never had so many apples on our trees - odd in a near-record drought year. Julie propped them up with poles so the limbs wouldn't break. Our worst preditor is usually the Scrub Jays.

File.Name: 7813c_7812-050.jpg
Notes:
This is looking close at the open top of the perfectly named Dead Man's Foot (Pisolithus tinctorius) mushroom in my [Previous] photo. I've also heard it called the "Dog Turd Fungus", "Dyemaker's Puffball", and "Bohemian Truffle" - yes it may be edible when fresh!

The genus name "Pisolithus" literally means "pea stone" - it gets its name from the pea-shaped peridioles which break down to release the spores. These spore-bodies are imbedded in a dark matrix that looks and feels like asphalt. I've read that this is a "mycorrhizal fungus" which means that it gets its nutrition in a symbiotic association with the roots of trees, probably oaks in our mountains. Both partners benefit. Ain't nature grand! (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2007)

File.Name: 7813d_7812-048.jpg
Notes:
I recognized this "thing" right away when I took the photo, but on my computer screen it looks like a rabbit just had a horrible accident!

It's nothing horrible. This is a remarkable summer mushroom pushing up through hard-packed soil along the road that's as hard as stone. Maybe "mushroom" is the wrong word. It's not at all "fleshy" - but it is the large fruiting body of a fungus. The fungus is the perfectly named Dead Man's Foot (Pisolithus tinctorius). The top of the fruiting body is splitting open to reveal the spore-bearing peridioles inside. See my [Next] photo for a closer look. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2007)

The species name "tinctorus" refers to its ancient use as a fabric dye for rich colors ranging from gold to dark blue and black depending on the mordant and soil in which it grows. This is on my very long list of things to try!

File.Name: 7813e_7813-081.jpg
Notes:
Here are two different fire bomber airplanes flying over our house on their way to and from dropping flame retardant on the the Zaca Wildfire. Bomber number 09 on the bottom is much larger than bomber number 55 above. Sandman - any idea what planes these are? (San Marcos Pass, 13 August 2007)

Now there's a new kid in town - a modified DC-10 Airtanker that can carry 12,000 gallons of flame retardant - that's gotta be 100,000+ pounds! It can drop retardant over an area 300 feet wide and a mile long, like an instant fire break. This giant airplane is being used in the backcountry, and home base is in the Mojave Desert near Victorville. I haven't seen it yet, but I reckon I'll hear it coming if it ever makes it our way!

The Zaca Wildfire now stands at 97,000 acres - 152 square miles. The big fire action is moving away from us on San Marcos Pass, but we're under a "Red Flag Alert" for high temperatures and very low humidity. This is really just the beginning of our fire season!

File.Name: 7814a_7814-102.jpg
Notes:
Remarkable firehead cloud looming over our canyon - this is the view from our porch! This is the thunderhead cloud-top of a giant smoke plume from The Zaca Fire which has been burning in the Santa Barbara backcountry since July 4. The wildfire now stands at 105,000 acres - 164 square miles. Containment isn't expected for another month. (San Marcos Pass, 12 August 2007)

These "firehead" clouds are properly called Pyrocumulonimbus clouds. For more info about these fire clouds, see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/23544901

Fortunately the wildfire is not as close as it looks, and it's mostly moving away from us. We've moved our important things to a safe place just in case, and we're heading up to Oregon. I'll be back here with new photos in about ten days.

File.Name: 7826a_7818-070.jpg
Notes:
Jazz artists David Frishberg and Dave Evans playing a tune for Jim Andrews along the river near Portland, Oregon last week. Jim is my brother-in-law who passed away this summer. Jim was a jazz scholar and collector of all media - he shared his knowledge on KBOO 90.7 FM radio in Portland. Thank you Jim. (Oak Grove, Oregon, 18 August 2007)

We got home from our Oregon trip this afternoon. The Zaca Wildfire looks quiet - at least it's far away, and our house is still here! More photos tomorrow.

File.Name: 7827a_7821-095.jpg
Notes: Remarkable Praying Mantis (order Mantodea) looks like it's playing keyboard on the flowers of Queen Anne`s Lace (Daucus carota in the Apiaceae family). Our native mantids at home are smaller and brown. This might be the introduced Mantis religiosa species, though I got this photo deep in the Oregon woods. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 August 2007)
ScientificName: Mantodea; Daucus carota; Apiaceae; Mantis religiosa
associatedTaxa: Mantodea; Daucus carota; Apiaceae; Mantis religiosa

File.Name: 7827b_7824-182.jpg
Notes: Instead of soccor ball eyes, this little Pine White (Neophasia menapia) butterfly should have "X" eyes. You have to look very close in the center-bottom of the photo to see the white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that brought it down. I guess you have to look closer than this butterfly did! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 August 2007)

File.Name: 7827c_7823-121.jpg
Notes:
