Pretty little Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae - probably genus Hesperia) butterfly slipping its proboscis into a pretty flower of Wild SweetPea (Lathyrus latifolius). This is a common non-native weed along the roads in Oregon. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 August 2007)

The curves of the butterfly's proboscis are interesting. Butterflies have no muscles in their tongues. They curl up naturally, and the butterflys extend them with hydrolic pressure. So how does this butterfly get that double "S"-curve to delve into the flower?

File.Name: 7827d_7821-102.jpg
Notes: Very dark Satyr (Satyrinae) butterfly with eye spots, maybe a Common Wood-Nymph (Cercyonis pegala). The wings look like turkey feathers! These butterflies are common in grassy places, but they don't visit flowers and they don't pose for photos. I chased this one for some time before I caught it at rest. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 August 2007)

File.Name: 7827e_7823-135.jpg
Notes: I guess this is a California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly perched on a conifer trunk in the Oregon woods. We have these butterflies at home in California too. It's anything but invisible with wings open like this, but it will disappear against the tree bark with wings closed. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 August 2007)

File.Name: 7827f_7824-172.jpg
Notes: Tattered fritillary butterfly working a late-season Aster (Asteraceae) wildflower high on the mountain divide between Steamboat Creek and Boulder Creek in the North Umpqua River drainage in Oregon. I guess this butterfly is an Atlantis Fritillary (Speyeria atlantis). It's late season even in the high Cascade Mountain meadows. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 August 2007)

File.Name: 7828a_7824-150.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful Indian Paintbrush (genus Castilleja) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) plant family. I know our species at home, but I won't try to guess about this plant growing on the high mountain divide between Steamboat Creek and Boulder Creek in the North Umpqua River drainage in Oregon. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 August 2007)

The red flower heads are striking, but the color mostly comes from leafy bracts. The real flowers are the green tubes sticking out from the bracts.

It's always dry at the end of summer at home in the Santa Barbara mountains. In this drought year it's VERY dry, and the woods are quiet. It was a treat to visit Oregon for two weeks to remember what rain smells like and visit the high Cascade meadows where there are still wildflowers and butterflies and the buzz of insects in the air!

File.Name: 7828b_7823-132.jpg
Notes: This looks like it should be growing in the ocean, but here it is growing on a Wild Rose (genus Rosa - maybe Rosa californica?) shrub in the Oregon woods. I believe this is the gall of the Mossy Rose Gall Wasp (Diplolepis bassetti) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. Galls are a specialized kind of reaction to wounding caused by insects - usually fly midges or tiny wasps who insert their eggs. The insects basically re-program the plant for its own purposes, but the shape of the gall depends on the genetic material of the host plant as well as material introduced by the insect. This is real applied biotech! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 August 2007)
CommonNames: Wild Rose; Mossy Rose Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Rosa; Rosa californica; Diplolepis bassetti; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Rosa; Rosa californica; Diplolepis bassetti; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 7828c_7821-082.jpg
Notes: Pretty "LBM" - "Little Brown Mushroom" - growing in the Oregon woods after a welcome day of rain last week. Look close at the viscid cap - you can see a reflection of the sky and forest trees! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 August 2007)

File.Name: 7828d_7821-079.jpg
Notes:
If I didn't know what this is, I'd wonder what is horribly wrong with some dog!

No - this is the Lobster Mushroom (Hypomyces lactifluorum) or kin. It's a fungus growing on and engulfing another mushroom - probably a Lactarius or Russula. The mushroom is firm, not squishy like I expected. Strange as it looks, this fungus is said to be edible. I wonder if it matters what mushroom is being consumed? (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 August 2007)

File.Name: 7828e_7824-186.jpg
Notes: Beautiful berries catching late afternoon light on the high mountain divide between Steamboat Creek and Boulder Creek in the North Umpqua River drainage in Oregon. I guess this is Mountain-ash (Sorbus scopulina) - or is it Pacific Red Elderberry (Sambucus racemosa)?? Either way I guess they're not edible?? But they sure are nourishing to look at! (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 August 2007)

File.Name: 7828f_7824-148.jpg
Notes:
Handsome Fritillary (genus Speyeria) butterfly working a late-season native Thistle (genus Cirsium in the Asteraceae) wildflower high on the mountain divide between Steamboat Creek and Boulder Creek in the North Umpqua River drainage in Oregon. There are many different fritillaries, so I won't try to guess a species. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 August 2007)

Look at the dots on the eye - like a little soccor ball! I'm sure this is an optical effect, not a pigment. I reckon the spots are a diffraction pattern of light reflecting from the thousands of six-sided ommatidia or "simple eyes" that make up the butterfly's compound eyes like a retina. I've seen these circles of six spots around the central dark spot in other butterflies, for example here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/22693204

All insects with large compound eyes show the pupil-like central spot in photos. I wonder why some butterflies also show the circles of small spots around it?

It's always dry at the end of summer at home in the Santa Barbara mountains. In this drought year it's VERY dry. It was a treat to visit Oregon for two weeks to remember what rain smells like and visit the high Cascade meadows where there are still butterflies!

File.Name: 7829a_7829-072.jpg
Notes:
It seems right to post a photo of a perfect red sunset after yesterday's perfect early morning full eclipse red moon. I was driving home today after our first day of back-to-school planning. I was trying to get to a favorite view place, but the colors were changing so fast I knew I had to stop RIGHT NOW to get the photo. Two minutes later, it was gone. (Santa Ynez Valley, 29 August 2007)

*UPDATE* We started having thunder and lightning and short fits of heavy rain at 11:00 PM or so. The lightning got as close as two seconds between flash and thunder. It's an electric night and it smells wonderful outside!

File.Name: 7829b_7828-056.jpg
Notes:
Red Moon during the full lunar eclipse last night/this morning, as seen from our porch. Conditions were perfect - except for the time. The eclipse was total from 2:52 to 4:22 A.M. with a peak at 3:37. I set my alarm for 3:30, but the moon was hidden behind a tree. I waited on the sofa... and then I woke up an hour later and almost missed it. The sun is starting to come back in the upper left. I used my 500 mm lens on tripod, the exposure is 2 seconds @ f/8 @ ISO 400. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2007)

Now I'm thinking about music. Nick Drake once did a song (and album) titled "Pink Moon". What other songs are there about red moons and eclipse moons?

Maybe it is an omen. Summer is over. I go back to school today. We have no classes until next week, but I have lots to do to get ready.
Locality:
and album) titled "Pink Moon". What other songs are there about red moons and eclipse moons?

Maybe it is an omen. Summer is over. I go back to school today. We have no classes until next week

File.Name: 7830a_7827-009.jpg
Notes:
Golden Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) working new summer flowers of a composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub in the Asteraceae growing on the summer-dry wash by the river. (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2007)

It's so easy to miss unusual beautiful creatures like this beefly, just like it's easy to sleep through a lunar eclipse. I work to get these photos every day. I hope future photgraphers will be able to catch their own photos of the golden flies. We have to work to protect the natural environments that the diverse natural life depends on. We must make an effort to protect our local natural environments. It's too easy to miss it.

File.Name: 7830b_7823-116.jpg
Notes: This pretty little "True Bug" (order Hemiptera) in the Oregon woods has 5-segmented antennae, so I guess it's a "Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae or kin. Most of these bugs don't stink at all - they smell like the herb cilantro! We were staying in my Dad's cabin near Susan Creek, and I didn't see many insects until I looked close. I saw lots of these bugs. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 August 2007)

File.Name: 7830c_7823-117.jpg
Notes: Preditory Robber Fly (family Asilidae in the order Diptera) perched on a leaf in the Oregon woods. These flies catch their prey in the air, trapping them with the stiff hairs on their front legs. They don't usually visit flowers, so it's just luck to get a photo. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 August 2007)

File.Name: 7830d_7821-075.jpg
Notes: Artist's Conk (Ganoderma applanatum or kin) is a woody fungus growing near the ground on a fallen conifer in the Oregon woods. Note the raindrops. What a treat to have a day of rain in Oregon after six months of drought at home in the Santa Barbara mountains. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 August 2007)

File.Name: 7830e_7821-080.jpg
Notes: It's hot and humid at home today after the thunderstorms last night and this morning, so it's a pleasure to look at this cool Snowberry (genus Symphoricarpos) in the Honeysuckle (Caprifoliaceae) family growing in the Oregon woods last week. We have snowberrys - even some berries - at home, but they are summer-dry and covered with ashes from the Zaca wildfire. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 August 2007)

File.Name: 7831a_7827-008.jpg
Notes:
Pretty little Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae butterfly - probably genus Hesperia) - trying to slip its proboscis into new flowers of a native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash along the river. (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2007)

The curve of the butterfly's proboscis reminds me of fly fishing on the North Umpqua River in Oregon! I'm back home on San Marcos Pass in California. It was 102 degrees (Fahrenheit) in the shade when I got home from school today, and the river and creeks are mostly dry. *Sigh*
Locality: Fahrenheit) in the shade when I got home from school today

File.Name: 7831b_7827-006.jpg
Notes: Pretty Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - I guess it is Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti - working flowers of a native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash of the river. I only saw this one. Late-season insects are scarce in this drought year. (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2007)
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: 7831c_7823-124.jpg
Notes: Common Checkered-Skipper (Pyrgus communis) butterfly working flowers of non-native Pennyroyal (Mentha pulegium) in the Mint (Labiatae) family in the Oregon woods. I've been seeing these butterflies at home too. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 August 2007)

File.Name: 7831d_7824-143.jpg
Notes: Cute little Weevil (family Curculionidae in the order Coleoptera) on a leaf in the Oregon woods last week. Most of the insects are in the high Cascade Mountain meadows at this time of year. (North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 August 2007)

File.Name: 7831e_7827-011.jpg
Notes: New flowers of native California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) flower in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family growing on a dry flat along the river. Summer flowers are scarce in the drought year. I've been finding a few of these flowers rather than many. Summer insects are scarce too. (Santa Ynez River, 27 July 2007)

File.Name: 7831f_7827-028.jpg
Notes:
Unusual native Rough-leaved Aster (Aster radulinus) - in the Asteraceae - wildflowers are starting to flower on ground that was cleared for a fire break a few months ago. This is a classic fire-follower plant, in our mountains at least. The "Santa Barbara Flora" by Clifton Smith cites collections from 1974 and 1988. I have photos from 27 August 1997 not far from my photo today. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2007)

These fire-follower plants are remarkable. Seeds can wait for decades until conditions are right to sprout. It's interesting that this area was cleared by machines ("The Masticator") but not burned. I reckon the summer sun on bare soil is enough to trigger these flowers.

Lynn - I found these on East Camino Cielo between the place where you found all the Monardella and Painted Cave Road. Stop at the first (and only) pull-off on the left and go down the hill.

File.Name: 7901a_7901-034.jpg
Notes:
All insects are scarce at the end of summer in this drought year. But just when I think I've seen everything, I find something new!

This is like a cubist painting.

Here is a toothpick-size and shape Walking Stick insect in the order Phasmatodea. It might be the Western Short-horned Walking Stick (Parabacillus hesperus). Look close and you can see the short antennae and long front legs. I found this critter on stems of flowering native California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) flower in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family growing on a dry flat along the river. I took many photos to get a few keepers. It was windy and the insect kept rocking back and forth when I got close. It's hard to get focus and exposure on this skinny creature! (Santa Ynez River, 1 September 2007)
CommonNames: Walking Stick; Western Short-horned Walking Stick; California Aster; Sunflower
ScientificName: Phasmatodea; Parabacillus hesperus; Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Phasmatodea; Parabacillus hesperus; Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: 7901b_7901-037.jpg
Notes: Unusual immature Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) on composite flower heads of Miss Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. There are still new summer flowers starting to bloom in the mountains to keep the insects alive at the end of this very harsh drought summer. (Santa Ynez River, 1 September 2007)
CommonNames: Short-Horned Grasshopper; Miss Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis; Composite

File.Name: 7901c_7901-016.jpg
Notes: Another golden Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) working new summer flowers of composite of a Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub in the Asteraceae growing on the summer-dry wash by the river. I only saw this one. (Santa Ynez River, 1 September 2007)

File.Name: 7902a_7902-047.jpg
Notes: Brilliant little male Wilson's Warbler (Wilsonia pusilla) bird taking a drink in the garden. Look close to see her reflection in the water. We've been keeping the bird bath full and fresh during this very hot weather, and the birds seem to appreciate it. Even the hummingbirds were dancing in the sprinkler this afternoon! I believe these warblers are migratory, just passing by on its way to Central America for winter. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2007)

File.Name: 7902b_7902-049.jpg
Notes: This female Nuttall's Woodpecker (Picoides nuttallii) bird took a notion to visit our apple tree today. I was hoping she would peck an apple for a photo, but she just climbed around and then flew back to the oaks and madrons. Just as well. We have the largest crop of apples ever, but the jays and deer are taking their share. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2007)

File.Name: 7902c_7902-044.jpg
Notes: Butterflies are getting scarce in the woods, but I can still find these pretty California Sisters (Adelpha bredowii) flying through the woods. This pretty butterfly is sitting on her perch high in a California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) tree on the edge of the garden. (I used my 500 mm telephoto lens.) These butterflies are aggresive. They rarely feed. They mostly perch in the trees and chase every other butterfly away. I don't know where they get their energy. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2007)
CommonNames: California Sisters; California Bay; Oregon Myrtle

File.Name: 7902d_7902-054.jpg
Notes: Little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) clutching its prey - an unfortunate furry Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae of the order Diptera. Insects are already scarce at the end of this drought summer, and the spiders are starting to do their part to finish off the survivors as Autumn approaches. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2007)

File.Name: 7902e_7902-043.jpg
Notes:
It sure has been HOT this weekend!

Well, it's not quite this hot. The thermometer on the porch was in the sun this morning, and the shade has just caught up with it at about 11:00 AM. It finally settled down to 105 degrees Fahrenheit. Air temperature must be measured in the shade - but what the heck, I was working in the sun! (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2007)

File.Name: 7902f_7901-027.jpg
Notes:
Here is another photo of the toothpick-like Walking Stick insect that I found on the dry wash along the river yesterday. It might be the Western Short-horned Walking Stick (Parabacillus hesperus), either in the order Phasmatodea or in the Phasmatodea suborder of the order Orthoptera depending which book I consult. (Santa Ynez River, 1 September 2007)

Thread-legged Bugs (subfamily Emesinae in the family Reduviidae of the order Hemiptera) are also very skinny and stick-like, but they have sucking mouthparts. The walkingsticks are hervorous and have tiny chewing mouthparts that I can't quite make out in the photo.
Locality: subfamily Emesinae in the family Reduviidae of the order Hemiptera) are also very skinny and stick-like
CommonNames: Walking Stick; Western Short-horned Walking Stick; Thread-legged Bugs
ScientificName: Parabacillus hesperus; Phasmatodea; Phasmatodea; Orthoptera; Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Parabacillus hesperus; Phasmatodea; Phasmatodea; Orthoptera; Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera

File.Name: 7903a_7903-076.jpg
Notes:
Handsome Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) perched on a non-native Mint (Lamiaceae) leaf in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2007)

*Sigh* I go back to school tomorrow (as teacher) after a wonderful summer taking photos almost every day in our canyon. We have short classes and a potluck dinner tomorrow, then a school camping trip to Monterey for the rest of the week. Can't complain! I`ll be back with new photos next weekend.
Locality: as teacher) after a wonderful summer taking photos almost every day in our canyon.  We have short classes and a potluck dinner tomorrow

File.Name: 7903b_7903-067.jpg
Notes: Little Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae - probably genus Hesperia) - butterfly working non-native Mint (Lamiaceae) flowers in the garden today. These are our most common late-season butterflies, though I keep looking for anything else. (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2007)

File.Name: 7903c_7903-075.jpg
Notes: Late summer Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working the flowers that are left on the native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrubs by our mailbox. Late-season flowers are done early in this drought year, and late-season butterflies are scarce. I saw one more of these Gray Hairstreaks in the garden, but that's it. (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2007)

File.Name: 7903d_7903-065.jpg
Notes:
This tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) could easily perch on top of a pencil with room to spare. It's working non-native Mint (Lamiaceae) flowers in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2007)

Syrphid Flies have been scarce in this drought summer - maybe because their host-insects like aphids have been scarce, because all summer flowers are scarce, because it's a very dry drought year. I have a good photo record from the last few years, so I have a good sense of what's missing. Even so, it's easy to notice when critters appear, but it's hard to notice when they don't show up!

File.Name: 7903e_7902-061.jpg
Notes: Fingernail-size Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched to kill in a native flower head of California Goldenrod aka Oreja de Liebre (Solidago californica) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. This is the only plant I've seen with flowers in the canyon. This is a drought year, and the lare-summer flowers are failing. Of course the insects suffer, and I wonder what next year will be like, wet or dry. (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2007)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; California Goldenrod; Oreja de Liebre; Composite

File.Name: 7903f_7828-063.jpg
Notes: New mature seeds of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed (Asclepiadaceae) in the garden are blowing in the wind headiing for a new home in the sun. I'm getting ready to back to school tomorrow as a teacher. Some of us never learn - we teach! (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2007)

File.Name: 7908a_7907-419.jpg
Notes:
Real living Jellyfish (class Scyphozoa) and real kids at the Monterey Bay Aquarium yesterday. The exhibits and glass are so clear that it's almost like a video background, but this is real! I got this photo before I was reminded that monopods are not allowed. (Monterey, 8 September 2007)

I just returned from our back-to-school camping trip to Monterey, California. This was a perfect way to start the new school year!

File.Name: 7908b_7907-416.jpg
Notes: Beautiful living Jellyfish (class Scyphozoa) at the Monterey Bay Aquarium yesterday. I got this photo before I was reminded that monopods are not allowed. (Monterey, 8 September 2007)

File.Name: 7908c_7907-423.jpg
Notes: Beautiful living Jellyfish (class Scyphozoa) at the Monterey Bay Aquarium yesterday. (Monterey, 8 September 2007)

File.Name: 7908d_7907-453.jpg
Notes: I guess these are American Avocet (Recurvirostra americana) birds - but they are not specimens in a diorama, nor are they wild on a beach. These birds and others are live in the remarkable Monterey Aquarium "Sandy Shore" exhibit. You enter the enclosed space through revolving doors, but there is no barrier between you and the birds and other life. Are the birds clipped so they can't fly? This is maybe my favorite place at the Aquarium. (Monterey, 8 September 2007)

File.Name: 7908e_7907-432.jpg
Notes: This is a natural collage - no Photoshop stuff. I was trying to get a photo of the Sea otters (Enhydra lutris) at the Monterey Aquarium. Shooting through glass with flash, I got this collage of otters and people reflections instead. It's like one of those evolution posters - or is it devolution?
Locality: Enhydra lutris) at the Monterey Aquarium.  Shooting through glass with flash

File.Name: 7908f_7907-465.jpg
Notes: The new "Cannery Row" in Monterey, under construction. I guess it's being remade as a tourist attraction. It now comes up before any John Steinbeck link on a google search. Steinbeck didn't enjoy visiting the new Monterey in his "Travels with Charley" in 1960 - he'd like it even less now. (Monterey, 8 September 2007)

File.Name: 7909a_7906-286.jpg
Notes: Beautiful Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly working flowers of a Strawberry Tree (Arbutus unedo) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family - related to native manzanita and madrone. This small tree is not native to California, but the butterflies don't seem to mind! (Monterey, 6 September 2007)

File.Name: 7909b_7907-458.jpg
Notes:
Walking through the kelp forest exhibit at the Monterey Bay Aquarium. The exhibit is amazing, and it sure is easier on the sinuses to walk rather than dive - but something is missing! (Monterey, 8 September 2007)

I'm concerned that people will mistake the aquariums and zoos for the real thing, and stop caring.

File.Name: 7910a_7908-002.jpg
Notes: Handsome white-faced bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working the Zinnia flowers in the garden. Note the pollen deposits on its legs. This interesting fly seems to have too many antennae - see my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2007)

File.Name: 7910b_7908-003.jpg
Notes: Strange double antennae on a bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working the Zinnia flowers in the garden. At first I thought it might be a fungus infection. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2007)

File.Name: 7910c_7907-450.jpg
Notes: Cute little Sanderling (Calidris alba>/I>) bird. These are the little beach birds that follow the waves in and out. This bird is not running wild on the beach. I got this photo in the remarkable Monterey Aquarium "Sandy Shore" exhibit. You enter the enclosed space through revolving doors, but there is no barrier between you and the birds and other life. Are the birds clipped so they can't fly? Maybe it's the good life - note the mealy worm near its feet. This was maybe my favorite place at the Aquarium. (Monterey, 8 September 2007)

File.Name: 7911a_7901-001.jpg
Notes: Golden Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on a towering seed pod of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. These plants are not native in California, and I always wonder how the pretty aphids find it year after year. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2007)
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 7911b_7812-033.jpg
Notes: Little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working one of the last summer flowers of this native Phacelia cicutaria in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family. Our woods are as dry as I've ever seen in this drought year. This native still has a few flowers growing on the edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2007)

File.Name: 7912a_7909-004.jpg
Notes: Beautiful native summer flower of California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family. What's remarkable is that they are flowering now at the end of our usual very dry summer after one of the driest years on record. Here they are, offering nectar to the hummingbirds when they need it. Even in a drought year, there is always something flowering in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 9 September 2007)

File.Name: 7913a_7901-012.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful golden Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) working new summer flowers of a composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub in the Asteraceae growing on the summer-dry wash by the river. (Santa Ynez River, 1 September 2007)

I saw another one of these remarkable flies at the river today with my after-school Hike Club kids, but I missed the photo. I've been busy.

File.Name: 7913b_7909-006.jpg
Notes: Little Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) munching non-native Mint (Lamiaceae) leaves in the garden. Note the little "training wings" - this grasshopper is not quite mature, but I reckon it will be with its next molt. (San Marcos Pass, 9 September 2007)

File.Name: 7914a_7913-041.jpg
Notes: It's a hardscrabble year for this mama deer at the dry end of the dry summer of one of our driest years ever. She has two fawns who pranced out of view, but she knows where they are. It doesn't help that this is hunting season for the bucks. I guess she is a Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus), though our mulies are much smaller than the deer in eastern California and Oregon. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2007)

File.Name: 7915a_7915-022.jpg
Notes: Nasty-looking Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus - or kin in the family Vespidae , order Hymenoptera) on a leaf of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. These are "paper wasps" that build little nests under the eaves of our roof and porch. They look nasty, but they don't bother me. They fly around the garden hunting for larvae to provision their nest. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2007)
ScientificName: Polistes fuscatus; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Buddleia davidii
associatedTaxa: Polistes fuscatus; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Buddleia davidii

File.Name: 7915b_7915-008.jpg
Notes: Interesting Long-Horned Grasshopper (Tettigoniidae in the Orthoptera) on a Canna Lily leaf in the garden. This hopper must be a kind of Katydid, but it's not green like the common Katydids. The long antennae are very different from our usual Short-Horned Grasshoppers (family Acrididae. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2007)
CommonNames: Long-Horned Grasshopper; Canna Lily; Katydid; Short-Horned Grasshoppers

File.Name: 7915c_7915-002.jpg
Notes: Butts-up view of a large Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa tabaniformis or kin, order Hymenoptera) working a morning flower (after the party) of native Evening Primrose (Oenothera elata ssp. hookeri) in the garden. These large bees drill all those neat nest-holes in the faschia boards all around our roof. Drill away little bees! (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2007)
ScientificName: Xylocopa tabaniformis; Hymenoptera; Oenothera elata; hookeri
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa tabaniformis; Hymenoptera; Oenothera elata; hookeri

File.Name: 7916a_7916-030.jpg
Notes: A pair of needle-thin Damselflies (suborder Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) resting on new male staminate flowers of native composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) shrub in the canyon. This shrub starts to flower late to keep the insects alive, and these skinny preditors know where to shop for prey! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2007)

File.Name: 7916b_7916-026.jpg
Notes:
Watch out - some flowers have thorns!

This thorn is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched to kill in a flower head of the the Chives (Allium schoenoprasum in the garden. Late-season insects are scarce in the canyon in this drought year. I guess I shouldn't be surprised that the late-season spiders that prey on the late-season insects are also scarce. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2007)

File.Name: 7916c_7916-024.jpg
Notes: Tattered Duskywing Skipper - maybe a Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis)? The seasons are changing and all butterflies are scarce at the end of summer in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2007)

File.Name: 7917a_7916-028.jpg
Notes:
I'm not sure if the caption should be "It's a dirty job but some critter has to do it" or "Honeybee Heaven"!

This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) in the woods is dusted with pollen, with full pollen sacks for the hive. The bee is collecting golden pollen from new male staminate pollen-bearing flowers of a native composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) shrub in the canyon. They are just starting to flower at the dry end of the dry summer at the dry end of one of our driest years ever! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2007)

I consider myself a "Friend of the Coyote Bush". These humble shrubs start to flower at the end of our dry summer to keep the insects alive, and they harbor oak tree seedlings for real plant succession. Too many people scrape them away and then wonder why there are no butterflies or new oak trees. D'uh!

File.Name: 7918a_7915-020.jpg
Notes:
Here's the shadow of a paper wasp - Golden Paper Wasp (Polistes fuscatus - or kin in the family Vespidae , order Hymenoptera) - on a leaf of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden with the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2007)

I posted a photo of the same wasp above the leaf two days ago at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/24941655
ScientificName: Polistes fuscatus; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Buddleia davidii
associatedTaxa: Polistes fuscatus; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Buddleia davidii

File.Name: 7919a_7916-032.jpg
Notes: New flowers of native - but weedy - composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family growing along the road in the canyon. I guess it gets the name from the way it follows roads - and maybe telegraph lines, disturbed places - through the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2007)

File.Name: 7919b_7916-033.jpg
Notes:
Small but pretty flowers of Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family growing in the canyon. It's a curious plant. Most of its relaties grow in wet places, but this late-summer species flowers along dry trails and roads in the mountains no matter how dry it has been. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2007)

The tiny flowers on tall stems are hard to photograph with even the slightist breeze.

File.Name: 7920a_7916-051.jpg
Notes:
Dry grass colored Long-Horned Grasshopper (Tettigoniidae in the Orthoptera order) on a Canna Lily (family Cannaceae) flower in the garden. It must be some kind of Katydid, but it's not green like the common Katydids. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2007)

The mouthparts of these insects are remarkable - the segmented maxillary and labial palps are like little hands that taste and feed food to its mouth. They remind me of Bender's little mini-arms when he played washboard with Beck! (Futurama episode 3ACV13 of course.)

This katydid looks happy munching the flower. I'm pretty happy too! We're flying to Portland, Oregon tomorrow morning. My son Ewan and dear Roz are getting married on Sunday, September 23! That's also the autumnal equinox and the first day of fall, so it's an auspicious date for a ceremony. I'll be back Tuesday. Bless us all!
Locality:
Futurama episode 3ACV13 of course.)

This katydid looks happy munching the flower. I'm pretty happy too! We're flying to Portland

File.Name: 7924a_7923-038.jpg
Notes:
This isn't my usual "Treebeard Photo" - but this wasn't my usual weekend either!

We flew to Portland, Oregon to celebrate the wedding of my son Ewan and bride Rosamund. This is our Kummel family photo. You can probably guess who Treebeard is, and entwife Julie is kitty-corner. Our son Ry, Auntie Laurie, my father Bob, and the bride and groom - Ewan and Roz - are all themselves. It was a wonderful weekend! (Portland, Oregon, 23 September 2007)

File.Name: 7925a_7923-056.jpg
Notes:
Bride and groom - Roz and Ewan - at their wedding in Portland, Oregon last weekend. I'm so happy for you both! (Portland, Oregon, 23 September 2007)

Roz is wearing my Mom's pearl earings and a broach you can't see in this photo. That really brings tears to my eyes. Life goes on...

File.Name: 7925b_7923-150.jpg
Notes:
My son Ewan playing Auntie Laurie's fine Gibson guitar after the wedding on Sunday. Ewan wanted a classic Duke Ellington look, and he sure got it with this natty antique pinstripe suit and that perfect hat! (Portland, Oregon, 23 September 2007)

I removed some color from this photo, but I left color in the poster at the lower left. The words say "Raw" "Wild" "Truth". It's a stumper - can you guess who this is? He was a mentor for me and my kids!

File.Name: 7926a_7915-017.jpg
Notes: Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a late-season non-native Aster flower in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family growing in the garden. I love the sun on the bee's wings, like reflections on still water. The seasons are turning after the equinox last weekend. Insects and flowers are getting scarce. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2007)

File.Name: 7926b_7920-126.jpg
Notes:
It came from the sky!

I was at school last Thursday waiting for our parent "Back-to-School" night - and thinking about packing for our wedding trip to Oregon - when I noticed this dark smoke-like "thing" falling from the sky. I watched it come down and jumped in my truck to go find it. I was hoping for something alien or maybe a fallen soviet satellite!

It turned out to be a huge piece of plastic bird netting. It stretched across a farm field and over the power lines on Roblar Road and on into the distance - longer than a football field. I guess a BIG dust devil twister sucked it up from some vintner's grape field and carried it away, like Dorothy left Kansas. (Santa Ynez Valley, 20 September 2007)

File.Name: 7927a_7927-220.jpg
Notes:
Looking close at a Sea Anemone (order Actiniaria in phylum Cnidaria) in a tide pool today during the very low minus tide that comes with the Harvest Moon. I guess we'll all see something different in this photo. It sure looks full of life! (Gaviota Coast, 27 September 2007)

I took my after-school hike club kids to a secret beach today between Refugio and Gaviota during a very low minus tide. Lots of fun and lots to see!

File.Name: 7927b_7927-181.jpg
Notes: Seagulls taking wing at the beach today after school. It was contrasty light shooting into the sun at the beach, so I made it more so! (Gaviota Coast, 27 September 2007)

File.Name: 7928a_7927-228.jpg
Notes: Pretty dandelion-like composite flowers of Cliff Aster (Malacothrix saxatilis var. saxatilis) growing on the bluffs at a secret beach between Refugio and Gaviota. This succulent species is endemic along the beach, but other non-succulent varieties are flowering in the mountains near home. It's a regular late-season plant offering flowers to the insects, even in this very dry drought year. (Gaviota Coast, 27 September 2007)

File.Name: 7928b_7927-206.jpg
Notes: Sea Anemone (order Actiniaria in phylum Cnidaria) in a tide pool at a secret beach yesterday during the very low minus tide that comes with the Harvest Moon. It looks like a burger with tentacles! (Gaviota Coast, 27 September 2007)

File.Name: 7928c_7927-223.jpg
Notes:
Wiping the slate clean...

The layered rock at this beach is Monterey Shale instead of slate, but the arm of this starfish (class Asteroidea of the phylum Echinodermata) really is wiping the shale clean yesterday afternoon during the very low minus tide that comes with the autumn Harvest Moon. (Gaviota Coast, 27 September 2007)

File.Name: 7928d_7927-188.jpg
Notes:
Trains and cars speed past the empty beach at Arroyo Hondo between Refugio and Gaviota. It's interesting to look back at the road. In the forground is a steel railroad trestle still in use. Then an abandoned concete arch bridge from the original two-lane Highway 101. The straight hill in the background is the new Highway 101 "bridge" - a dirt-filled embankment that fills the canyon over a culvert for what's left of the creek. (Gaviota Coast, 27 September 2007)

There's a sign on the pillar of the trestle - that white rectangle - that warns that fishing is illegal here to protect the endangered Southern Steelhead Trout. Seems silly now at the end of a drought summer when there is no creek and no anadromous fish. I wonder if it made a difference for the steelhead replacing the creek with a buried culvert?

File.Name: 7929a_7929-040.jpg
Notes: Pretty late-season Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae - probably genus Hesperia) - butterfly working a flower of Common Aster (Aster chilensis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family growing in the garden today. They are the last of the summer butterflies as the season turns to autumn. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2007)

File.Name: 7929b_7929-023.jpg
Notes: Little Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) hiding among the flowers of a native composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis (family Asteraceae) growiiing in the school garden. (Santa Ynez Valley, 29 September 2007)
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Coyote Bush

File.Name: 7929c_7929-035.jpg
Notes:
Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) dusted with golden pollen working new male staminate pollen-bearing flowers of a native composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis). This is a late-season shrub that flowers at the end of our dry summer, when the insects need it most. (Santa Ynez Valley, 29 September 2007)

I'm involved with the Santa Ynez Natural History Society. We had our annual Plant Sale at my school today. Busy day, but I took a break in Julie's garden for a few photos.
   http://www.syvnature.org/

File.Name: 7930a_7930-052.jpg
Notes:
With that amazing eye-pattern and rabbit ear antennae, I call this a TV fly!

I believe this is a Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens - family Stratiomyidae in the insect order Diptera) trying to warm up on a grape leaf in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2007)

File.Name: 7930b_7929-030.jpg
Notes: Tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working the male staminate pollen-bearing flowers of a native composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the school garden. This pretty fly could easily perch on the eraser of your pencil! (Santa Ynez Valley, 29 September 2007)

File.Name: 7930c_7930-055.jpg
Notes:
New flower buds of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. Even in this drought year, they know when it's time to flower after the equinox. It feels like a miracle every year when the first new flowers appear. How do they know? (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2007)

Last year I captured my first Ribes malvaceum flowers on October 28, so these flowers are early.
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/16751258

File.Name: 7a01a_7929-044.jpg
Notes:
Nasty looking coin-sized Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a stem of an almost-ready-to-flower Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2007)

This is the dry end of a drought year in my corner of the hemisphere. It's easier to notice new critters than the critters that are missing - but I've noticed that all spiders are scarce this year. I haven't seen any of the large Orb-Weaver Spiders (family Araneidae) that are usually trapping the late-season insects at this timed of year. That makes sense - I don't see many insects either. I hope we get rain this winter, though the forecast is not good.
Locality: family <i>Araneidae<i>) that are usually trapping the late-season insects at this timed of year.  That makes sense - I don't see many insects either.  I hope we get rain this winter
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Chaparral Currant; Orb-Weaver Spiders

File.Name: 7a01b_7929-051.jpg
Notes: Another photo of the interesting Window Fly aka Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens - family Stratiomyidae in the insect order Diptera) that I found in the garden yesterday. It's about 1/2 inch long - ,ore than a centimeter. The fly is perched in the center of a grape leaf in the garden - a natural solar collector - so it can warm up in the chilly autumn morning. See my [Previous] photo for a closer shot of the fly's amazing eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2007)

File.Name: 7a02a_7930-057.jpg
Notes: I guess this is the same coin-sized Crab Spider (Thomisidae) as my [Previous] photo, but it's moved over to the Canna Lily (family Cannaceae) flowers in the garden nearby. These crab spiders usually hide in flowers to trap their prey, but this spider prefers stems. Maybe that huge abdomen is full of eggs ready to deposit? (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2007)

File.Name: 7a03a_7a03-083.jpg
Notes: Julie's garden kids found this bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) in the school garden today. I'm sure the spider is genus Argiope - probably a Banded Argiope (Argiope trifasciata). These large garden spiders have been scarce in this drought year at the end of our dry summer, so I was happy to get this photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 October 2007)
ScientificName: Araneidae) in the school garden today.  I'm sure the spider is genus Argiope; Argiope trifasciata
associatedTaxa: Araneidae) in the school garden today.  I'm sure the spider is genus Argiope; Argiope trifasciata

File.Name: 7a04a_7a03-086.jpg
Notes:
The underside of the same bottlecap-size Banded Argiope (Argiope trifasciata) Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) that we found in the school garden yesterday. See my [Previous] photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 October 2007)

These spiders look scary, but they don't jump to attack you. They hang upside-down on their webs patiently waiting for prey. The different species usully have different scary "faces" on their exposed underside. It works for me!

File.Name: 7a05a_7a04-248.jpg
Notes: Pretty late-season flowers of native Stephanomeria virgata in the ccomposite Sunflower (Asteraceae) family, with the sun behind. The plants look weedy and tend to grow along the mountain roads, but the flowers are beautiful up close. This humble plant seems to lack a common name. I've seen it called "Rod Wirelettuce" and "Twiggy Wreath Plant" - it deserves better. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a05b_7a04-237.jpg
Notes: Interesting native Rough-leaved Aster (Aster radulinus) - in the Asteraceae family - wildflowers are still flowering on dry ground that was cleared for a fire break at the beginning of summer. This is a classic fire-follower plant that's growing in an area that was cleared by machines ("The Masticator") but not burned. I reckon the hot summer sun on bare soil is enough to trigger these flowers that usually appear after wildfires. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a06a_7a06-004.jpg
Notes:
Fly in motion - this is a sharp photo!

Here is a large Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae, order Diptera) on a leaf in the garden this morning. The leaf and the fly's feet are sharp, but I captured the photo just as the fly was revving up to fly away. I've read that flies fly more like helicopters than birds and butterflies that flap their wings - and this photo shows it! The whole body and wings of this fly are twisting counter-clockwise (left) on the verge of leaping into the air. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2007)

The seasons really are turning. It was cold this morning - down to 38 degrees Fahrenheit last night. We fired up the woodstove for the first time in six months. This is our in-between time - insects are scarce, and there are no mushrooms yet.

File.Name: 7a06b_7a06-014.jpg
Notes:
Did you notice the Crab Spider (Thomisidae) hiding in this new flower cluster of native composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis (family Asteraceae) in the canyon this morning? (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2007)

Of course I noticed the spider when I took the photo, but I didn't realize it is butts-up pointing into the flower rather than waiting for prey. Maybe it has already captured a breakfast snack? Autumn flowers are scarce after our long, dry summer, so these humble late-season composite shrubs are important to the life of the woods.

File.Name: 7a06c_7a06-008.jpg
Notes:
Here are the first new flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. I reckon these are the the first flowers of the new year - the new "rain year" at least. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2007)

We have a summer-dry and winter-wet Mediterranean climate here in central California. I'm still finding late-season flowers at this dry end of a drought year, but these pretty flowers mark the beginning of a new year. I hope we get real rain this winter, though the forecast is not good.

File.Name: 7a07a_7a07-051.jpg
Notes: Acrobatic Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) munching a petal of native Common Aster (Aster chilensis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in the garden today. Nice legs! Other insects have been early in our drought summer, but the grasshoppers are late and still immature. This large hopper (compare the very different hopper in my [Previous] photo) still has its training wings. It has one more molt to go before it becomes an adult ready and able to mate. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a07b_7a07-046.jpg
Notes: Little Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) looking up at me in the garden today. Other insects have been early in this drought summer, but the grasshoppers are late. This little hopper still has its training wings. It has one more molt to go before it becomes an adult ready and able to mate. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a07c_7a07-056.jpg
Notes: Honeybee-size (and color) Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a flower of native Common Aster (Aster chilensis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in the garden today. This fly can inflate its face like a balloon to produce its dark proboscis to work the flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2007)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Common Aster; Sunflower

File.Name: 7a08a_7a07-055.jpg
Notes:
Another shot of this Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) munching a petal of native Common Aster (Aster chilensis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in the garden yesterday. Life is good! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2007)

Other insects have been early in our drought summer, but the grasshoppers are late and still immature. This large hopper still has its training wings. It has one more molt to go before it becomes an adult ready and able to mate. See my [Previous] photo.

File.Name: 7a09a_7a07-065.jpg
Notes: These moth-like Skippers are the last butterflies on the wing as we move into winter in the Santa Barbara mountains. This Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae - probably genus Hesperia) butterfly is working a late-season native flower of composite Bush Groundsel (Senecio douglasii) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family near home. These late-season flowers keep the late-season insects going for next year. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a09b_7a06-017.jpg
Notes: The Skippers are our last butterflies as we move into winter. This Grass-Skipper (subfamily Hesperiinae - probably genus Hesperia) butterfly is working a late-season flower of native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family growing along the road in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a10a_7a06-037.jpg
Notes:
I believe this is a tiny Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae or kin in the order Hymenoptera) visiting the male staminate pollen-bearing flowers of a native composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family growing in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2007)

These wasps locate hidden spider and insect prey and use their long ovipositor to lay eggs on or inside them. The parasitoid larvae will emerge from their host after it has pupated. Think about the movie Alien! Look close and you can see that this wasp has a two-part ovipoitor with a sheath on the left and the delivery tube more to the right. I wonder if it is really looking for host-prey or just munching on the flowers and "keeping busy" - one of those sex and food things.

This is as close as I can get. I had my 105 mm macro lens high on my tripod near 1:1 magnification, and cropped it tight. There was a slight breeze, and this is as sharp as I could get.

File.Name: 7a11a_7a07-068.jpg
Notes: Plump new acorn of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia - Fagaceae) in the woods today. But note the little acorn at the bottom that never developed - "Many are called but few are chosen." Most Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) acorns have failed this year, so these fat acorns are good news for all the critters that depend on them at the end of a drought year when life is hard. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a11b_7a11-129.jpg
Notes: Late-season flowers of the native composite shrub Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa var. Grindelioides) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. This is the more congested variety growing in the rocks near the "Lizard Mouth" a few miles from home. The woodland plants are also flowering now. It's interesting that so many of our late-season flowering plants are yellow composite shrubs. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a12a_7a12-161.jpg
Notes:
It's raining! These first winter rains always feel like a literal gift from above after six months of summer drought in our summer-dry mountains.

I noticed this female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) perched in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near the porch just as the rain was beginning this evening. I wonder what she thinks of this new rain? (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a12b_7a12-138.jpg
Notes: Forked-horn buck Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus) watching and listening in the woods just beyond the apple trees in the garden this morning. A few minutes before, he was standing on his hind legs like a goat munching the apples and leaves. I go to school late on Friday mornings, so I probably interrupted his usual morning schedule. There's still enough apples for us all. Deer hunting season ended a week or two ago. This two-point legal buck made it. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a13a_7a13-016.jpg
Notes: Mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) guarding her egg sac in a native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family flowering along the road in the canyon. She will perch here for weeks waiting for her spiderlings to emerge. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2007)
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Telegraph Weed; Sunflower
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae

File.Name: 7a13b_7a13-009.jpg
Notes:
There are new seedlings in our summer-dry woods! I missed the first rain of the year when I flew to Oregon for my son's wedding a few weeks ago. We had another 1/4 inch of rain last night, and it's been overcast all day. There's even some drizzle now as I type. It's New Year in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2007)

I guess these are sprouts of non-native Chickweed (Stellaria media). The natives usually wait for more rain before sprouting, but I also saw native Fiesta Flower (Pholistoma auritum) seedlings today.

We started last winter with an El Nino condition and a forecast for a wet year. That was wrong. We're starting this winter with a La Nina condition and a forecast for a dry year. I hope that's wrong too!
Locality: Stellaria media). The natives usually wait for more rain before sprouting

File.Name: 7a13c_7a13-004.jpg
Notes: New flowers and buds of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. They have the sheen of raindrops from our little storm last night. I reckon these flowers mark the start of the new rain year for the woods. What a pleasure after six months of summer drought! (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a14a_7a14-024.jpg
Notes:
Just another fly...

Elliott Smith sang about being "Just another guy..." You know how it feels. My photo shows just another Fly (order Diptera) working female pistillate flowers of late-season native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis - order Asteraceae) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2007)

Just another fly... I don't know what this little fly-like fly is or how it lives. It's working the flowers rather than our garbage or compost. It's something else...

File.Name: 7a14b_7a14-023.jpg
Notes:
Pretty dry-grass-colored Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) in the autumn leaves. I would never have spotted it if I didn't almost step on it and notice where it jumped! This little hopper is immature with training wings. It's still one molt away from being mature and able to mate. (I'll bet it's ready!) (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2007)

I recognize those red and blue legs - see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/25752346

File.Name: 7a14c_7a13-018.jpg
Notes:
I got too close with my camera and spooked this Mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) - she clutched her egg sac even tighter, and I wasn't about to argue! See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2007)

This impressive spider made her nest in a native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family flowering along the road in the canyon. She will perch here for weeks waiting for her spiderlings to emerge.
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Telegraph Weed; Sunflower
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae

File.Name: 7a15a_7a13-022.jpg
Notes: We had 1/4 inch of early rain last weekend. Here are raindrops hanging from the stamens and style of the Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis) flowers in the garden. You can see the woods upside down in the raindrops! Look how the female style of the flower is touching the male stamen just to be sure that the flower is consummated to produce seed. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a15b_7a06-025.jpg
Notes: Like dancers showing their stuff! There are still late-season flowers of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) flowering in the canyon. It's a native shrub in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family that started flowering months ago. There are few insects left in the woods now to visit these remarkable flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a16a_7a07-059.jpg
Notes:
Balloons or bazooms?

These are autumn berries of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis) in the woods. Maybe they are not toxic, but the berries are bland at best. Even the birds ignore them. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a16b_7a06-020.jpg
Notes: Beautiful autumn nuts of spring-flowering native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis var. redivivus aka S. redivivus) in the Storax (Styracaceae) family in the canyon. These interesting native shrubs are common in the canyon, but they are scarce in California. I don't know what critters eat the nuts, but they are disappearing fast. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2007)
ScientificName: Styrax officinalis; redivivus; S. redivivus; Styracaceae
associatedTaxa: Styrax officinalis; redivivus; S. redivivus; Styracaceae

File.Name: 7a17a_7a06-001.jpg
Notes: New composite flowers of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae family with shadows on the gray underside of a leaf. These probably aren't flowers you would pick for a bouquet, but all our late-season flowers are important in the mountains to keep the pollinators alive for next year. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a18a_7a07-058.jpg
Notes:
Eye to eyes with a Honeybee-size (and color) Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a flower of native Common Aster (Aster chilensis) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in the garden. Did you ever guess that flies have hairy eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2007)

I have another photo of the same fly at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/25751756

File.Name: 7a18b_7a14-027.jpg
Notes:
Eye to eyes with "just another fly" working female pistillate flowers of late-season native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis - order Asteraceae) in the canyon. I guess this is a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae, order Diptera). That 6-sided gleam in its eyes is an insect thing, not a camera thing like the polygons that show up when you when you shoot into the sun. It has to do with the 6-sided "ommatidia" simple-eyes that make up the large compound eyes of these insects. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2007)

I have another photo of the same fly at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/26014628

File.Name: 7a19a_7a07-074.jpg
Notes: Golden Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on the Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. We cultivate the aphids as well as the flowers because they are so pretty! These plants are not native in California, and I always wonder how the aphids find it year after year. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2007)
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 7a20a_7a19-134.jpg
Notes:
Looking up at a female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a Zinnia flower in the garden. I didn't have time to change tripods when I spotted this butterfly close up. It's past the end of summer, and this is the last butterfly I've seen so far - maybe it's the last butterfly of the year in the canyon.

There are insects and butterflies that over-winter, but it's remarkable that many native insects will die and leave only their eggs to come again next year, God willing. It seems like a big gamble for insects - whole species could dissapear in one bad season!

I'm glad I got these photos yesterday morning. It was too windy for photos today, and the forecast is for more wind tomorrow. The sky is full of dust - an ominous combination of dry fields in a drought year and maybe ash from the summer Zaca wildfire.
Locality: Colias eurydice) butterfly working a Zinnia flower in the garden. I didn't have time to change tripods when I spotted this butterfly close up. It's past the end of summer

File.Name: 7a20b_7a19-127.jpg
Notes: I found this little brown Cricket (family Gryllidae in the order Orthoptera) snuggled into a Zinnia flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a20c_7a19-120.jpg
Notes: Handsome dry-grass-colored Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae in the order Orthoptera) munching leaves of native Horseweed (Conyza canadensis - a weedy-looking composite in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family that's still flowering after most native flowers are done. (San Marcos Pass, 19 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a21a_7a21-036.jpg
Notes:
Little Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) resting on my finger. It's something the cat brought in. I rescued the lizard and took it outside for a photo. A moment later it jumped away, so I reckon it's OK! (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2007)

Today is Elliott Smith day, in honor of his death on October 21, 2003. It's a perfect day to think about what's good and a reminder not to f*ck it up. Your biggest mistake was thinking you couldn't relate. Thank you Elliott, I miss you. It still hurts after four years.
   http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fd-zwe1fWB0
   http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=37k_Ri1XxEc

If you don't know Elliott Smith's music, check the Live Music Archive starting here:
   http://www.archive.org/details/esmith1998-06-02.flac

File.Name: 7a21b_7a20-003.jpg
Notes: I found another little lobster-like brown Cricket (family Gryllidae in the order Orthoptera) snuggled into the faded end of a leaf of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) by the creek. It's good camouflage, but not perfect - I spotted it! (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a22a_7a21-021.jpg
Notes:
Little Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working late-season flowers of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. Dry as it is this year - despite two little autumn rains - there are still flowers and insects in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2007)

There's a new wildfire in the mountains at the Sedgwick Reserve a few miles from school. I'm snuffling from breathing the smoke all day. It's a bad fire day in all Southern California south of us. There's no way to stop these huge wildfires driven by Santa Ana (or is it Santana?) winds gusting over 100 mph.
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Sunflower

File.Name: 7a22b_7a21-016.jpg
Notes: Fumbling Bumble Bee (family Bombus in the order Hymenoptera) moving between late-season flower heads of a composite shrub in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family in the mountains. There's a colony of these shrubs where West Camino Cielo turns off from Highway 154. I don't recognize these plants. The flowers are discoid without ray-flowers. Maybe they were introduced during highway work last year? I guess they are a subspecies of Goldenbush (Ericameria cuneata?) I'll find out when I have some time to hit the books and post an update. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a23a_7a21-004.jpg
Notes:
Little long-legged Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched in a late-season Bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) Spiders are scarce this year because all insects are scarce in this drought year. I'm looking closer this year and finding more. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2007)

It's an interesting week with hot dry Santa Ana winds and fire and smoke in the air. The big fires are all south of us, but I've been taking my USB harddrive with me to school every day just in case!

I'm listening to The Doors, L.A. Woman:
   Well, I just got into town about an hour ago
   Took a look around, see which way the wind blows...

   I see your hair is burnin'
   Hills are filled with fire
   If they say I never loved you
   You know they are a liar...

File.Name: 7a23b_7a13-011.jpg
Notes:
Mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) guarding her egg sac in a native Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family flowering along the road in the canyon. Note the rain drops on her web! She will perch here for weeks waiting for her spiderlings to emerge. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2007)

I got this photo a week ago after an early rain - just 1/4 inch. Everything is different this week with hot dry Santa Ana winds and fire and smoke in the air. The big fires are all south of us, but I've been taking my USB harddrive with me to school every day just in case!
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Telegraph Weed; Sunflower
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Heterotheca grandiflora; Asteraceae

File.Name: 7a24a_7a21-031.jpg
Notes: Even at the dry end of a drought year - with fire weather and smoke in the air - here are new flower buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) in the Sumac Anacardiaceae) family full of promise for new life next spring. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2007)

File.Name: 7a25a_7a07-062.jpg
Notes:
Late-summer flowers of the native composite shrub Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. This is the species growing in our woodland canyon. A more compact variety grows on sandstone outcrops in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2007) We have many late-season composite plants with yellow flowers that are hard to key out, but they are easy to recognize once you learn them. These Hazardia flowers only have disk flowers, with distinctive overlapping recurved phyllaries below the flowers like little cones. It's easier to recognize them than to say in words what makes them unique!

It still feels like fire weather. I'm waiting for real rain. I like Rich Seibert's idea of rain dances on the beaches!

File.Name: 7a25b_7a21-005.jpg
Notes: Late-summer flowers of California Goldenrod aka Oreja de Liebre (Solidago californica) in the Composite (Asteraceae) family. These natives are flowering in the woods just beyond our garden, the first I've seen so close to home. The yellow flowers usually appear in these flat lateral sprays. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2007)
CommonNames: California Goldenrod; Oreja de Liebre; Composite

File.Name: 7a26a_7a21-012.jpg
Notes:
Looking close at a late-season composite flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. This shows the very female Y-shaped stigmas with the clasping pollen-bearing male anthers below. Flowers are scarce at the end of our dry summer season in California as we wait and dance for winter rain to settle the ashes from the wildfires. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2007)

My school stumper this week was about the Devil Winds that pumped up the Southern California fires:

Halloween is fun. Chaparral wildfires driven by Santa Ana winds are scary! We've been lucky, so far. The local Sedgwick Fire was contained because the winds didn't happen, though this smoky fire weather is scary enough. Why didn't we get the dangerous Santa Ana (or Santana?) winds? We do have local Sundowner winds, so what's the difference? Heat rises, but all of these devil winds get hotter and drier as they blow down the canyons. How does this work? The real stumper is how should we live in a natural landscape that is adapted to burn?
Locality: or Santana?) winds? We do have local Sundowner winds
CommonNames: Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Sunflower

File.Name: 7b01a_7b01-250.jpg
Notes:
Our mountains are haunted by these ancient Native American Chumash rock art images that I find here and there in secluded places like this. I don't know what they mean, but they speak to me, like a whisper from the past I don't quite understand. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2007)

Last night was Halloween, when kids dress up in costumes and try to scare each other and eat too much candy - that really is scary! The name "Halloween" is short for All-Hallow-Evening, as it is the night before "All Hallows' Day" - which is now known as All Saints' Day.

There are ancient spirits at work in this holiday! Halloween is also one of the cross-quarter days, midway between the autumnal equinox and the winter solstice. It's interesting that the next cross-quarter day is Groundhog Day on February 2, midway between the winter solstice and the vernal equinox. The night of the living dead and the day when the groundhog comes out the the ground - both holidays seem so strange and silly, but there is a real connection about crossing between realms!

If you read the last Harry Potter book, you know that hallows are sacred things worthy of veneration. Today seemed the right day to take my after-school hike club kids to this ancient hallowed place in the mountains near home.

File.Name: 7b01b_7b01-245.jpg
Notes:
Part of a bedrock mortar where native American Chumash people once ground acorns under a huge Live Oak tree that still stands. These deep bowls ground into the sandstone bedrock are 1-2 feet across. It's remarkable how the holes merge together from so much use. This is magic country. It's remarkable to sit under the tree and think about the Chumash kids who sat under the same tree and dreamed about their future. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2007)

I guess it's a Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae , order Hymenoptera trying to warm up in the morning sun. It's resting on a weedy plant that I think is Hairy Horseweed (Conyza bonariensis in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. These are the wasps that build the small open-faced paper combs that hang by a stalk under the eaves of our porch. (I've mistaken them for Polistes fuscatus.) They don't bother me, and they are good hunters for caterpillars and other soft insects in the garden. This pose with coiled antennae is perfect! (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2007)

File.Name: 7b04a_7b03-039.jpg
Notes:
There's something NEW in the sky!

This is Comet Holmes in the evening sky last night. It's the bright new "head" of the Perseus constellation in the northeast evening sky along the Milky Way below Cassiopia. This remarkable comet has mysteriously brightened almost a million-fold over the last week. It looks to be heading right at us with its tail stretched out behind, but you can see a slight slew to the right if you look close. It looks like a varmit's last view of a bullet! (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2007)

I got this photo last night with my Nikon D70 and Tamron 200-500 telephoto lens cranked out to the max, and then cropped tight. The exposure is 25 seconds @ f/6.3 @ ISO 1600. The streaks show the motion of the stars during this long exposure. Sure it's a macro shot - of something far away!

Get outside soon to see this comet if you can. There's more info and sky charts at:
   http://www.skyandtelescope.com/observing/home/10862521.html

"I heard the song

It'll happen to you
Also without fail,
If it happens to me
Sang the world's last whale."

We should worry for these long-lived leviathons - they are the BIGGEST canaries in our coal mine! But we expect the pretty butterflies of summer to disappear as the seasons shift to winter. I haven't seen any butterflies except a few skippers for several weeks as we slide past the equinox hump into winter in the mountains.

But yesterday was a beautiful warm "indian summer" autumn day, and I spotted four different butterflies in four different species - just one of each. I managed to capture this surprising Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a late-season Zinnia flower in the garden. Look how it's catching some color from the flowers! It looks tattered, I wonder where it's been? (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2007)

I also captured a California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly on the same flowers - see my [Previous] photo . I also captured a California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly on the same flowers - see my [Previous] photo. And I saw a single California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) and a single Checkered White (Pontia protodice) fly through the garden, though they didn't feel like posing.

Life goes on.
Locality: Pontia protodice) fly through the garden

The mushrooms are named for their color rather than their taste - though they are tasty if fresh. These mushroom caps are already sun-cracked from the low humidity in this seemingly-endless drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2007)

I always find these mushrooms about this time of year, rain or shine. How do simple mushrooms know how long the days are to recognize their season to fruit?

Which end is which?

----

Friday update - I found my book at school today, and I'm pretty sure this is a Navanax - Navanax inermis aka Chelidonura inermis. It's a predatory sea slug, a marine opisthobranch gastropod mollusk in the family Aglajidae the order Cephalaspidea - one of the headshield slugs and bubble snails.

It's NOT a nudibranch, though it looks like one. It belongs to the more ancient group of opisthobranchs. It's an active preditor that follows the mucus trails of its bubble snail and nudibranch prey.

I believe its head is to the right in my photo.

Sunburned autumn berries of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis) in a place that was "masticated" for a fire break last summer by an enormous TimbCo T425 "Hydro-shredder" machine - aka "The Masticator". These plants seem to be stump-sprouting, but are less than a foot (30 cm) tall with narrow leaves. They look very different from the plants in the woods at home that are several feet tall and have pure white berries. But here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2007)

Maybe the berries are not toxic, but they are bland at best. Even the birds ignore them.

These mushrooms are Honey Mushroom (Armillariella mellea or kin) in the garden. They are named for the color, not the taste. They are edible if you get them before the flies do, but they have a way of turning to mush overnight as fly maggots consume them. The white flecks on the caps used to connect with the white collars on the stem to make a partial veil to protect the gills before opening. Check my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2007)

This pretty wasp gall is the larval home of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella). The solitary wasp larva living inside will soon be ready to emerge as an adult ready to mate and deposit new eggs in the oaks to keep the cycle going. The galls are really a kind of tumor on the oaks that is genetically engineered by the wasps to nourish their young. It's a bio-tech war in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2007)
