Notes:
Little Western Spotted Cucumber Beetle (Diabrotica undecimpunctata, order Coleoptera) perched on a white daisy flower petal in the school garden. What a beautiful world! (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 November 2007)

I found this little critter in the school garden today. I've had five full days of student conferences ths week, but I had a break today to get a few photos. Conferences are over, and I'm ready for a vacation week!

File.Name: 7b16b_7b16-045.jpg
Notes:
I thought this was just another fly in the garden. With the photo up on the screen, I can see it's something different. Note the long face with Y-shaped antennae low on the snout. Now I'm not even sure if it's a Fly (order Diptera) - maybe it's a Scorpionfly (order Mecoptera)? (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 November 2007)

Update: I think Marcoptero (http://www.fotolog.com/marcoptero) got this right. This is a March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera). Compare the photos at http://bugguide.net/node/view/3834

File.Name: 7b16c_7b16-053.jpg
Notes:
Make a circle with your thumb and forefinger - this garden spider is about that big!

Look close at the patterns on the spider's abdomen - do you see faces - maybe lots of faces? These garden spiders hang on their webs in the open all day long, and many of them seem to have "scary faces" to frighten preditors.

I guess this is a Banded Argiope (Argiope trifasciata) Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae). I found this spider perched on her web in the school garden today. I've had full days of student conferences all week, but I had a break today to get a few photos. Conferences are over, and I'm ready for a vacation week! (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 November 2007)

File.Name: 7b17a_7b17-030.jpg
Notes:
I didn't expect to find any butterflies on this fine autumn day, but here is a perfect Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly working the Mexican Sage (Salvia sp.) flowers in the garden today. This admiral is really a lady (genus Vanessa)! (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2007)

That circle of orange bands on its upper wings is a stumper. Why would any butterfly wear a bullseye pattern to attract preditors? (See my [Next] photo tomorrow!)

School conferences are done! I have a week of vacation to explore the woods and get a few photos ;=)

File.Name: 7b17b_7b17-021.jpg
Notes: I was surprised to find our first new wildflower of the year today! This is native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family. It's especially surprising because it's growing along the berm of East Camino Cielo road that was recently re-paved. I've seen this before - that disturbing the soil encourages new growth. It doesn't feel like time for new flowers. This is the summer-dry end of a drought year, but we have had a bit of rain this fall (less than an inch) and here are the flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2007)

File.Name: 7b17c_7b17-008.jpg
Notes:
Autumn colors - and a critter!

Look close to spot the sleepy brown night-shift Cricket (family Gryllidae in the order Orthoptera) snuggled into this oak leaf like a natural hammock in a spent flower head of native composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family growing in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2007)

File.Name: 7b17d_7b16-039.jpg
Notes: Little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a Wild Mustard (Brassica sp.) flower in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family in the school garden yesterday. Use the mustard flower for scale - this pretty fly is really tiny! (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 November 2007)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Wild Mustard; Mustard

File.Name: 7b18a_7b18-042.jpg
Notes: Spent flower heads of native Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family posed in the woods with the sun behind to highlight the bristles. We're in that confusing period between late-season summer insects and flowers and new-season winter mushrooms, but there's always something ready for a photo! (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2007)

File.Name: 7b18b_7b18-052.jpg
Notes: I was trying to get a photo of these late-season flowers of Longstem Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum in the Polygonaceae family) still going in our mountains today, when this tiny Fly (order Diptera) moved into my photo. There's so much to learn in my backyard - what is that shiny black hump on this little fly's back? (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2007)

File.Name: 7b18c_7b17-032.jpg
Notes: I found this surprising Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly working the Mexican Sage (Salvia sp.) flowers in the garden yesterday. From this angle, the orange bands on its upper wings look more like giant threatening legs than a bullseye target! That criss-cross pattern between antennae and wings helps the illusion. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2007)

File.Name: 7b19a_7b19-095.jpg
Notes: I don't expect to find any butterflies in the woods at this time of year as we move into winter, but here is a tattered Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working the Mexican Sage (Salvia sp.) flowers in the garden today. It looks like a proud veteran with stories to tell about every wound. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2007)

File.Name: 7b19b_7b19-092.jpg
Notes: This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) looks like it"s trying to chew a path into this furry Mexican Sage (Salvia sp.) flower in the garden today. Most bees and wasps go right for the calyx at the base of the flower to get to the sweet nectar. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2007)

File.Name: 7b19c_7b19-071.jpg
Notes: Here's a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. It's one of our last insects working one of our last flowers as we move from autumn into winter. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2007)
CommonNames: Honey Bee; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Sunflower

File.Name: 7b20a_7b20-108.jpg
Notes:
Little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a flower of Salvia greggii) in the garden today. The sun is behind, and it has a glow. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2007) Garyblau posted another Syrphid Flower Fly from across the continent today. I guess it is Syrphid time!
   http://www.fotolog.com/garyblau/20918059

File.Name: 7b20b_6b18-020.jpg
Notes: Yellow-legged Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) or kin in the family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera perching on a leaf of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. These large wasps need to "catch rays" in the morning sun to warm up. Sunlight is getting scarce in our garden on the north-facing side of the mountains as we get closer to the winter solstice. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2007)
ScientificName: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 7b20c_7b19-072.jpg
Notes: There are still late-season flowers of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family flowering in our summer-dry mountains. The styles of the flowers look like they've been dipped in sugar. I didn't see any pollinator insects working them, but look close and you might spot a few tiny orange Thrips (order Thysanoptera) at the base of the flower tubes. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2007)
CommonNames: Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Sunflower; Thrips

File.Name: 7b20d_7b19-075.jpg
Notes: There are still a few flowers in the mountains like these native California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) flowers in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) plant family. I've been hunting for any flowers, insects, or mushrooms to photograph. The summer-dry woods are as dry as I've ever seen in this drought year. Even the Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) plants in the understory are dying back. We need real rain. (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2007)
ScientificName: Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Rubus ursinus
associatedTaxa: Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Rubus ursinus

File.Name: 7b21a_7b17-006.jpg
Notes:
The Grateful Dead used to have the Wall of Sound...

Here's a wall of honey-colored Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea or kin) in the garden on an old stump of a California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) tree on the dry side of the garden. These pretty mushrooms have since become Thanksgiving Dinner for maggots - see my [Previous] photos! (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2007)
CommonNames: Honey Mushrooms; California Bay; Oregon Myrtle

File.Name: 7b21b_7b17-007.jpg
Notes: Pretty honey-colored Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea or kin) in the garden on an old stump of a California Bay aka Oregon Myrtle (Umbellularia californica) tree on the dry side of the garden. These mushrooms have a "partial veil" that covers the gills before they open. As the cap expands, it leaves a "ring" of tissue on the stem, and you can see the remnants of the veil on the mushroom cap in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2007)
CommonNames: Honey Mushrooms; California Bay; Oregon Myrtle

File.Name: 7b21c_7b19-090.jpg
Notes: Maggots of Pomace Flies aka Small Fruit Flies in the family Drosophilidae in the order Diptera feeding on what's left of a a cluster Honey Mushroom (Armillariella mellea or kin) near the garden. Sure, different species give thanks for different feasts - but we should all give thanks now and then! (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2007)
CommonNames: Pomace Flies; Small Fruit Flies; Honey Mushroom

File.Name: 7b21d_7b19-087.jpg
Notes: What's left of this Honey Mushroom (Armillariella mellea or kin) in the garden has become Thanksgiving dinner for these maggots of Pomace Fly aka Small Fruit Fly in the family Drosophilidae in the order Diptera. Sure, different species give thanks for different feasts! (San Marcos Pass, 19 November 2007)
CommonNames: Honey Mushroom; Pomace Fly; Small Fruit Fly

File.Name: 7b21e_6b21-143.jpg
Notes:
I believe this is a Pomace Fly aka Small Fruit Fly in the family Drosophilidae of the order Diptera. I remember these from high school biology many years ago! These are the flies that lay the eggs that become the maggots that are consuming the Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea or kin) in the garden today! It's walking the line on a white-spore-dusted mushroom cap.

I never got a good photo of the adult flies this year, so I'm cheating with a never-before-posted photo from last year dated 21 November 2006 - this very day! (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2006)
CommonNames: Pomace Fly; Small Fruit Fly; Honey Mushrooms

File.Name: 7b22a_7b21-119.jpg
Notes: New growth from the base of a native Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree in a place that burned last summer near the river. This wasn't the huge Zaca fire that burned so much of the backcountry, but it was closer to home. And it shows something about life to be thankful for today - it goes on. (Santa Ynez River, 21 November 2007)

File.Name: 7b23a_7b23-153.jpg
Notes:
Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a flower of (I guess) Mexican Tree Daisy (Montanoa bipinnatifida) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family growing in my Dad's garden in a canyon in the Santa Monica Mountains. This is a remarkable fowering shrub with daisy flowers that is almost big enough to be a tree. (Santa Monica Mountains, 23 November 2007)

We shared Thanksgiving dinner with my Dad last night. It's good to be home, but it sure is cold. This might be our first frost night of the year.
CommonNames: Honey Bee; Mexican Tree Daisy; Sunflower

File.Name: 7b23b_7b21-138.jpg
Notes: Toasted Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei in the Lily Liliaceae) family) plants are still showing some green after the 500 acre Rancho Oso fire last summer near the river. They look like roasted pineapples! These natives survived this fire, but I'm worried about the backcountry where the huge Zaca fire burned so hot this summer. (Santa Ynez River, 21 November 2007)

File.Name: 7b23c_7b21-117.jpg
Notes: This stump-sprouting native Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) in the Sumac Anacardiaceae) family is coming back to life after the 500 acre Rancho Oso fire last summer near the river. This was before the huge Zaca fire that burned so much of the backcountry, and burned much hotter and longer. The big fire country is still closed to entry. I hope all the natives are stump-sprouting and showing new life like this. (Santa Ynez River, 21 November 2007)

File.Name: 7b24a_7b23-163.jpg
Notes: Large nickel-sized Horse Fly (Tabanus punctifer or kin in the order Diptera) working the non-native Mexican Tree Daisy (Montanoa bipinnatifida) flowers in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family growing in my Dad's garden in the canyon in the Santa Monica Mountains yesterday. (Santa Monica Mountains, 23 November 2007)
CommonNames: Horse Fly; Mexican Tree Daisy; Sunflower
ScientificName: Tabanus punctifer; Diptera; Montanoa bipinnatifida; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Tabanus punctifer; Diptera; Montanoa bipinnatifida; Asteraceae

File.Name: 7b24b_7b23-160.jpg
Notes: This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) has full pollen-baskets, but it seems to be working the flowers for nectar. I guess this flower is Mexican Tree Daisy (Montanoa bipinnatifida) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family growing in my Dad's garden in a canyon in the Santa Monica Mountains yesterday. (Santa Monica Mountains, 23 November 2007)
CommonNames: Honey Bee; Mexican Tree Daisy; Sunflower

File.Name: 7b25a_7b24-006.jpg
Notes: These are maybe the last flowers of California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium) in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae) family in the canyon. Look at the white pollen grains, and you can see that these flowers have done their stuff to make seeds. These are hummingbird flowers. I'm not sure what the hummers will do next. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2007)

File.Name: 7b25b_7b23-162.jpg
Notes: Here's a flower cluster of the interesting non-native Mexican Tree Daisy (Montanoa bipinnatifida) flowers in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family growing in my Dad's garden in the canyon in the Santa Monica Mountains yesterday. The plants are roof-high. We brought home a cutting, though I don't know if it will take our weather on the dark north-facing side of the mountains. We did have our first frost last night. Winter is coming. (Santa Monica Mountains, 23 November 2007)

File.Name: 7b25c_7b23-148.jpg
Notes: Another photo of this large nickel-sized Horse Fly (Tabanus punctifer or kin in the order Diptera) working the non-native Mexican Tree Daisy (Montanoa bipinnatifida) flowers in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family growing in my Dad's garden in the canyon in the Santa Monica Mountains. These big flies have a nasty bite, but it wasn't interested in me. Maybe this is a male and only the females need the "blood meal" to produce eggs? (Santa Monica Mountains, 23 November 2007)
CommonNames: Horse Fly; Mexican Tree Daisy; Sunflower
ScientificName: Tabanus punctifer; Diptera; Montanoa bipinnatifida; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Tabanus punctifer; Diptera; Montanoa bipinnatifida; Asteraceae

File.Name: 7b26a_7b24-003.jpg
Notes:
I found this egg-sized silk cocoon of a native Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus) high in a native shrub of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family in the autumn woods in the canyon. I couldn't get close, so I used my 500 mm telephoto lens to get this photo in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 24 November 2007)

I haven't seen any of the hand-sized moths this year, but this cocoon looks intact, so I don't want to disturb it until it's done. I'll come back for it! The cocoons feel and sound like touching the paper cone of a stereo speaker. It's a magical sound, and Native American Chumash shamans made rattles from the cocoons. The adult moths are uncommon, and the cocoons are a rare find. I've been collecting them for years. I have a basket full, and I plan to make my own rattle when the time is right. I'll be looking for the adult moths on the windows at night.
CommonNames: Ceanothus Silk Moth; Greenbark Ceanothus; Buckthorn
ScientificName: Hyalophora euryalus) high in a native shrub of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Hyalophora euryalus) high in a native shrub of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: 7b27a_7b18-045.jpg
Notes:
I believe this is native Squirreltail Grass (Elymus elymoides) or kin in the Poaceae family of grasses growing in the canyon with the sun behind. Looking closer, I can see at least two insect eggs attached on hair-like stalks at about 2:00 and 7:00 positions. (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2007)

This is as close as I can get. I had my 105 mm macro lens on my low tripod near 1:1 magnification, and cropped it tight. Remember how big a single aphid is!

File.Name: 7b28a_7b20-104.jpg
Notes:
Insects are scarce in the autumn woods in this drought-year, but there's always something to find as I look closer and closer. Here's an adult Milkweed Aphid (Aphis nerii or A. lutescens?) on the Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. It's perched to fly away, "carrying Mother Nature's seed to a new home in the sun". I find these golden aphids on the milkweeds every summer, but I don't know how they get from NOW to THEN. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2007)

This is as close as I can get. I had my 105 mm macro lens on my low tripod near 1:1 magnification, and cropped it tight. Remember how big a single aphid is!
ScientificName: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa
associatedTaxa: Aphis nerii; A. lutescens; Asclepias tuberosa

File.Name: 7b29a_7b13-018.jpg
Notes: Here's another photo as close as I can get. I had my 105 mm macro lens on my low tripod near 1:1 magnification, and cropped it tight. This is a tiny Looper or Inchworm caterpillar of a Geometrid Moth (family Geometridae or kin in the order Lepidoptera). The flowers are the last of the non-native Mints (Lamiaceae) in the garden. The little caterpillar isn't much larger than the stamens of the little mint flowers. It's a sign that autumn insects are scarce when I start posing tiny critters this size! (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2007)

File.Name: 7b30a_7b30-113.jpg
Notes:
Here is perfect camouflage that is invisible in the thumbnail - look close and the deer will jump out from the photo, just as they hide along the road until the last moment and jump out in front of your car if you're not careful!

I spotted this yearling Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus) trying to be invisible under the apple tree on the edge of the garden early this morning. The upright branches are supports we put in to hold up the apple limbs when they were full of apples. There are only a few apples left, too high for the deer or us to get to, but the birds are pecking at them, and knocking a few down for the deer to browse. Sweet juicy apples must be a real treat at the end of our drought year summer. (San Marcos Pass, 30 November 2007)

File.Name: 7b30b_7b29-100.jpg
Notes:
The late autumn sun is low in the sky, so the canyons get dark early, but the tall native deciduous Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) trees stand up to catch the last and best light of the day behind their golden leaves that will soon drop. It's a brief moment of glory, from the right place at the right time. (Santa Ynez River, 29 November 2007)

Do the autumn leaves glow if nobody sees them?

File.Name: 7c01a_7c01-007.jpg
Notes:
Insects and flowers are scarce at the end of this drought year, but there are new birds in the woods. Here is a brilliant male Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) bird in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near the creek today. These little birds fly south for the winter, and we are their "south". (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2007)

We had a trace of rain yesterday, but the cold north winds have been blowing all day today as the dry storm moves out. I captured a few winter birds with my long lens. I hope tomorrow will be a better day for photos.

File.Name: 7c01b_7c01-004.jpg
Notes: Little female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) perched in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near the porch today. It's been cold and very windy today. This hummer looks fluffed-up and ready for a nap, but it has been working hard all morning. Note the yellow pollen on its bill from the Salvia flowers in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c01c_7a27-001.jpg
Notes: Perky-looking Ruby-crowned Kinglet (Regulus calendula) bird in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees near our upstairs porch. These little birds fly south for the winter, and we are their "south". They've been around for a month or more, but it's hard to get a photo of these fast little birds! (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c02a_7c01-010.jpg
Notes: Like Icarus, I shot too near the sun - but it's still a nice photo, like natural solarization! Here are new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family with spider webs and the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c02b_7c02-031.jpg
Notes:
New flowers in the woods after our long dry summer should be exciting, but these new flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family are disturbing. I guess these native shrubs are programed to flower in fall, but in this drought year before the winter rains, the flowers are malformed and disturbing. (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2007)

Compare my photo of the first Manzanita flowers last year:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/17688074

File.Name: 7c03a_7c02-024.jpg
Notes: Ripe Christmas Berries aka Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood) (Heteromeles arbutifolia) in the Rose (Rosaceae) family. The berries look good and plentiful - good news for the birds in this drought year! (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2007)
CommonNames: Christmas Berries; Toyon; California Holly; Rose

File.Name: 7c03b_7c01-010.jpg
Notes: This is the same shrub as my [Previous] photo, but I moved my tripod a few inches to the left to get the sun glare out of my lens. These are new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family with spider webs and the sun behind. All that technical camera stuff is important for a phoito, but moving a few inches is important too! I'm not sure which photo I like best. (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c04a_7c02-017.jpg
Notes: Like a classic pawn shop logo - ripe nuts of our interesting native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis var. redivivus aka S. redivivus) shrubs in the Storax (Styracaceae) family. These interesting native shrubs are common on San Marcos Pass, but they are scarce in California. I don't know what critters eat the nuts, but they are disappearing fast. (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2007)
ScientificName: Styrax officinalis; redivivus; S. redivivus; Styracaceae
associatedTaxa: Styrax officinalis; redivivus; S. redivivus; Styracaceae

File.Name: 7c04b_7b01-268.jpg
Notes: Toyon berries aren't the only red "Christmas berries" in the woods. These are the ripening fruit of my favorite native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees in the Heath (Ericaceae) family. The berries are usually high up in the trees and hard to photograph. I used my long lens. The berries are tasty and less astringent than the toyon berries. They've been disappearing fast. (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c05a_7c05-089.jpg
Notes: When I was a surfer kid in Santa Monica High School back in the '60's, we used to ditch a day now and then when the surf was up. Now I'm the teacher, and I'm so proud that we ditched our whole school to check out the big waves today! This is C-Street aka California Street in Ventura today. There were only a few surfers in the water, and I didn't have much time for photos. Here's one of the jet skis that was doing tow-in service for the surfers. (C-Street, Ventura, 5 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c06a_7c06-196.jpg
Notes:
Kids and dogs agree - there's nothing better than chasing birds at the beach!

I took my after-school hike club kids to remote Surf Beach at the mouth of the Santa Ynez River today. I was hoping for more huge waves, but our "Big Wednesday" swell has already passed. No problem. This is a magical beach at a minus low tide. Beach, waves, land, and sky seem to merge into one. And there are gulls to chase! (Surf Beach, 6 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c07a_7c06-194.jpg
Notes:
Gulls taking wing from remote Surf Beach at the mouth of the Santa Ynez River during a minus low tide yesterday. Look at my [Previous] photo to see why! (Surf Beach, 6 December 2007)

Looking close along the bottom of the photo, I see three small birds that have joined the flock. I guess they are Western Snowy Plovers (Charadrius alexandrinus nivosus) that nest on the ground in the nearby dunes. The birds are endangered in California, and this is an important nesting site. The beach is closed each year from March to October to protect them.
Locality: Charadrius alexandrinus nivosus) that nest on the ground in the nearby dunes. The birds are endangered in California

File.Name: 7c08a_7c08-008.jpg
Notes:
I believe these new mushrooms are Ringed Panaeolus (Panaeolus semiovatus aka P. separatus) growing from old horse dung in the canyon. It started raining late Thurday night, and we still have some drizzle today. Total rainfall was only about 3/4 inch (2 cm), and today is windy and cold. Maybe that's enough to break the drought, at least it was enough to bring up the first mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2007)

Look close - below the big mushrooms, you can see tiny Horsehair Fungi (Marasmius androsaceus or kin) mushrooms sprouting from the fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves. See my [Previous] photo.
CommonNames: Ringed Panaeolus; Horsehair Fungi; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Panaeolus semiovatus; P. separatus; Marasmius androsaceus; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Panaeolus semiovatus; P. separatus; Marasmius androsaceus; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 7c08b_7c08-009.jpg
Notes:
You get down on your knees to capture a photo of the small things in the woods like these Ringed Panaeolus (Panaeolus semiovatus aka P. separatus) mushrooms that I found today. Then you lay down on your belly and notice the tiny things like these Horsehair Fungi (Marasmius androsaceus or kin) mushrooms sprouting from fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the woods! Then you start wondering about all those teeny dark spots on the fallen leaves... (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2007)

   "Great fleas have little fleas upon their backs to bite 'em,
   And little fleas have lesser fleas, and so ad infinitum..."
   - Augustus De Morgan
CommonNames: Ringed Panaeolus; Horsehair Fungi; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Panaeolus semiovatus; P. separatus; Marasmius androsaceus; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Panaeolus semiovatus; P. separatus; Marasmius androsaceus; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 7c08c_7c08-004.jpg
Notes: I lose track of the seasons between autumn and spring in our mountains, and so do the plants. I found this new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Phlox (Polemoniaceae) family in the canyon today. We had 3/4 inch of rain starting Thursday night, and we still have drizzle today - and a chance of snow flurries tonight as the temperature drops. But here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c09a_7c02-023.jpg
Notes:
Yipes! - this shrub has an aneurysm, it's going to blow!

Nah, this is a fresh-looking "oak apple" gall on a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) shrub in the mountains. Oaks make acorns of course. These galls are really tumors created by tiny Gall Wasps in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera to make a home for their young. I guess this gall is home to larvae of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) or kin. Scrub Oaks are "White Oaks" related to Valley Oaks (Quercus lobata). "Black Oaks" like Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) have similar galls caused by different Cynipidaewasps. Nature is complicated like that. (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2007)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Gall Wasps; California Gall Wasp; Valley Oaks; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Quercus lobata; Quercus agrifolia; Cynipidae
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Quercus lobata; Quercus agrifolia; Cynipidae

File.Name: 7c09b_7c02-013.jpg
Notes: Mature acorn of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae family. The leaves are tattered after this drought year, but it's still a pretty good acorn year despite the weather. That's good news for all the critters who depend on the acorns in the fall! (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c09c_7c02-026.jpg
Notes: Empty seed heads of native BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family. It's interesting that most of the pods have three sections for seeds, but that one pod at the bottom clearly has four sections, like a four-leaf clover. All taxonomy is tentative, like a still photo of a moving subject. (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c10a_7b18-034.jpg
Notes:
Here's a different wasp gall on a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) shrub in the mountains. These galls are really tumors created by tiny Gall Wasps in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera to make a home for their young. I guess these galls are home to larvae of the Twig Gall Wasps (Disholcaspis mellifica) or kin in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. These aren't the usual wasps that disturb our barbeques. They look more like small flies, and they really have no interest in us humans or our stuff. Notice the hole in the upper gall. Either a wasp came out, or a preditor went in, or an inquiline preditor or commensal that has been in there all-along came out. The pencil-erasor-size galls are just the visible stub of rich life in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2007)

Yes Puppy_k, I wonder why the "Oak apple" galls in my [Previous] photo are bright red to attract attention. And why do these "Muffin galls" (my name) exude sweet honeydew in summer that attracts ants and yellowjacket wasps who harvest the nectar. I also wonder why mushrooms are so beautiful and colorful and strange when their only job (according to the books) is to release spores. I'm sure there are communities o/f life here between many organiosms based on mutual aid that we don't yet understand!
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Gall Wasps; Twig Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Disholcaspis mellifica; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Disholcaspis mellifica; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: 7c11a_7c08-016.jpg
Notes: We had 3/4 inch of rain on Friday, and I found this new Cortinarius mushroom (there are many species) pushing up through the oak leaves fully formed on Saturday morning! It looks like a king of the woods crowned with oak leaves! (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c12a_7c08-026.jpg
Notes: Strange Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus or kin) "mushroom" - fungus at least - growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the canyon. It looks like it walked here from the beach! They start out round like a puffball. The outer rind splits and curves backwards into rays that actually lift the fruiting body from the ground. The thin white inner sac soon releases its spores and vanishes, though you can see the base of it here. They must open fast. I always find the old rinds with no connection to the earth, as though they really did walk here. As the species name suggests, they are natural hygrometers that open and close with changes in humidity. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c12b_7c08-021.jpg
Notes:
This is the same mushroom as my [Previous] photo that I found a day after our Friday rain in this drought year. I plucked it and moved it to the sun for a photo - and then replanted it in the same place, of course. It's a Cortinarius mushroom with a remnant veil of cobwebby hairs that originally covered the gills, but are now fallen and dusted with rusty-brown spores. There are many species, but I reckon this is subgenus Bulbopodium with a slimy cap and an abrupt basal bulb - maybe Cortinarius fulmineus or kin. I'm surprised to find it so early in the rainy season. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2007)

I don't even think about eating our Cortinarius mushrooms - there are too many different kinds, and they all end up looking the same as they age. This one looks ochre or tawny-orange color, but I see some violet in the gills. Which is the real color?

File.Name: 7c13a_7c02-030.jpg
Notes: Cottonwood, Maple, Willow, and Sycamore Trees get top bill for fall colors in our mountains, but this uncommon native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax officinalis var. redivivus aka S. redivivus) shrub in the Storax (Styracaceae) family shows some pretty autumn colors too. I think this is a stumper why these winter-deciduous plants drop their leaves just as the first winter rains arrive and many other native plants start to come alive after our long dry summer. (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2007)
ScientificName: Styrax officinalis; redivivus; S. redivivus; Styracaceae
associatedTaxa: Styrax officinalis; redivivus; S. redivivus; Styracaceae

File.Name: 7c13b_7c08-024.jpg
Notes: I believe this is the same kind of Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus or kin) "mushroom" - a fungus at least - as my [Previous] photo. This one is just opening up in the woods, showing its precious dark spore mass that will carry the genetic seed into the woods to start new Earthstars. It's really a kind of puffball. The thick outer rind splits and bends back to make the starfish-like legs that lift the fruiting body from the ground and spread spores into the air and away. It's not beautiful, but here is life doing its stuff! (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c14a_7c02-019.jpg
Notes: These flower buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) family look fully-formed with Christmas colors, but they will wait a few more months before opening for business next spring. It's an interesting strategy. Several plants form their buds early, but many don't. I wonder if there's an advantage, or is this just how it's always been done? (San Marcos Pass, 2 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c15a_7c15-026.jpg
Notes:
Face-to-face and eye-to-compund-eyes with a surprising late-season Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly that I found in the woods today near the creek. This is a true "Brush-footed" (subfamily Nymphalinae) butterfly. "Stem-footed" is more like it, with those skinny legs. Look close, and you can see that this butterfly is resting on just four legs. You can see the tips of the other two reduced front legs below the eyes, combing its shaggy Santa Christmas whiskers! (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2007)

I also spotted a California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly today, though I missed the photo. Different butterflies over-winter as eggs or larvae or pupae, and some butterflies do over-winter as adults.

File.Name: 7c15b_7c15-021.jpg
Notes: Pretty Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly in the woods today with cryptic camouflage on the contrasty fallen autumn Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the canyon. It's been cold and frosty this week, so I was surprised to find any butterflies in the woods today, but here it is. These Ladies occasionally stream north from Mexico in the spring in mass migrations. Maybe this one is trying to get home. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c15c_7c15-009.jpg
Notes:
It's been frosty all week, with lows in the mid-20s Fahrenheit, so I was surprised to find this pretty Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly in the woods today on the sunny side of the canyon. It doesn't seem like camouflage with those bright butterfly colors, but it sure was hard to spot this butterfly at rest on the fallen oak leaves in the woods today! (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2007)

I also spotted a California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly today, though I couldn't get a photo. Different butterflies over-winter as eggs or larvae or pupae, and some butterflies do over-winter as adults.

File.Name: 7c16a_7c15-008.jpg
Notes:
Skeletonized leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon. It's like fairy ring mushrooms in the grass - a fungus started in the center of the leaf and it's spreading out to the edges cell by cell. All that's left are tiny translucent panes, like a stained-glass window. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2007)

We had rain last weekend, but the woods are now cold and dry. There's a chance of more rain tomorrow night. Our woods are still caught between the dry-season summer and wet-seaoin winter. It's not easy to find these new photos every week, but I'm looking closer and finding more.

File.Name: 7c16b_7c16-050.jpg
Notes: New flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family. Look close, and you can spot a clutch of insect eggs in the middle-right. I have no idea what they are. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c16c_7c16-038.jpg
Notes:
Little Western Spotted Cucumber Beetle (Diabrotica undecimpunctata, order Coleoptera) reaching from flower to seed head on native Bush Sunflower (Encelia californica in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family). (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2007)

The beetle and the shrub are both introduced, at least here. I usually find these native plants lower in the mountains, but here they are at 3,000+ feet along East Camino Cielo where they don't belong. I'm sure the highway work crews spread the seeds and the weeds as they do their work on the mountain roads. I don't mind the Encelia, but the Star Thistle and Spanish Broom along the mountain roads are a real problem. There must be ways to clean the equipment of seeds before a new job in a different environment.
CommonNames: Western Spotted Cucumber Beetle; Bush Sunflower; Sunflower
ScientificName: Diabrotica undecimpunctata; Coleoptera) reaching from flower to seed head on native Bush Sunflower (Encelia californica; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Diabrotica undecimpunctata; Coleoptera) reaching from flower to seed head on native Bush Sunflower (Encelia californica; Asteraceae

File.Name: 7c17a_7c15-006.jpg
Notes:
There are still new flowers appearing on the native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrubs in the Grossulariaceae family growing on the dry edge of our garden, though I don't see many flowers in the summer-dry woods away from the garden. I consider this the first flowering plant of the new rain year. I noticed the first flowers this year at the end of September, and here they are still going when there are very few other flowers in the woods. This humble shrub is an important food source for late-season hummingbirds, bees, and Syrphid flies during the most difficult time of the year. They keep the woods alive. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2007)

The good news is that it rained today, and we are expecting more rain tomorrow!

File.Name: 7c17b_7c16-034.jpg
Notes:
Tiny new umbel flowers of native Tauschia arguta in the Parsley plant family (Apiaceae). I found it flowering in a place that was "masticated" for a fire break last summer by an enormous TimbCo T425 "Hydro-shredder" machine - aka "The Masticator". I'm surprised to find any native flowers at the end of this drought year, but here it is. I looked through my archive, and I first photographed this flower in Februrary in past years. I know that disturbing the land does sometimes stimulate the natives to bloom out of season. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2007)

I like to nibble on the seeds of this plant later in spring - they have a pleasant caraway taste ("kummel" in german!) - but I have no idea if they are "edible"!

File.Name: 7c18a_7c18-072.jpg
Notes:
Impressive Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) with a bottle cap-sized abdomen trying to keep dry under the eave of our porch on a rainy night. Of course I got this photo at night with flash. I only noticed this spider because we're cleaning house tonight (in the dark) to get ready for a school teacher/staff party tomorrow night. I'm sure glad I didn't walk into this spider web! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2007)

The best news is that we're getting a real drought-buster rain today. So far, we've had over 6 inches (15 cm) of rain - that's getting close to our total for all last year! I don't want to jinx it, but it's funny that last year was an El NiÒo year with a forecast for a wet year - that became a drought; and this year is a La NiÒa year with a forecast for a dry year - that looks pretty wet, tonight at least! This sounds like a stumper...
Locality: 15 cm) of rain - that's getting close to our total for all last year!  I don't want to jinx it

File.Name: 7c19a_7c16-041.jpg
Notes: Spent flowers and a few last seeds on a head of native composite Bush Sunflower (Encelia californica) in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. The bare receptacle of the head looks like a binding ring. Heh - the flat seeds have little Santa beards for Christmas! (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c20a_7c16-048.jpg
Notes:
Looking up at a sunny flower of a native composite Bush Sunflower (Encelia californica) shrub in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. I usually find these native shrubs below 1500 feet in the mountains, but here they are at 3,000+ feet where they don't belong. I'm sure the highway work crews and machinery spread the seeds and the weeds as they do their work on the mountain roads. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2007)

I got this photo last weekend at the end of our drought year summer. Everything looks different today. We had 7.3 inches of rain yesterday, and more drizzle today!

File.Name: 7c20b_7c16-043.jpg
Notes:
A Christmas garland of new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family in the mountains. I got this photo last weekend at the end of a drought year summer. Everything looks different today. We had 7.3 inches of rain yesterday, and more drizzle today. It's perfect timing for me - today was my last day of school before the holidays. I think there will be much to photograph as the woods come alive after the rain. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2007)

There's nothing better than a Christmas gift of rain after a drought year, except maybe our kids and family all coming together for the holidays!

File.Name: 7c21a_7c21-275.jpg
Notes: There is new life in the woods after our drought-buster storm dropped 7.5 inches of rain a few days ago! Here are the first little white-spored Mycena mushrooms coming up from the fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the woods, just in time for the Winter Solstice tonight at 10:08 P.M. PST. I expect many more mushroom to soon appear. The days will start getting longer tonight in our mountains north of the Equator. It's the perfect time for new life and new hope in the woods - and in our lives. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c21b_7c21-272.jpg
Notes: It rained three days ago, and already I found this hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) exploring the woods today! This is our largest terrestrial mollusk (Gastropoda). Wikipedia says it's the second-largest species of slug in the world. I wonder how they appear so soon after the first big rain after a drought summer. Where do they hide during the dry summer, and how do they revive so fast after rain? (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c21c_7c21-241.jpg
Notes:
New life is already appearing on this fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) branch in the woods after the good rain this week. That slimy yellow mass is the Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica or kin) mushroom. I guess it's edible, it's said to be "harmless but flavorless". These gelatinous fungi are mostly water. They shrivel up to nothing when dry and can revive quickly after a rain - even after a long drought summer. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2007)

There's lots more life on this branch. I believe the brown shelf-fungi at the top are Sterium striatum or kin. There are also green foliose lichens, and many more critters if you look close.
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Tremella mesenterica; Sterium striatum
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Tremella mesenterica; Sterium striatum

File.Name: 7c22a_7c22-002.jpg
Notes: Perfect little Earthstar (Astreus hygrometricus or kin) "mushroom" - at least it's the fruiting body of a fungus - growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. These mushrooms appear suddenly, like they creep in on starfish-like feet from somewhere else. It's really a double-skinned puffball mushroom. The thick outer skin opens like a star and curves down to lift the thin-skined inner spore sack to expose the rusty spores to the elements. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c22a_7c22-003.jpg
Notes: More little white-spored mushrooms coming up from the fallen leaves in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c23a_7c22-037.jpg
Notes:
This is looking into the same cave as my [Previous] two photos, but from the other side. The cave passes through a sandstone outcrop near Lizard Mouth in the mountains. This is looking through the cave towards the setting sun yesterday on the winter solstice day. The naturally sculpted sandstone rock is remarkable. I call this kind of sculpted rock a "rain rock" - though I can't remember why. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2007)

Lizard Mouth has become a popular place for rock climbing and partying and drum circles and shooting in our mountains. Trash and graffiti are a problem. There was a horrible murder near here a few years ago - the story is told in the movie Alpha Dog. Serious bad vibes, like murder in the cathedral.

Lizard Mouth is a sacred place for the native American Chumash people. There are many hidden rock-art painted caves nearby that mark the ancient trail over San Marcos Pass from ocean to river. I could feel those serious good vibes on the solstice day yesterday.

File.Name: 7c23b_7c22-041.jpg
Notes: Looking into an open cave (or arch?) that passes through a sandstone outcrop near Lizard Mouth in the mountains yesterday with the sun behind on thel winter solstice evening. The open back of the cave almost looks like it's been shaped - but what shape is it? (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c23c_7c22-050.jpg
Notes: An open cave through a sandstone outcrop near Lizard Mouth in the mountains yesterday. It's picking up some extra color from the sunset behind me on this magical winter solstice evening. See my [Next] few photos. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c23d_7c22-062.jpg
Notes: Winter solstice sunset over the rocks and oaks near Lizard Mouth in the mountains yesterday. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c24a_7725-145.jpg
Notes:
Peace on Earth,
good will toward men, women, and children,
and all the plants, mushrooms, and critters in the woods,
and I wish all the best to my fotolog friends for the holidays!

I believe this is a female Rufous/Allen's humingbird (Selasphorus rufus/sasin) - I can't tell the two species apart, so it seems best to lump them together. It has angel wings! I got this photo in the garden last summer, but I knew right away that I wanted to save it for Christmas. And yes, I appreciate the irony that hummers are feisty little birds that are anything but peaceful - to other hummingbirds at least! (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2007)

File.Name: 7c25a_7c25-089.jpg
Notes:
Pretty little bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) resting on a leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata in the Mint (Labiatae) family) on the sunny side of the canyon this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2007)

I reckon the real question is why these pretty flower flies are still on the wing when there are almost no flowers left in the canyon. See my [Previous] two photos! I appreciate that this is purely circumstantial evidence, but I have to guess that this fly and its kin are preying on the spiders. It's the web of life.
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Wood Mint; Mint

File.Name: 7c25b_7c25-086.jpg
Notes:
Tiny little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) resting on a sandstone outcrop with new moss buds across the creek on the sunny side of the canyon. This is as close as I can get - a tight crop from a photo taken at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. I have to measure depth of field in sand grains! The little spider is pretty with Christmas colors. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2007)

Now look closer between the spider's two body parts ("cephalothorax" that combines head and legs, and separate abdomen) and spot the brown shape with bulges, like an arm reaching around from under. Spiders have no body part like this! I'm pretty sure this is a fly (order Diptera) larva/maggot that has found it's prey.

Look at this photo and my [Previous] photo taken a minute later. You can see that the fly larva has moved. Look very close at the white hairs on the upper end of the spider's abdomen. They are standing up in this photo, but they are pushed down flat in my [Previous] photo to mark the larva's path. I reckon this is bad news for the spider, but a fine holiday gift for the fly. As the Grateful Dead used to sing:

"Talk about your plenty, talk about your ills,
One man gathers what another man spills."

Like a CSI - crime scene investigation, I noticed one interesting fly loitering nearby enjoying the morning sun. See my [Next] photo!
Locality:
order Diptera) larva/maggot that has found it's prey.

Look at this photo and my [Previous] photo taken a minute later. You can see that the fly larva has moved. Look very close at the white hairs on the upper end of the spider's abdomen. They are standing up in this photo

File.Name: 7c25c_7c25-087.jpg
Notes:
Tiny little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) resting on a sandstone outcrop with new moss buds across the creek on the sunny side of the canyon. This is as close as I can get - a tight crop from a photo taken at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. The little spider is pretty with Christmas colors. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2007)

Now look closer between the spider's two body parts ("cephalothorax" that combines head and legs, and separate abdomen) and spot the brown shape with bulges, like an arm reaching around from under. Spiders have no body part like this! I'm pretty sure this is a fly (order Diptera) larvae/maggot that has found it's prey. See my [Next] photo.
Locality: cephalothorax" that combines head and legs

File.Name: 7c25d_7c25-085.jpg
Notes:
Its's too cold for insects and too dry for mushrooms - despite 7.5 inches of rain last week, the humidity is low, and we had very strong winds last night to dry everything out. When I can't find anything new to photograph in the woods, I like to find a sunny spot and sit and wait to see what comes along.

Here's a little Wolf Spider (family Lycosidae) in the fallen oak leaves near the still-dry creek. After I spotted one spider, I noticed a dozen more prowling through the leaves for prey! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c26a_7c21-249.jpg
Notes:
Unknown perennial native grass-like plant in the woods. I'm pretty sure it's not a real grass in the Poaceae family, but more likely a Sedge (Cyperaceae) or Rush (Juncaceae), even though it's growing in the woodland and not by a creek. I can't tell what it is from the photo. I'll try to get back to it tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2007)

Good hike today - though it was too windy for photos - followed by a wonderful Italian dinner. I'm ready for a nap in front of the wood stove!

File.Name: 7c27a_7c26-124.jpg
Notes: This is a Native American Chumash shell bead resting on a gopher mound near the "rain rock" in my [Previous] photo. You can use the sand grains for scale - the shell bead is about the diameter of a pencil erasor. I noticed the bead resting on a gopher mound at the site of the Chumash village Soxtonokmu that was occupied into the 1800s. Looking closer, we found several more beads on other gopher mounds. The gophers bring them up, and the first rains wash them clean before the new grass hides them. I didn't take or even touch any of the ancient beads. It's the law, and I do feel the ancient presence in our mountains. (Figueroa Mountain, 26 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c27b_7c26-120.jpg
Notes: Here is Julie touching a "rain rock" at the site of the Native American Chumash village Soxtonokmu that was occupied into the 1800s. Only the rocks remain. It's easy to walk past this clearing in the woods without noticing anything special, but it is a haunted place with ancient vibes. The rain rocks were ground down into shallow cups - maybe they offer the cups in hope to catch rains to come? There are several of these ancient rain rocks in the mountains near home on San Marcos Pass. In this drought year, maybe I should make a new one! (Figueroa Mountain, 26 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c27c_7c26-109.jpg
Notes: My family hiking into the woods above Birabent Creek at the base of Grass Mountain on the edge of the backcountry yesterday. The Santa Ynez Mountains run east-west. We live on the north-facing slope of the mountains where the sun is scarce at this time of year, so we went for a hike on the sunny south-facing slopes of the San Rafael Mountains that are north of us across the river and valley. It's a completely different landscape. The pines in this photo are Gray Pines aka Digger Pines (Pinus sabiniana), and the golden-leaved oaks are Blue Oaks (Quercus douglasii) in the Fagaceae family. Both trees are scarce on our side of the valley. (Figueroa Mountain, 26 December 2007)
CommonNames: Gray Pines; Digger Pines; Blue Oaks

File.Name: 7c28a_7c25-081.jpg
Notes:
I was surprised to find any caterpillar in the woods on a very cold frosty morning, but here it is! I'm pretty sure this is the caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on foliage of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Diplacus longiflorus) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family in the canyon on Christmas morning. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2007)

These are our most common butterflies in early summer, and I'm just starting to understand their life cycle in our canyon. The adults are on the wing in June and July. I notice late-summer caterpillars on native plants in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family, but they disappear before they mature. My book says that they "overwinter or spend extended dry periods as partially grown caterpillars in a special 'thicker' skin with a more dense covering of spines." I guess they go dormant until about now when they emerge again.

It's confusing, because more new caterpillars will also emerge later in spring - I guess they over-winter both as larvae and eggs. It's even more confusing because some of the caterpillars are web-spinners that form communal webs on Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) plants in the spring, but other caterpillars are solitary and don't seem to make webs. Maybe I'm confusing the different species of Checkerspots? I'll keep watching.

It's been busy all week with our kids visiting for the holidays. Ry left last night, and Ewan and Roz left early this morning. *Sigh* It's quiet tonight, but I will have more time for photos this week.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Bush Monkey Flower; Figwort; Figwort; Climbing Penstemon
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Keckiella cordifolia
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus longiflorus; Diplacus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Keckiella cordifolia

File.Name: 7c28b_7c28-158.jpg
Notes:
New staminate male catkins of Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the Silk-Tassel (Garryaceae) family. This native shrub is "dioecious" with separate male and female plants, and the males usually appear first. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2007)

Despite good rain a week ago, the weather has been very dry and windy this week. I was happy to find any new flower in the woods today - even a strange one like this! The good news is that we're getting some drizzle tonight - not quite rain, but better than dry wind.

File.Name: 7c28c_7c26-114.jpg
Notes: Here is a green woody shrub growing from the branch of a winter-bare tree! I didn't climb the Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree to check, but I guess the shrub is Big-leaved Mistletoe (Phoradendron macrophyllum) growing on a bare branch of the winter-deciduous tree. Mistletoe really is a woody shrub, and it is a true parasite, not just an epiphyte - it sinks its roots into the host tree for sustenance. Watch where you walk in the woods - even better, hike with someone you want to kiss under the real mistletoe! (Figueroa Mountain, 26 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c29a_7c29-004.jpg
Notes:
Hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) exploring the woods today after just a drizzle of rain yesterday. This is our largest terrestrial mollusk (Gastropoda), and they are only active after rains (even drizzles) when there is some fresh moisture in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2007)

These critters are shy and sensitive. They have two eye-stalks above and two feelers below to sense where they are in the woods. This one is sensing the fallen oak leaf with its lower-right sensor. I also like to crawl through the woods, and I feel kinship with these creatures.

File.Name: 7c29b_7c29-003.jpg
Notes: New flower buds of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) in the Grossulariaceae family with the sun behind. The red-colored "petals" are leafy bracts at the base of the new growth. The flowers will come later. After this drought year, I'm happy to see any new life in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c29c_7c29-022.jpg
Notes: New flower buds of native Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata) in the Berberidaceae family. It only grows in shady places where the sun never shines. I cheated and used flash for this photo because it was so dark. This shrub is a kind of "Oregon Grape" that really belongs further north in Oregon, but here it is in our canyon along with the remnant Madrone and Tanoak trees that also belong further north. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c29d_7c29-018.jpg
Notes: New flowers of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family growing where the sun never shines at this time of year. I reckon this is the first wildflower of the year. I was wondering why it flowers when ther are no insects on the wing, but looking closer at thew full-res photo, I can see a little aphid on the left and a thrip on the right. This close to the winter solstice, any flower and any insect are interesting! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c30a_7c30-072.jpg
Notes:
A mosh of Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) on a leaf of non-native Blue Gum (Eucalyptus globulus or kin) tree. It's a butterfly tree - now preserved in the Ellwood Main Monarch Grove as part of the Coronado Butterfly Preserve. Here's some info:
   http://www.sblandtrust.org/coronado.html

The butterflies come back to the same trees year after year. There are so many thousands of butterflies of butterflies that the real challenge is to photograph just a few! (Ellwood Mesa, 30 December 2007)

I got all of today's photos with my Tamron 200-500 mm lens. I took lots of photos today and selected a few. I want to go back and just sit and watch for a while to understand, and then maybe take a few more that really capture the scene.

Seeing all these butterflies reminds me that there are NO winter ladybugs converging in the canyon yet. It's easy to spot what's new, but it's hard to notice what's missing in a drought year. I've also noticed that the Manzanitas (Arctostaphylos) in the Heath (Ericaceae) family don't have the usual bright red aphid-galled leaves.
CommonNames: Monarch Butterflies; Blue Gum; Manzanitas; Heath
ScientificName: Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera; Eucalyptus globulus; Arctostaphylos; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera; Eucalyptus globulus; Arctostaphylos; Ericaceae

File.Name: 7c30b_7c30-053.jpg
Notes:
Incoming Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) trying to join the crowd on a butterfly tree at the Ellwood Butterfly Preserve today. Thousands of these butterflies gather here (and other butterflly trees along the west coast) from all the western states on this side of the Rocky Mountains. (Ellwood Mesa, 30 December 2007)

I had my camera and long lens set up on my tripod, and I was using the camera self-timer to minimize vibrations. This photo was good luck. It helps that I took 130 photos today - it's that kind of good luck!

File.Name: 7c30c_7c30-049.jpg
Notes: Strange fruit hanging from the Blue Gum (Eucalyptus globulus or kin) trees... Here are masses of Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) that gather here (and other butterflly trees along the west coast) from all the western states on this side of the Rocky Mountains. Eastern Monarchs head to Mexico. It's hard to make sense of this many butterflies at once, and a challenge to photograph. (Ellwood Mesa, 30 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c30d_7c30-026.jpg
Notes:
Branches of a non-native Blue Gum (Eucalyptus globulus or kin) tree - with a bonus branch of Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera)! They almost look like leaves in the shade. (Ellwood Mesa, 30 December 2007)

There are masses of Monarch butterflies in the Eucalyptus trees at the Ellwood Butterfly Preserve right now. This Eucalyptus grove is now protected. The butterflies come to the same trees every winter. I wanted to get closer, but I stayed behind the rope barrier. ;=)

There's more info here:
   http://www.sblandtrust.org/coronado.html

File.Name: 7c31a_7c31-089.jpg
Notes:
A new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Phlox (Polemoniaceae) family in the canyon today. I follow the sunbeams here on the north side of the mountains at this time of year, so it's easy to get a natural dark background. This phlox wasn't flowering two days ago, but life goes on. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2007)

I've really been looking for late-season insects, but the flower caught my eye. Hah - I didn't notice until I got the photo on my computer screen, but look behind the leaves below the flower - I guess this is a Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae in the family Reduviidae of the order Hemiptera). I even moved to the other side of the flower to get a photo with different light, and the insect counter-moved around the plant to hide. I guess the lesson is that there's always something new to find in the woods!
Locality: subfamily Emesinae in the family Reduviidae of the order Hemiptera). I even moved to the other side of the flower to get a photo with different light
ScientificName: Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera

File.Name: 7c31b_7c31-079.jpg
Notes: I believe these little mushrooms in the garden are Stalked Puffballs (Tulostoma brumale or kin). Look close and you can see their nipple-like "apical pore" on top that releases the rusty-orange spores. Note the cap has two layers. The rough outer cap (or peridium) is covered with sand and peeling away to show the smooth inner cap. I sometimes find related species in the desert. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c31c_7c31-084.jpg
Notes: A fly-sized fly in the woods. I recognize those bright red eyes - I guess this is a Scavenger Fly (family Sarcophagidae in the order Diptera) resting on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. These flies seem to overwinter as... flies, and I can usually find them active in a sunny place even in winter. I guess they live up to their latin name, and there is always new business for the scavengers. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2007)

File.Name: 7c31d_7c30-023.jpg
Notes: With thousands of Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) in the Ellwood butterfly trees, the hardest thing is to get a photo of just one! (See my [Previous] photos.) This butterfly is "puddling" on the wet grass for water. It's one of those shots where you want to reach out to move the plants over a bit, but you know you can't. (Ellwood Mesa, 30 December 2007)

File.Name: 6113a.jpg
Notes:
We hope you have enjoyed meeting some of the great variety of
birds who share the Christmas season with us. Next year - in
the spirit of the times - recycling will begin. But our
holiday greeting will always be as fresh as a new Christmas tree!
With Christmas love and all good wishes - Bob and Norma

Bob and Norma are my Dad and Mom. Recycling means that Mom started reusing her old cards when she could no longer cut out new ones.

See http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=12078071

My Mom is no longer with us. I've been posting her paper cut-out Christmas cards of our local birds - keep clicking [Previous] to see them all. I think this is Mom's last new card, when arthritis was turning her hands into claws and she could no longer use scissors to cut out paper birds.

Thanks Mom, I'll always notice the birds and remember.

I love these images. It's an honor to share them here. Please copy and use them for your own personal loving uses and give credit to Norma Kummel. No other use is allowed - I want to publish these for Mom. Thank you all who have left your comments and love here. Life goes on...
