File.Name: 6114a_6114-018.jpg
Notes: One of the first flowers of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) in the canyon, with raindrops. We had a good soaking rain today. I managed to get out between the showers for some photos. No flash on this dark day. This photo is 1/2 second @ f/22. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2006)

File.Name: 6114b_6114-009.jpg
Notes: New flower of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) with rain drops. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2006)

File.Name: 6114c_6114-002.jpg
Notes: New leaf of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) holding rain drops. Every drop is a lens showing the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2006)

File.Name: 6114d_6114-013.jpg
Notes: Finding mushrooms like this brilliant red Russula emetica (or maybe R. rosacea?) in the winter woods is like finding treasure in the ocean - it's so bright when everything else is dark! Not edible, but it has a hot peppery taste that I like - nibble on a small piece and then spit it out. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2006)

File.Name: 6114e_6114-012.jpg
Notes: Perfect edible and delicious Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom in the woods. I laid on my belly to capture the underside in this photo. It has smooth folds underneath instead of sharp-edged gills like most mushrooms, easy to recognize once you've seen it. We're having a good year for Chanterelles to make up for last year that was wet but almost Chanterelle-less. My organic farmer friend Jacob has been collecting bushels of them to sell at the local Farmers' Market for $15/pound. I have mixed feelings about that. These delicious wild mushrooms are one time that us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2006)

File.Name: 6114f_6112-071.jpg
Notes:
To see the world in a grain of sand, heaven in a wild flower,
to hold infinity in the palm of your hand and eternity in an hour.
- William Blake

My friend Aidan is holding this small white Russula mushroom - maybe a Short-Stemmed Russula (Russula brevipes or kin) - in a beam of late afternoon sunlight. Most of these mushrooms are fist-size or larger, said to be "edible but insipid - better kicked than picked". (Figueroa Mountain, 12 January 2006)

File.Name: 6115a_6114-030.jpg
Notes: New leaves of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha) twining up the dark bark of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree on the dry side of our garden. I took this photo in the rain. In the dark of Winter there are signs of Spring. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2006)

File.Name: 6115b_6114-014.jpg
Notes: This Brown Witch's Butter (Tremella foliacea) mushroom is growing from a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) branch in the woods. It sure looks like kelp in the ocean! Said to be edible and tasteless - no surprise, it's mostly water. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2006)
CommonNames: Brown Witch's Butter; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 6115c_6114-023.jpg
Notes: A new cluster of edible Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in the woods. I thought they were all done for the year, but here they are. The fungus that produces these mushrooms (like apple trees produce apples - mushrooms are "fruiting bodies") is also known to gardeners as Oak Root Fungus). Should I worry? Look close and you can see a deposit of white spores on some of the caps that has fallen from above. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2006)

File.Name: 6115d_6114-011.jpg
Notes: I think this elegant mushroom is a Deer Mushroom (Pluteus cervina). I found this mushroom on the ground, though it should be growing from wood. I guess it's growing from buried wood or roots. The dappled pattern around the edge of the cap is beautiful. Said to be edible but mediocre. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2006)

File.Name: 6115e_6114-006.jpg
Notes: Here's why Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms are fetching $15 per pound at the farmers' market and more in local restaurants - they're hard to spot in the woods under the A new cluster of edible Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in the woods. I thought they were all done for the year, but here they are. The fungus that produces these mushrooms (like apple trees produce apples - mushrooms are "fruiting bodies") is also known to gardeners as Oak Root Fungus). Should I worry? Look close and you can see a deposit of white spores on some of the caps that has fallen from above. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2006) trees. I know what I'm looking for, and I see them everywhere. It's a good year for Chanterelles. Last year we had lots of rain and few mushrooms, but we're making up for it this year. These mushrooms have become a local industry, but I don't approve of the commercial harvest. This is one time that us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings - they can find their own darn mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2006)
ScientificName: Cantharellus cibarius; Armillariella mellea; Oak Root Fungus
associatedTaxa: Cantharellus cibarius; Armillariella mellea; Oak Root Fungus

File.Name: 6115f_6113-141.jpg
Notes: Yum - delicious Chanterelles (Cantharellus cibarius) from the canyon for dinner! We always "dry saute" these mushrooms to concentrate the flavor. Clean them with a knife instead of water - they're like sponges. Then slice them into a hot dry pan, and let them stew in their own juices to concentrate the flavor until almost dry. Maybe add a bit of fresh pepper and garlic and a splash of olive oil or butter. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2006)

File.Name: 6116a_6114-028.jpg
Notes: First true leaf of a seedling Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius). All plants begin with one or two "seed leaves" or cotyledons that are usually different from later leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2006)

File.Name: 6116b_6106-018.jpg
Notes: New flower bud of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in evening light is almost ready to open. This is growing in a chaparral area near Lake Cachuma that burned a few years ago. (Santa Ynez Valley, 12 January 2006)

File.Name: 6117a_6116-042.jpg
Notes:
Male Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) taking wing in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our porch. These pretty little birds come south to our house for the winter. They never stop moving in the oak trees! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2006)

I'm inspired by my Mom's Christmas cards and Sergey Yeliseev's continuing bird collection at
   http://www.fotolog.com/yeliseev

I'm learning. I'll get better at bird photos. I need to get up earlier and find a better tripod.

File.Name: 6117b_6116-048.jpg
Notes: Male Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) trying to hide in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our porch. I'm sure these pretty little birds know when I'm trying to focus my camera. They don't like it! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2006)

File.Name: 6117c_6116-041.jpg
Notes: Male Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) about to take wing in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our porch. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2006)

File.Name: 6117d_6116-049.jpg
Notes: Male Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) shivering in the shade on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. These pretty birds rarely stop moving even on an icy morning, hard to get a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2006)

File.Name: 6118a_6116-059.jpg
Notes: Female Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. It's remarkable to think how BIG one tree is for these little birds! I was lucky to catch this bird at rest - they are usually in constant motion. This female has more gray colors than the male. Compare my photos yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2006)

File.Name: 6118b_6116-052.jpg
Notes: Female Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) taking wing in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our porch. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2006)

File.Name: 6118c_6116-062.jpg
Notes: Female Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) looking for caterpillars on the leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. It seems to be hanging from a branch and fluttering its wings as it explores the leaves. Click [Previous] to compare the male. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2006)

File.Name: 6119a_6116-033.jpg
Notes: Tiny male Ruby-crowned Kinglet (Regulus calendula) - not much bigger than a hummingbird - standing on a leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree like he's surfing through the foliage looking for action. These nervous little birds are always on the move, hopping from branch to branch in the oaks. Females don't have the ruby-colored crown. Males can hide it or make it stand up like a little crest. The "incomplete" white eye-ring is distinctive. We are "South" for these winter-visitor birds, and they disappear for the summer. They look like little Flycatchers, but they have a distinctive way of fluttering in front of the oak leaves looking for prey. I'm still trying to get that photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2006)

File.Name: 6119b_6116-069.jpg
Notes: Acrobatic little Ruby-crowned Kinglet (Regulus calendula) looking for breakfask in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. You can see a hint of red color on its head. Compare my [Next] and [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2006)

File.Name: 6119c_6116-070.jpg
Notes: Tiny male Ruby-crowned Kinglet (Regulus calendula) showing its ruby-red crest but keeping it down. These little insectivore birds are constantly moving through the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. Look at all the chunks missing from those oak leaves. We'd lose our trees to the caterpillars without these active little hunters doing their work. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2006)

File.Name: 6120a_6120-214.jpg
Notes: New flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) as open as it ever gets. These flowers always face the earth, so it's easy to miss them as you walk past. I was laying on the ground and looking up to get this photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 20 January 2006)

File.Name: 6120b_6119-140.jpg
Notes: Look in the upper-left corner - the Dragonfly (order Odonata) is resting on the sandstone next to the strange insect-like Native American Chumash pictograph at a hidden site in the mountains. I felt a chill when I noticed the dragonfly. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2006)

File.Name: 6120c_6119-124.jpg
Notes: The old Native American Chumash rock art in our mountains is disappearing as the elements take their toll, like a faded newspaper clipping photo of a former lover we try to remember. What was she dreaming way back then? (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2006)

File.Name: 6120d_6119-131.jpg
Notes: Our mountains are haunted by these old Native American Chumash rock art images that I find here and there in secluded places in the canyons. I don't know what they mean, but they speak to me, like a whisper from the past I don't quite understand. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2006)

File.Name: 6120e_6119-129.jpg
Notes: Native American Chumash rock art in our mountains. The ancient pictographs are slowly disappearing due to wind and rain and people and time. It's important not to touch the rock or even breath on it if you find one of these sites. Even the green moss and lichens on the sandstone take a toll. There are at least a dozen of these "painted caves" in our mountains, mostly hidden in remote places like this one. I think of this as the Chumash TV antenna. What channel were they watching before the world changed? (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2006)

File.Name: 6121a_6121-016.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful but deadly "Destroying Angel" (Amanita ocreata or kin) mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the canyon. Mostly white cap and gills, though this mushroom has some tan/flesh colors. The cap is not "striate" with lines from the gills underneath. You can see the ragged edge of the cap that matches the ring on the stem - both are the remains of the "partial veil" membrane that once covered the gills and tore apart as the cap expanded. You can also see the sacklike "volva" or "death cup" that sheaths the base of the stalk like a broken eggshell - that's the "universal veil" that originally enclosed the mushroom like an egg. Some Amanitas keep remnants of this veil on the cap, but not this one. This lethal-looking mushroom is deadly poisonous, but it is elegant and beautiful. It's not dangerous to handle or photograph! (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2006)

Did you notice the little fly (order Diptera) on the cap? I tried to get closer and scared it away.
ScientificName: Amanita ocreata; Quercus agrifolia; Amanitas; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Amanita ocreata; Quercus agrifolia; Amanitas; Diptera

File.Name: 6121b_6121-012.jpg
Notes: I will always stop to get another photo of the brilliant red Russula emetica (or kin) mushrooms in the woods. Mushrooms are the fruiting bodies of fungi, like apples on an apple tree. We're eat the apples and spit out the seeds, which makes sense as an adaptation. Mushrooms just drop microscopic spores, so why are they so surprising and beautiful? (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2006)

File.Name: 6121c_6121-002.jpg
Notes: I guess this little white mushroom is a Waxy Cap (family Hygrophoraceae) because it has a "waxy" feel when I smush a piece between my fingers - but I really don't know, or care. The cap is not slimy, and the white gills are "adnexed" - narrowly attached to the stem. It's growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2006)

File.Name: 6121d_6121-019.jpg
Notes: Tiny Mycena acicula mushroom growing from the leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. The spines on the oak leaves in the photo are 1 mm or less. This is the smallest mushroom I know by name, but it has substantial beauty. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2006)

File.Name: 6121e_6121-010.jpg
Notes: This Zeller's Bolete (Boletus zelleri) mushroom has a brilliant color combo - smoky "fuscous" cap with a frosted "glaucous" look, bright yellow pores (no gills), and deep red stem. This pretty 'shroom is growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. Said to be edible, but I'd rather find them along my trail. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2006)

File.Name: 6121f_6121-024.jpg
Notes: New flower of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) in the canyon with backlight. It has red sepals, small white petals, and dark male stamens hugging the female pistils. The red dots on the ovary above the sepals are gland-tipped hairs. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2006)

File.Name: 6122a_6122-034.jpg
Notes:
Canyon Wren (Catherpes mexicanus) singing its wonderful song of cascading falling and slowing notes. I got this photo right at the end of a song-sequence when it holds with a hissing sound. Look on the Web and you might find a recording. This wren has been "jeeting" around our house, but today was the first time I heard it break into song. It's the song of the mountains. I hope it nests here. Its starting to feel like spring! (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2006)

More Canyon Wren photos tomorrow!

File.Name: 6122b_6122-037.jpg
Notes: California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly "puddling" on damp soil - taking up moisture with its proboscis - look close on the left and see my [Previous] two photo. It mostly kept its wings folded together, but I got a glimpse of the good stuff. It looks pretty ragged. This butterfly is a veteran rather than a fledgling. Adults emerge in mid-summer, fly through fall, somehow survive winter, and fly again the following spring to mate and continue the cycle. Seems like a hard life for such a delicate creature, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2006)

File.Name: 6122c_6122-039.jpg
Notes: California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly "puddling" on damp soil - taking up moisture with its proboscis - look close on the left and see my [Previous] photo. I saw this critter land, but I don't think I'd notice it on the ground walking past. The "cryptic" pattern of it's underwings reminds me of the "marbling" on the inside covers of old Victorian books. This is the first butterfly I've seen at home this year. It let me crawl close. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2006)

File.Name: 6122d_6122-041.jpg
Notes: Looking close at a California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly "puddling" on damp soil - taking up moisture with its proboscis. I couldn't move the new sprouts without disturbing it. This is the first butterfly I've seen at home this year. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2006)

File.Name: 6122e_6122-043.jpg
Notes: One-half second in the life of these tiny Dance Flies and Fungus Gnats (etc.) that dance in the afternoon sunbeams in the woods. When the light is right, they get this glow. You can follow the tracks of their life! The exposure is 1/2 second at f/11, leaning against a tree with my monopod instead of a real tripod. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2006)

File.Name: 6122f_6121-021.jpg
Notes: After seeing fellow flogger Yeliseev's photo of his thermometer at 30+ degrees BELOW zero in Moscow, I'm almost embarrassed to admit we still have Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweeds with seeds (even a few flowers) in the garden. That's a 60 degree difference! It's been freezing every night. There are hints of spring here in the Santa Barbara mountains, but we need more winter to have a fabulous summer. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2006)

File.Name: 6123a_6122-026.jpg
Notes:
Perky little Canyon Wren (Catherpes mexicanus) perched on a pile of old lumber near the garden. These birds stay close to the ground and like to explore the dark cracks in our piles of stuff. You can see all the distinctive characteristics you see in a book - long bill, short tail, rufous colors that contrast with white breast and throat. I can't show how they move, but that's the real distinguishing feature you have to see to learn. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2006)

This wren is singing. I usually hear these birds rather than see them. They have a wonderful song of descending clear musical notes that echoes in the canyons. There are some recordings at:
   http://www.naturesongs.com/trogpeuc.html

File.Name: 6123b_6122-030.jpg
Notes: Little Canyon Wren (Catherpes mexicanus) perched on a rusty yellow wheelbarrow near the garden - singing its song! The highlights are blown out in this photo, but the attitude is perfect. His wonderful song is a cascade of falling and slowing notes that is a signature part of our mountain canyons. It feels like spring. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2006)

File.Name: 6123c_6122-029.jpg
Notes: Little Canyon Wren (Catherpes mexicanus) perched on a rusty yellow wheelbarrow near the garden, watching my every move. I realize that depth of field is a problem even with long lenses like my Tamron 200-500 mm. I'm still learning how to use my new long lens. There are tough tradeoffs between f/stop (for depth of field), and shutter speed (to freeze motion and tripod jitters), and ISO speed (to avoid the digital "noise" you can see in the dark background @ ISO 800). (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2006)

File.Name: 6123d_6122-025.jpg
Notes: Little Canyon Wren (Catherpes mexicanus) perched on a 4x4 fence post near the garden. It gives a scale for this pretty little bird. A "4x4" is really 3-1/2 inches on a side - not quite 9 cm. My book says the bird is 5-3/4 inches long from tip of bill to tail - almost 15 cm - and it weighs about 10.5 grams. There's a lot of life in that small package! (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2006)

File.Name: 6124a_6121-001.jpg
Notes:
Rufous-sided Towhee (Pipilo erythrophthalmus?) - with breakfast in its beak. It's perched in a native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) bramble near the garden. These pretty robin-sized birds like to scratch around in the fallen oak leaves. They always disappear into the brambles when I bring my camera out! It's hard to get a sharp photo in the morning shade at this dark time of year. I'll keep trying. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2006)

My Mom put this pretty bird on one of her Christmas cards:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=12101769

File.Name: 6124b_6122-028.jpg
Notes: Here is the first Dark-eyed Junco (Junco hyemalis oreganus) - aka "Oregon Junco" - that I've seen for a while. I think of these birds as always here, but I'm wrong. This is the first one I've seen for at least for at least a month. It's always easy to remember the FIRST time; but it's hard to remember the LAST time you saw something you take for granted! Blurry in the morning shade. I'll do better. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2006)

File.Name: 6125a_3628-031.jpg
Notes:
No time for new photos so far this week, so here's one from deep in my "flog-ready" folder:

This "baby ducky" joined us at our secret swimming hole. We finally left because it wouldn`t go away and we were worried what Mom would think. I'm pretty sure it`s a Pied-billed Grebe (Podilymbus podiceps). "Pied" means "having sections or patches colored differently and usually brightly" which fits. It's blowing bubbles! This little bird could dive like a seal! (Santa Ynez River, 28 June 2003)

I have more photos of our close encounter at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?photo_id=287265
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?photo_id=287304
  
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?photo_id=8890805

File.Name: 6126a_6126-075.jpg
Notes: Elegant new flower of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the woods. The female pistil is held high and out of the way of the two ripe male stamens to avoid self-pollination. Look close - it sure looks like there's a bit of yellow pollen on the lower lip of the pistil, so maybe there's already new life on the way. As the flower matures, the pistil will drop down and then move to the side. Note how the flower in the back has a different outline. Maybe this is a mechanism to self-pollinate if there are no takers? I need time to watch these flowers more closely. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2006)

File.Name: 6126b_6126-079.jpg
Notes: Pretty new flower of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the woods. It's a long red/magenta flower with exerted stamens and pistil, and I've seen hummingbirds work the flowers. But it sure looks like it's built for Bumblebees (Bombus). The lower petal looks like a landing platform for a heavy insect that would lever the stamens and pistil down to its back. I'll try to watch these pretty flowers to see who really visits. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2006)

File.Name: 6126c_6126-082.jpg
Notes: First flower I've seen this year of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora). See [Previous] photo for more info. It has tiny yellow-green petals below that look inviting, but the male stamens are hidden in the red part of the flower above, and the female pistil is exerted at the tip of the flower. I'm not sure how pollination happens. I've seen this listed as a hummingbird flower, and it has that look, but the whole plant is only a few inches high. I need to spend a morning sitting and watching these flowers to see who visits. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2006)

File.Name: 6126d_6126-081.jpg
Notes: Exotic-looking new red leaves of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the chaparral. This plant comes up fast like a mushroom and then disappears - there is a fungal connection. It's a "scroph" related to Penstemon and Paintbrush (Castilleja)in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. It's a root-parasite of some chaparral shrubs, probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in this place, though I also find it under other shrubs. It has some green, but the foliage is mostly red like this. I reckon it's a three-way relationship between plant-fungus-shrub in which the invisible fungus partner makes the connection between herb and shrub. I wonder if every partner benefits? Sometimes I wonder if the whole forest is connected by mycelial strands into one thing. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2006)
CommonNames: Indian Warrior; Penstemon; Paintbrush; Figwort; Chamise
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Castilleja; Scrophulariaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Castilleja; Scrophulariaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum

File.Name: 6127a_6127-126.jpg
Notes:
The flowers of this native Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) near Lake Cachuma are unusually blue/lilac colored. The flowers are usually white. The spring flowers cover the shrubs - sometimes whole hillsides - with a white bloom like snow. The individual flowers show even more colors up close - bright yellow stamens and that orange ripe pistil on top. I like the five-pointed star geometry of the flower buds. (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 January 2006)

Today's photos are new flowers in the mountains. I keep looking for insect pollinators, but they are scarce - or really tiny.

File.Name: 6127b_6126-071.jpg
Notes: New male flowers of native Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or California Manroot (Marah fabaceus) growing on a recently burned area in the mountains. It's sometimes called "manroot" because it grows from a huge underground tuber that can get child size. This vine uses springy tendrils to clamber over native plants and then dies back to the root in winter. It's in the same family (Cucurbitaceae) as cucumbers, melons, squash, and gourds, but you would never think of eating the spiny fruit. The flowers have a frosted but immature look. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2006)

File.Name: 6127c_6126-080.jpg
Notes:
Small but charming new flowers of Common Rush-Rose (Helianthemum scoparium) growing on a bare rocky place in the chaparral that was cleared for a firebreak a few years ago. It's a little bushy perennial that only grows a foot or so high. It's one of those common plants we step over without noticing as we hike along. There is a glandular endemic species on the Channel Islands (H. greenei) that I probably walked right over without noticing! It's interesting that this little native in the Rockrose (Cistaceae) family is related to the garden Rockrose (Cistus sp.) that persist around old homesteads and sometimes naturalize in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2006)

FYI - the species name scoparium is from the Latin scopae, "a broom". The root is used for many plants that lose most of their leaves in summer and become bare and broom-like. Another native "broom plant" is Deerweed (Lotus scoparius). The aggresive invasive shrub Scotch Broom (Cytisus scoparius) is a pest in Oregon. I studied Latin for two years in junior high school - I regreted it then, but not now. The latin names are helpful and sometimes funny!
Locality: Cytisus scoparius) is a pest in Oregon. I studied Latin for two years in junior high school - I regreted it then
CommonNames: Common Rush-Rose; Rockrose; Rockrose; Deerweed; Scotch Broom
ScientificName: Helianthemum scoparium; H. greenei; Cistaceae; Cistus; scoparium; scopae; Lotus scoparius; Cytisus scoparius
associatedTaxa: Helianthemum scoparium; H. greenei; Cistaceae; Cistus; scoparium; scopae; Lotus scoparius; Cytisus scoparius

File.Name: 6127d_6126-089.jpg
Notes: New flower of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the chaparral catching late afternoon light. This flower looks like a tulip - it's pushed away it's sepals, but it's not quite open for business. This native shrub grows more than head high and has pretty poppy flowers most of the year. We've had no luck getting it to grow in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2006)

File.Name: 6128a_6126-077.jpg
Notes: Smaller-than-pinhead-size flies (order Diptera) perched on the edge of a petal of a new flower of Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata). I've been puzzled finding new flowers before pollinators, but I think the answer is that the early pollinators are really tiny. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2006)

File.Name: 6128b_6128-001.jpg
Notes: Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) tending aphids on a branch of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus). There are a dozen of these dark ant/aphid colonies visible in the shrub outside our bathroom window. Notice the two ants on the right touching antennae. I've seen these ant colonies on other Ceanothus shrubs. The ants look and smell like our usual "piss-ants" - the California Harvester Ant (Pogonomyrmex californicus or kin). Are all the ants on this shrub part of one colony? Where's the queen? (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2006)
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Pogonomyrmex californicus
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Pogonomyrmex californicus

File.Name: 6128c_6128-004.jpg
Notes:
Here is an intimate moment between Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) and Aphid (family Aphididae in the order Hemiptera) on a branch of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus). The ants look and smell like our usual "piss-ants" - the California Harvester Ant (Pogonomyrmex californicus or kin).

The fancy names don't matter. Look how the ant is massaging the aphid with its antennae! Is it "milking" the aphid for honeydew? (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2006)

Nano, I know it's not quite sharp, but this is an action shot. Hard to hold steady with these #$%@ ants crawling all over me!
CommonNames: Ant; Aphid; Greenbark Ceanothus; California Harvester Ant
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Pogonomyrmex californicus
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Pogonomyrmex californicus

File.Name: 6128d_6128-019.jpg
Notes: Skeletonized husk of a Tomatillo (Physalis ixocarpa) fruit in the compost pile. You can see the fruit inside the lace. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2006)

File.Name: 6128e_6128-016.jpg
Notes: Strange Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum or kin) mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in a disturbed place. Sometime if you touch one of these, it gives off a puff of spores. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2006)

File.Name: 6128f_6128-013.jpg
Notes: Mama Nature surely has a sense of humor! Here's an Elfin Saddle aka Hooded False Morel (Gyromita infula) mushroom I found in the woods today. I resisted the temptation to draw eyes on the cap. This mushroom is POISONOUS - it contains MMH (MonoMethylHydrazine) which is carcinogenic and used as rocket fuel. It's so interesting that mushrooms contain such powerful compounds that effect body and mind. I guess it's a reminder that all life on Earth has common heritage. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2006)

File.Name: 6129a_6129-030.jpg
Notes: These ripe flower buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus Ovata) look like they're ready to burst. The waxy flowers wait fully-formed for months before opening, but their time is close. You know the feeling! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2006)

File.Name: 6129b_6129-026.jpg
Notes: New fruit of native Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca). (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2006)

File.Name: 6129c_6129-023.jpg
Notes:
New mushroom-like plant of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) growing under Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca). It's a root-parasite that gets support from its host plant. It flowers early and then disappears until next year. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2006)

I have more photos and info at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=12275886

File.Name: 6129d_6129-020.jpg
Notes: I think this is a honey-colored Suillus psuedobrevipes mushroom in the woods growing under Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) and Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) and not far from a native Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri). I almost walked over it. See my [Previous] photo for more info. I'm always puzzled by mushrooms. They are the fruiting bodies of fungi in the ground. Why do they bother with bright colors or camouflage colors like this just to drop their spores? (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2006)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Big-berry Manzanita; Coulter Pines
ScientificName: Suillus psuedobrevipes; Quercus berberidifolia; Arctostaphylos glauca; Pinus coulteri
associatedTaxa: Suillus psuedobrevipes; Quercus berberidifolia; Arctostaphylos glauca; Pinus coulteri

File.Name: 6129e_6129-021.jpg
Notes: Looking under the hood of an interesting mushroom in the woods. I guess this is Suillus psuedobrevipes, but compare S. brevipes. I found it growing under Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) and Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca), but there were native Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri) a stone-throw away. This mushroom has pores rather than gills, and a short stem. The brown cap is viscid/slimy and the small pores are yellow. I didn't notice a ring, but I can see white cottony remnants around the edge of the cap, so I think it had a partial veil when young. Probably edible but not incredible. Caveat emptor. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2006)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Big-berry Manzanita; Coulter Pines
ScientificName: Suillus psuedobrevipes; S. brevipes; Quercus berberidifolia; Arctostaphylos glauca; Pinus coulteri
associatedTaxa: Suillus psuedobrevipes; S. brevipes; Quercus berberidifolia; Arctostaphylos glauca; Pinus coulteri

File.Name: 6129f_6129-025.jpg
Notes: Native succulent Lanceleaf Liveforever aka Rock Lettuce (Dudleya lanceolata or kin) growing from a bed of moss. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2006)

File.Name: 6130a_6127-119.jpg
Notes:
Gnarly flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) starting to drop petals after being pollinated, and giving a glimpse inside. This plant in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family has many separate floral parts, so it's considered "primative" in the evolution of flowering plants. Compare my [Previous] photo. Post-modern or old-and-in-the-way, here they are in the woods - both designs still work! (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 January 2006)

It's hard to see into these shy downward-pointing flowers before they fall apart. Compare my recent photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=12210145

File.Name: 6130b_6127-123.jpg
Notes: Looking close at a flower of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora). With the sun behind, you can see through its petals and spot two round male stamens and the red-tipped female pistil exerted from the end of the flower. (It really has four stamens - they must be stacked up in pairs.) This flower in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family is considered "advanced" in the evolution of flowering plants because it has relatively few floral parts which are fused together and irregularly shaped. Compare my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2006)

File.Name: 6130c_6129-035.jpg
Notes: Here is the first Shooting Star (Dodecatheon clevelandii in the Primulaceae) wildflower I've seen near home this year. I know it's not quite sharp - F/11 @ 1/30 @ ISO 200 in the shade with a breeze - but seeing these new flowers is like meeting a good friend after a year apart. Here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2006)

File.Name: 6131a_6126-084.jpg
Notes: Tempting-looking cluster of Yellow-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus xanthodermus) mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. The unpleasant phenol-disinfectant smell (and worse taste) with the yellow-staining cap are signs that it's poisonous. The usual commercial supermarket mushrooms (Agaricus bisporus) and some of their wild kin are delicious and choice. They all have dark gills, a ring, and no cup or "volva" at the base. I find the whole Agaricus genus very confusing and usually avoid eating them. But the Meadow Mushrooms (Agaricus campestris) are so sweet eaten raw in the woods... they look like the commercial mushrooms - just like one of those hard supermarket tomatoes looks like a living ripe tomato still on the vine in the garden and warm from the sun - no comparison at all! Caveat emptor. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2006)
CommonNames: Yellow-Staining Agaricus; Coast Live Oak; Meadow Mushrooms
ScientificName: Agaricus xanthodermus; Quercus agrifolia; Agaricus bisporus; Agaricus; Agaricus campestris
associatedTaxa: Agaricus xanthodermus; Quercus agrifolia; Agaricus bisporus; Agaricus; Agaricus campestris

File.Name: 6131b_6126-087.jpg
Notes: The most obvious signal that this Yellow-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus xanthodermus) mushroom is poisonous is the "phenolic" disinfectant-like small. The taste is worse. It has a "double-ring" with thick patches on the underside while the inner partial veil is still attached covering the gills as in the photo. The edge of the cap and the base of the stem usually turn bright yellow after you rub them, like this. Looks good - tastes bad! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2006)

File.Name: 6201a_6128-007.jpg
Notes:
Disturbing colony of ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) - I guess they are common California Harvester Ants (Pogonomyrmex californicus or kin) - tending aphids (family Aphididae in the order Hemiptera) on a branch of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus). The ants and aphids have been in a dogpile all winter outside our window. They're starting to wake up and move around. Disturbing! (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2006)

You have to be really careful using the word "formication" - said properly, it means "the sensation that insects or snakes are crawling over the skin". Yipes, I'm formicating!
CommonNames: California Harvester Ants; aphids; Greenbark Ceanothus
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Pogonomyrmex californicus; Aphididae; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Pogonomyrmex californicus; Aphididae; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus

File.Name: 6202a_6202-088.jpg
Notes:
New flowers of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanun xanti) appearing early in a recently burned area in the mountains. These flowers are "shakers" - the yellow male pollen-bearing stamens are curled into tubes with loose pollen inside. They clasp the female style tightly so that pollen falls out when an insect pollinator jiggles the flower, like salt from a saltcellar. It was very windy today - that works too, also photographers trying to get too close. Many familiar garden plants in the Nightshade (Solanaceae) family have the same design and trick - tomatoes, chiles, eggplant, potatoes, and many more. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2006)

Today is Groundhog Day - one of the "cross-quarter days" midway between solstice and equinox. We don't have groundhogs in California, but if any gopher stuck his head out of the ground this week, I'm sure he saw his shadow because it's been sunny and clear and dry. By tradition, this means we'll have six more weeks of winter. Cool - we really need the rain this year. But isn't this backwards? Is there any reasonable way to explain why sunshine means more winter, while clouds mean that spring is here? Shouldn't it be just the opposite?

See my stumper (and answer) at: http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/05feb99.html

File.Name: 6203a_6126-088.jpg
Notes:
Is this little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) praying, or preying, or threatening? I think it's trying to scare me away. I saw it drop from the Chaparral Nightshade (Solanun xanti) flower in the [Previous] photo when I disturbed it. It kept this threatening "cruciform" pose on an old flower stem even when I got very close in the wind. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2006)

There are many small Orb-Weaver (Araneidae) and Sheet Web (Linyphiidae) spiders in the woods trapping the tiny flies that dance in the sunbeams. But this is the first hunting spider I've seen for a while. Interesting that these Crab Spiders are also one of the last of our insect predators. Compare my September 9, 2005 photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=11197694
Locality: Linyphiidae) spiders in the woods trapping the tiny flies that dance in the sunbeams. But this is the first hunting spider I've seen for a while. Interesting that these Crab Spiders are also one of the last of our insect predators. Compare my September 9

File.Name: 6204a_6204-045.jpg
Notes:
Mourning Cloak (Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly resting in the canyon near the creek, spread out like it wants to be a museum specimen. I rarely see them here in the mountains. They emerge in mid-summer and somehow survive winter to mate in early spring - now. Maybe they then enter a "resting stage" until next fall? Is that why I don't often see them?

D'oh - I was reading about rainbows, halos, and glories and I missed fotolog midnight!
Locality: Nymphalis antiopa) butterfly resting in the canyon near the creek

File.Name: 6204b_6204-023.jpg
Notes:
Very strange bows! I usually lean close to look a photo, but it might help to lean back for this one. It's common to see a "ring around the sun" - sometimes you can even see rainbow colors. This double ring has TWO bows with different curves intersecting at the top and diverging on each side with different radii like a pair of crocodile jaws. The sun is straight-down below the trees. Both halos are red on the inside and blue on the outside. The high clouds were moving overhead south-to-north - the breeze at home was moving north-to-south - and there were interesting bows all the way until it passed. See my [Previous] photo. There were no more halos or bows after this cloud passed, so I went looking for mushrooms. I reckon it's something about ice crystals? (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2006)

I have Robert Greenler's classic "Rainbows, Halos, and Glories" book sitting on my desk in front of me. It's on my must-read list. I didn't find an easy explanation just looking at the photos, and the details about ice-crystal refraction effects are daunting. I'm happy to notice another stumper.

File.Name: 6204c_6204-035.jpg
Notes: Strange bow! Looking north at Fremont Ridge with the sun more than 90 degrees behind me. Notice the sun halo curving UP at the top of the photo. I saw faint rainbow colors, but they didn't come out in the photo. There are bright spots scattered along the halo, though maybe that was just the clouds. This is probably the first time I ever wanted a fisheye lens! The strangest thing is that the halo didn't circle the sun - it seemed to wrap around and intersect the sun, like a ring with the sun as the signet jewel. This is the same cloud as my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2006)

File.Name: 6204d_6204-040.jpg
Notes: Strange little grape-sized Cup Fungus (maybe Peziza sylvestris or kin?) growing up through the fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the canyon. The light-colored warts on the outside suggest that it originally had a white covering before it opened. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2006)

File.Name: 6204e_6204-046.jpg
Notes: A new Cortinarius mushroom poking up in a "mushrump" of fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the woods. This mushroom has violet colors that don't show in the photo - maybe Cortinarius varius or kin. It's been so dry and warm this month that new mushrooms are scarce and some spring flowers are appearing early. Every year is different. Our natives in all phyla are the survivors that can best handle the changes over decades and centuries and millenia and more... (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2006)
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Cortinarius varius; phyla
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Cortinarius varius; phyla

File.Name: 6204f_6203-132.jpg
Notes: I think this is the same kind of little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) as in the [Previous] photo, though I took this photo miles away in the valley rather than on the mountan ridge. The spider is trying to hide from me behind a flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica). (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 February 2006)

File.Name: 6205a_6205-058.jpg
Notes: I played peek-a-boo with this new Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) looking over the edge of a sandstone rock outcrop in the woods. "Them there eyes" - It sure looks like it has eyelashes! It felt like spring today. I guess this cold-blooded critter is living proof. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2006)

File.Name: 6205b_6205-060.jpg
Notes: Little Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly resting on a leaf of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the canyon. It's a Hairstreak (subfamily Theclinae) butterfly without a tail, but it still rubs its hindwings together just like its tailed cousins. It looks brown from a distance, but in the photo it looks more like a pointillist painting by Georges Seurat - many color swatches that combine to an interesting brown. My books say it's often the first non-hibernating butterfly to appear at the first sign of spring. Sounds right. It feels like spring today. I also saw a male California Dogface (Zerene eurydice) and a little Blue flutter by without stopping to pose. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2006)
CommonNames: Brown Elfin; Miner's Lettuce; Hairstreak; California Dogface; Blue
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; Claytonia perfoliata; Theclinae; pointillist; Zerene eurydice
associatedTaxa: Callophrys augustinus; Claytonia perfoliata; Theclinae; pointillist; Zerene eurydice

File.Name: 6205c_6205-053.jpg
Notes: Little Bee Fly (order Diptera, family Bombyliidae) and shadow, on a new leaf of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the canyon. Prodigious proboscis! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2006)

File.Name: 6205d_6205-056.jpg
Notes: A micro-forest of tiny moss (Bryophyta) sporophytes in the canyon. Mosses are so common and so strange up close. Open your botany textbook to see how strange they really are! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2006)

File.Name: 6206a_6204-004.jpg
Notes:
This little Wrentit (Chamaea fasciata) bird caught breakfast in the California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) bramble at home. I think that's a tasty little immature Cricket (order Orthoptera) in its beak. These little birds fly through the brambles like seals swim in the kelp! I hear them more than I see them - rustling in the brush and making their "twip twip twip tidrtidrtidrdrdr" song - three (or four) long notes, then a run-together fast repeat and trill. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2006)

Photographing these little birds - tele-macro! - is hard. I have so much respect for amaw and yeliseev who do it every day:
   http://www.fotolog.com/amaw
   http://www.fotolog.com/yeliseev/

File.Name: 6206b_6204-009.jpg
Notes: Look close - this is a stumper photo! I saw our first House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bird in the California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) bramble at home. My autofocus telephoto got it wrong. Can you see the little wren hiding behind the blackberry leaves with head down and tail high? Every summer these perfect little wrens nest in our birdhouses and reward us with morning song. I hope for many more photos. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2006)

File.Name: 6206c_6204-037.jpg
Notes: Tiny Mycena acicula mushroom in a sunbeam casting a shadow on a coin-size fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. It's my constant stumper why these tiny mushrooms that just drop spores - the fruiting bodies of hidden fungi - are so darned beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2006)

File.Name: 6207a_6204-011.jpg
Notes: Look close! This pretty little Canyon Wren (Catherpes mexicanus) has a bit of spider web trailing from its bill. (Look close.) It's resting on an old fiberglass shower stall behind our house. These wrens love exploring our junk piles - hunting through old lumber, under the deck, behind the wall, all the dark secret places - looking for spiders and other bugs, I reckon. Glad to oblige! We have at least three of these wrens around the house singing and chitting. I've heard them in the canyon before, but this is something new. I hope they nest here. We "belled" our cat, and we're trying to keep her inside for the mice. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2006)

File.Name: 6207b_6204-010.jpg
Notes: I've been trying to photograph birds in the woods, but this Rufous-sided Towhee (Pipilo erythrophthalmus) hiding in the California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) bramble is watching my every move with intense red eyes. It's a stumper - why are the little birds so wary? They should know me by now. I'm packing my camera, not a gun! (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2006)

File.Name: 6207c_6204-001.jpg
Notes: Another little Wrentit (Chamaea fasciata) bird on a branch of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) with flowers. I watched the bird flutter in the air for a moment and take an unripe berry, but I missed the shot. Compare my [Previous] photo. These birds like a balanced diet! (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2006)

File.Name: 6208a_6205-052.jpg
Notes: Flowers of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the woods. It's a woodland native in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family related to the weedy yellow mustards that cover the California hills. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2006)

File.Name: 6208b_6205-067.jpg
Notes: The spider and the fly... This is a near-silhouette in the shade under the eave with the bright sky behind. The spider is a little Orb-Weaver (Araneidae), and the unfortunate fly is one of those tiny flies that dance in the sunbeams. Life goes on. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2006)

File.Name: 6208c_6205-050.jpg
Notes: Little Wrentit (Chamaea fasciata) bird in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) behind our house. It looks angry! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2006)

File.Name: 6209a_6209-105.jpg
Notes:
The sweet-smelling white flowers of BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) are starting to cover the south-facing slopes of the Santa Ynez Mountains like snow. The mass flowering is made up of gazillions of these tiny individual white flowers that go unnoticed as we drive past at 60 mph. Up close, the combination of hooded white petals, yellow stamens, violet pistils, and the magenta fleshy gland around the base of the ovary is remarkable. We miss so much. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2006)

I have a photo of another mass flowering at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=6606020

File.Name: 6209b_6209-092.jpg
Notes: New flowers of Douglas Nightshade (Solanum douglasii) in the Nightshade (Solanaceae) family growing on a recently burned area in the mountains. I usually find it closer to the beach. We got tricked by the similar white flowers and allowed the weedy Black Nightshade (Solanum nigrum - aka S. nodiflorum or maybe S. americanum) to get established at home. *Sigh* We shouldn't take anything for granted as naturalists! (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2006)
ScientificName: Solanum douglasii; Solanaceae; Solanum nigrum; S. nodiflorum; S. americanum
associatedTaxa: Solanum douglasii; Solanaceae; Solanum nigrum; S. nodiflorum; S. americanum

File.Name: 6209c_6209-087.jpg
Notes: New male flowers of native Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or California Manroot (Marah fabaceus) growing on a recently burned area in the mountains. The female flowers that bear the spiny fruit are starting to appear lower on the stem, but I need a better photo. Male and female flowers both have a frosted look, like they're covered with little crystals. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2006)

File.Name: 6210a_6209-097.jpg
Notes: New flowers of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanun xanti) in a recently burned chaparral area in the mountains along with golden tendrils of native Dodder (probably Cuscuta californica). Dodder is a twining parasitic vine that starts as a seedling, but loses it's connection with the ground and lives by sucking the juices of other plants. Vines like this dodder, Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata), and Wild Cucumber (Marah fabaceus) are trying to take over the burn areas this year. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2006)
CommonNames: Chaparral Nightshade; Dodder; Western Morning-Glory; Wild Cucumber
ScientificName: Solanun xanti; Cuscuta californica; Calystegia purpurata; Marah fabaceus
associatedTaxa: Solanun xanti; Cuscuta californica; Calystegia purpurata; Marah fabaceus

File.Name: 6210b_6209-111.jpg
Notes: New flowers of Chaparral Lotus (Lotus grandiflorus) in the Pea Family (Fabaceae). The flowers develop the red colors as they age. Light from the setting sun helps. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2006)

File.Name: 6210c_6209-085.jpg
Notes: Looking close at new flowers of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus). It's a blue-flowered relative of the shrub in the [Previous] photo. It doesn't cover the hillsides with bloom, but there are still gazillions of tiny flowers on each shrub. The flowers have remarkable structure, folded up like little origami treasure boxes. Look close - the flower on top is full of little insects! I've been wondering why the flowers are starting to open when there are few insects in the woods. I guess there are insects - they're just very tiny and hiding! (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2006)

File.Name: 6211a_6211-030.jpg
Notes:
Looking close at a new male staminate catkin on an Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) tree by the creek. Note how each little scale on the catkin holds a forked Y-shaped filament with two pollen-bearing anthers. Willows look like "wind-pollinated" plants, but they do produce nectar to attract insects. I saw no insect activity on these flowers today. The willow trees still have no leaves. I saw the first flowers on top a week ago. These are the first flowers I can reach. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2006)

We've had a strange week of perfect warm weather - highs in the 80s and frost in the morning for a 50 degree temperature swing! This should be our rainy season, but there are already fires down south. Interesting. I'm trying not to think about summer.

File.Name: 6211b_6211-017.jpg
Notes: New male staminate catkin on an Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) tree by the creek. The unopened stamens look like little yellow lollipops. Notice how the stamens come in pairs from little scales on the catkin. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2006)

File.Name: 6211c_6211-039.jpg
Notes: Unknown little Wasp (order Hymenoptera) stepping on new flowers of a small Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) near the creek. My first thought was Sawfly (Tenthredinidae). Insects are starting to appear in the woods after a week of perfect warm weather. It's hard to complain about shorts and t-shirt weather in February, but its very dry. I'm trying not to think about summer. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2006)

File.Name: 6211d_6211-007.jpg
Notes: Unusual tiny Wasp (order Hymenoptera) perched on a small vegetative leaf of Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) growing under an Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) tree by the creek. I reckon its a female - the long rigid oviposter is probably used to insert eggs into wood. Maybe she is a Horntail Wasp (family Siricidae)? (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2006)
CommonNames: Wasp; Miner's Lettuce; Arroyo Willow; Horntail Wasp
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Claytonia perfoliata; Salix lasiolepis; Siricidae
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Claytonia perfoliata; Salix lasiolepis; Siricidae

File.Name: 6211e_6211-014.jpg
Notes: Little Bee Fly (order Diptera, family Bombyliidae) on a new leaf of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the canyon. The sun is behind me, but the reflection from the round leaf - like a little solar dish - is perfect backlight to show its body under the coat of dense fur. That long proboscis-"tongue" is permanent. I saw them working the little urn-like Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa) flowers today. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2006)
CommonNames: Bee Fly; Miner's Lettuce; Eastwood Manzanita
ScientificName: Diptera; Bombyliidae; Claytonia perfoliata; Arctostaphylos glandulosa
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Bombyliidae; Claytonia perfoliata; Arctostaphylos glandulosa

File.Name: 6211f_6211-002.jpg
Notes: Flower of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) catching a beam of sunlight in the woods - like a dancer lifting her skirt in the air to show her petticoats. The red sepals catch our eye, while the white "petticoats" are the real petals. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2006)

File.Name: 6212a_6212-040.jpg
Notes: This California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly in the new growth by the creek is a veteran. Note the chunk missing from its exposed right wing - probably a close-encounter with a bird. These butterflies overwinter as adults - through rain, snow, wind, and cold - and then emerge in spring - today - to face hungry birds and start the circle of life again. This butterfly is a hero. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2006)

File.Name: 6212b_6212-041.jpg
Notes: A butterfly's world - California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly taking water near the creek, hiding behind a Mouse-ear Chickweed (Cerastrium glomeratum seedling. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2006)

File.Name: 6212c_6212-038.jpg
Notes:
Little fly-like fly (order Diptera) using it's proboscis to suck water from a drop on a leaf of Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the canyon near the creek. There's another fly and flowers in the background. Look close to see it's actually pushing into the water drop against the surface tension. Insects are still scarse in the canyon despite the new flowers and warm weather. This patch of Miner's Lettuce near our leaky pump under the willows by the creek is a water hole for many insects in the morning. The leaves either collect or exude water drops that the insects can use. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2006)

I guess the water drops on these plants is the result of "guttation" - more info at:
   http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guttation/

File.Name: 6212d_6212-045.jpg
Notes: I can't resist getting photos of these cute furry Bee Flies (order Diptera, family Bombyliidae) whenever I can! I love the long proboscis and see-through vestiture that shows the inner-fly within. This little fly is resting on a leaf of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the canyon. I saw one of these flies poking into the Miner's Lettuce flowers today. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2006)
CommonNames: Bee Flies; Miner's Lettuce; Miner's Lettuce

File.Name: 6213a_6212-048.jpg
Notes:
The past ten days of 80+ degree weather in what should be our cold rainy season has confused some native plants like this California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia, aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) flowering in the canyon. They normally flower in late summer, but here it is with more buds ready to open. Are the flowers early or late? (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2006)

This winter weather is remarkable. One day last week we had morning frost and a high of 88 degrees Fahrenheit - almost a 60 degree temperature change. There's already been a wildfire down south.

File.Name: 6213b_6209-100.jpg
Notes: New flower of Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata) opening at a recently burned chaparral area in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2006)

File.Name: 6214a_6212-032.jpg
Notes: The recent warm weather feels like spring, and the hummers are getting frisky! I heard a loud explosive "mewk" sound and thought it was a squirrel. This female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) heard it too and got excited spreading her tail wings and looking up. I first thought she made the sound, but it was the male at the end of it's steep J-shaped mating dive overhead. She was perched at the very tip-top of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree at home. She was obviously interested, but didn't pursue. We've all had those close mating encounters. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2006)

File.Name: 6215a_6212-Brandon Mann - 100_1964.jpg
Notes: I wish I took this photo - but I didn't. My student Brandon spotted this adult Greater Roadrunner (Geococcyx californianus) bird through the window on his porch railing holding a breakfast-lizard in its beak. This is Brandon's photo. I usually spot these birds driving at 40+ mph in the mountains. I didn't know they have that brilliant red, white, and blue color patch behind the eye. Beep Beep! (Santa Ynez Valley, 12 February 2006)

File.Name: 6216a_6216-112.jpg
Notes: Male Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) perched in a Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) at school looking for tiny insects on the wing. The brilliant red and green colors come and go as he looks around. The color is iridescence not pigment - like the color in spilled gas or a CD under a light. (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 February 2006)

File.Name: 6216b_6216-110.jpg
Notes: Male Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) perched on the tip-top of a Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) at school. It was acting like a flycatcher - sitting on it's high perch looking left and right, flying off to catch some little flying critter, and returning to the same perch. Their color changes with every move. Look at the black patch under his bill and compare the [Next] photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 16 February 2006)

File.Name: 6217a_6217-148.jpg
Notes:
How big is a Hermit Thrush (Catharus guttatus)? This bird is not quite as round as a tennis ball! He/she was scratching in the oak leaves in front of our house this morning, with a block wall behind. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2006)

It was cold and drizzling when I got today's photos. This is our first rain in over a month, after unseasonably warm and dry weather. The forecast is for "likely snow" tomorrow! Maybe the mushrooms aren't all done yet.

File.Name: 6217b_6217-156.jpg
Notes: Hermit Thrush (Catharus guttatus) scratching in the oak leaves. They sing for us later in spring with the most ethereal of songs - the spirit of our forest. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2006)

File.Name: 6217c_6217-153.jpg
Notes: This male Dark-eyed Junco (Junco hyemalis oreganus) - aka "Oregon Junco" - has a black "Hood" on his head. He's munching seeds on a Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis) shrub on the edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2006)

File.Name: 6217d_6217-151.jpg
Notes: This female Dark-eyed Junco (Junco hyemalis oreganus) - aka "Oregon Junco" - has a gray "Hood" on her head, lighter than the male in the [Previous] photo. She's crunching seeds of a Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis) shrub on the edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2006)

File.Name: 6217e_6217-154.jpg
Notes: Female Dark-eyed Junco (Junco hyemalis oreganus) - aka "Oregon Junco" - looking for seeds in a Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis) shrub on the edge of our garden. Notice the California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) leaves mixed in - our garden is like a mosh between wild and domestic plants. I believe the "junco" was the medieval hangman, and the birds are named for their dark "hangman's hood". That's a tough name for these sweet little birds. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2006)
ScientificName: Junco hyemalis oreganus; Rosmarinus officinalis; Rubus ursinus
associatedTaxa: Junco hyemalis oreganus; Rosmarinus officinalis; Rubus ursinus

File.Name: 6218a_6218-006.jpg
Notes:
Male ` - facing a smorgasbord of annual weeds in the garden, mostly Chickweed (Stellaria media) and Common Bedstraw Galium aparine). (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2006)

Rain yesterday, cold all day - in the 30s, and we expect snow tonight. I'm glad winter is back in place after temperatures in the 90s last week. I got more photos, but these Junco shots want to be a series by themselves. I was about 10 feet away with my Tamron 200-500 mm lens at about 400 mm.

File.Name: 6218b_6218-007.jpg
Notes: This male Dark-eyed Junco (Junco hyemalis oreganus) - aka "Oregon Junco" - is going for the Chickweed (Stellaria media) in the garden. These non-native weedy plants have just started flowering, so it must be eating the flower buds rather than the seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2006)

File.Name: 6218c_6218-009.jpg
Notes: Male Dark-eyed Junco (Junco hyemalis oreganus) - aka "Oregon Junco" - munching a flower bud of Chickweed (Stellaria media) in the garden. These non-native weedy plants have just started flowering, so the bird must be munching the flower buds rather than the seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2006)

File.Name: 6219a_6218-014.jpg
Notes:
Perky Oak Titmouse (Baeolophus inoratus) - aka "Plain Titmouse" - in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree at home. It can lay that pointed crest down - or was that an immature that I saw two weeks ago who couldn't get it up? (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2006)

Perky Oak Titmouse (Baeolophus inoratus) - aka "Plain Titmouse" - in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree at home. It can lay that pointed crest down - or was that an immature that I saw two weeks ago who couldn't get it up? (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2006)

We had 1/2 inch of snow late last night - followed by rain, so there was just slushy ice by the time I got out with my camera. At least it feels like winter again, after 80+ degree highs last week!
CommonNames: Oak Titmouse; Coast Live Oak; Oak Titmouse; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Baeolophus inoratus; Quercus agrifolia; Baeolophus inoratus; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Baeolophus inoratus; Quercus agrifolia; Baeolophus inoratus; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 6219b_6218-002.jpg
Notes: Ghost bird! This Ruby-crowned Kinglet (Regulus calendula) must have taken flight just as I took the photo. It's one of those little birds that never stops moving in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees at home. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2006)

File.Name: 6219c_6218-011.jpg
Notes: Male Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree at home. I usually see these brilliant birds mixed in with a flock of Ruby-crowned Kinglets (Regulus calendula). Is that a strategy? (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2006)
CommonNames: Townsend's Warbler; Coast Live Oak; Ruby-crowned Kinglets
ScientificName: Dendroica townsendi; Quercus agrifolia; Regulus calendula
associatedTaxa: Dendroica townsendi; Quercus agrifolia; Regulus calendula

File.Name: 6220a_6220-080.jpg
Notes:
Northern Elephant Seals (Mirounga angustirostis, family Phocidae, sub-order Pinnipedia) on the beach near the Piedras Blancas Lighthouse north of San Simeon and Cambria - a two hour drive from home on a perfect Presidents' Day school field trip. We weren't as close as it looks - I wouldn't dare, and it's illegal. We were on a bluff above the beach behind a railing looking down. I felt like a voyeur, but the seals didn't seem to mind. (San Simeon, 20 February 2006)

This looks like a family scene with father, momma, and pup - but more is going on. The huge males can measure 16 feet and 5,000 pounds. The much smaller females measure 10-12 feet and 1500 pounds. The males arrive first and fight for dominance and gather harems if they are macho enough. The females give birth on the beach, but then come into season and mate just two weeks later to conceive next year's pups. This male is being more than friendly! The pups are still nursing, but one day soon the females will swim away north to Alaska bearing her already-conceived new pups for next year, leaving the new "weaner" seals to fend for themselves in "weaner pods". It's a hard life for these critters.

There's more info about these remarkable seals at:
   http://www.elephantseal.org/ and
   http://www.parks.ca.gov/default.asp?page_id=1115

D'oh - at the worst time, I had camera problems today and couldn't use my long lens. I made do with my 105 mm (macro) lens, but I'm disappointed. I've never seen the Elephant Seals up close before. This will become a new yearly trek.

File.Name: 6220b_6220-062.jpg
Notes: Male and female Northern Elephant Seals (Mirounga angustirostis) on the beach near San Simeon. This male with the great schnoz - the prodigious proboscis - the elephant's trunk - better than Jimmy Durante - has more on his mind than affection! See my [Next] photo for more info. (San Simeon, 20 February 2006)

File.Name: 6220c_6220-086.jpg
Notes: Alpha-male Northern Elephant Seal (Mirounga angustirostis) on the beach near San Simeon with his harem and pups. Birthing, nursing, and mating happen almost together for these huge seals. See my [Next] photos for more info. (San Simeon, 20 February 2006)

File.Name: 6221a_6220-097.jpg
Notes:
Life is good - sunshine and warm sand - never mind the snow in the mountains and the family of females and pups making noise... This male Northern Elephant Seal (Mirounga angustirostis, family Phocidae, sub-order Pinnipedia) is soaking up the rays on the beach near the Piedras Blancas Lighthouse north of San Simeon and Cambria - a two hour drive from home. (San Simeon, 20 February 2006)

Click [Previous] a few photos for more info at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=12578903

File.Name: 6221b_6220-092.jpg
Notes: Alpha-male Northern Elephant Seal (Mirounga angustirostis) on the beach near San Simeon. Don't swallow your nose, big guy! My school kids named him Fred - he's bellowing with authority, surrounded by his harem of wives and pups. It's more of a deep croaking sound. Next year I'll bring a microphone and recorder to capture the event! (San Simeon, 20 February 2006)

File.Name: 6221c_6220-075.jpg
Notes: These female Northern Elephant Seals (Mirounga angustirostis) on the beach near San Simeon seemed more aggresive than the males. They are feline, cat-like. I don't know what they were fighting over. (San Simeon, 20 February 2006)

File.Name: 6221d_6220-069.jpg
Notes: Female Northern Elephant Seals (Mirounga angustirostis) fighting on the beach near San Simeon. How many fish have gone down that red gullet? Note Big Daddy's "hand" in the background. He stayed out of it. (San Simeon, 20 February 2006)

File.Name: 6222a_6218-022.jpg
Notes: New flowers of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) at home, with raindrops. The water drop magnifies the inside and reflects the outside - it makes the big small and the small big! The weekend storm brought an inch of rain and snow just in time after a month of hot and dry weather. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2006)

File.Name: 6222b_6218-021.jpg
Notes: New frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) opening in the woods. This is our "summer fern". Other native ferns have been open all winter. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2006)

File.Name: 6222c_6216-132.jpg
Notes: New flowers of native Giant-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) showing the typical "scorpioid inflorescence" of all Phacelia species in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family - curled up like a scorpion`s tail or a fiddle's neck, making new flowers as they keep unrolling new buds. Note the glandular hairs with a sticky "pinhead" at the tip. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2006)

File.Name: 6223a_6216-123.jpg
Notes: Twisted new flowers of native Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata) opening on a recently burned chaparral area in the mountains. These vines are taking over and trying to cover everything - including some of my favorite flowers. I've seen this succession pattern before after wildfires. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2006)

File.Name: 6223b_6216-137.jpg
Notes: Like Jack's beanstalk, the native Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata) vines are climbing up the Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) flower spikes trying to engulf them on a recently burned chaparral area in the mountains. These vines are taking over and trying to cover everything - including some of my favorite fire-follower flowers. I've seen this happen after other local wildfires. I guess this is part of the succession pattern after wildfires that marks the transition from low herbs to taller chaparral shrubs that rise above the tangle of vines. I'd rather photograph the herbs, but *gulp* there will always be new fires and new flowers to photograph. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2006)

File.Name: 6223c_6216-135.jpg
Notes: It's a race between the Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) flower spikes and the Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata) vines trying to engulf them. Both plants are native on a recently burned area in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2006)

File.Name: 6224a_6216-138.jpg
Notes: Golden tendrils of native Dodder (probably Cuscuta californica) trying to engulf a flowering Chaparral Nightshade (Solanun xanti) in a recently burned area in the chaparral. Dodder is a true parasitic vine that starts as a seedling, but loses it's connection with the ground and lives by sucking the juices of other plants. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2006)

File.Name: 6224b_6216-116.jpg
Notes: Little flower of native California Figwort aka Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica?). (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2006)

File.Name: 6225a_6225-013.jpg
Notes:
Tiny fly (order Diptera, suborder Nematocera) - maybe a Dance Fly (family Empididae)? - working a male staminate flower catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) down by the creek. It's a dirty job, but I'd rather be dusted with pollen than coal dust! Willows sure look like typical wind-pollinated plants with male and female catkins in the air, and maybe they are. But they also produce nectar in the glands at the base of each y-shaped stamen. This tiny fly has a long proboscis to work that sweet spot down between the pollen-dusted stamens. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2006)

Is it trying to clean its proboscis with its pollen-dusted front legs? That would be like cleaning my camera lens with my sweaty t-shirt!

File.Name: 6225b_6225-014.jpg
Notes: Same tiny fly as the [Previous] photo - (order Diptera, suborder Nematocera) - maybe a Dance Fly (family Empididae)? - working a male staminate flower catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) down by the creek. You can see the mosquito-like fly using its long proboscis to reach the sweet nectary at the base of the stamen. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2006)

File.Name: 6225c_6225-018.jpg
Notes: Very different "muscoid" fly (order Diptera, suborder Brachycera) working the male staminate flower catkins of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) down by the creek. Compare the [Next] two photos. This fly is slurping up pollen with its short proboscis rather than sucking nectar like the [Next] fly with its long proboscis. Different strokes for different folks! (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2006)

File.Name: 6225d_6225-019.jpg
Notes: Pollen-dusted "muscoid" fly (order Diptera, suborder Brachycera) working the male staminate flower catkins of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) down by the creek. These willow trees look wind-pollinated, but they provide pollen and nectar for many early insects. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2006)

File.Name: 6225e_6225-030.jpg
Notes: California Harvester Ants (Pogonomyrmex californicus or kin) tending brown aphids (family Aphididae in the order Hemiptera) on a budding branch of native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) by the creek. The ants are paying close attention to the large aphid on the right. I have lots to learn about the ant-aphid connection in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2006)
CommonNames: California Harvester Ants; aphids; Arroyo Willow
ScientificName: Pogonomyrmex californicus; Aphididae; Hemiptera; Salix lasiolepis
associatedTaxa: Pogonomyrmex californicus; Aphididae; Hemiptera; Salix lasiolepis

File.Name: 6225f_6216-140.jpg
Notes:
Golden tendrils of native Dodder (probably Cuscuta californica) putting a stranglehold on a stem of Chaparral Nightshade (Solanun xanti). Dodder is a true parasite - it will surround and enter the host plant to steal its juices. (San Marcos Pass, 16 February 2006)

I've been looking for new insects - sometimes I find them first in my photos. Look very close along the biggest dodder tendril on the left - the first up from the bottom - and about 1/3 across. What a strange silhouette!

File.Name: 6226a_6225-006.jpg
Notes:
This little flowering Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) is a perfect breakfast plate for this pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) stopping by for a morning drink. The round leaves collect (and maybe exude?) water. It's a favorite morning stop for many early insects who go to the flowers rather than the creek about 10 feet away. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2006)

Windy today with a gathering storm - maybe 5-10 inches of rain in the next few days. We need it!

File.Name: 6226b_6225-007.jpg
Notes: Pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) cleaning up after breakfast. (See my [Previous] photo.) Look close to see it has one leg wiping its left antenna, and the other leg wiping its compound eye! These flies lay eggs that hatch and prey on aphids and other larvae. It's interesting that they are among the first - and last - insects to appear each year. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2006)

File.Name: 6226c_6225-047.jpg
Notes: A different Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) resting on a leaf of California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2006)

File.Name: 6226d_6225-009.jpg
Notes: Native hornet or yellowjacket wasp (order Hymenoptera, family Vespidae, genus Vespula) taking a morning drink from a cup of flowering Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the canyon. I'm confused by these feisty wasps. This one is more black than our usual summer yellowjackets that are mostly yellow. Maybe this is the wasp that builds the paper "hornet nests" I sometimes find fallen under the trees. Our summer wasps make paper nests in old gopher holes in the ground. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2006)

File.Name: 6226e_6225-045.jpg
Notes: Tiny aphid-sized flowers of Bitter-cress (Cardamine oligosperma) - in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family - with a real winged aphid (family Aphididae in the order Hemiptera) posing for scale. This little mustard is native, but it grows like a weed along trails and in disturbed places. Look close and you can see new seeds developing in the pods. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2006)

File.Name: 6226f_6225-016.jpg
Notes: Tiny fly with a long proboscis working a male staminate flower catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) down by the creek, with another catkin behind to create a glow. I guess this mosquito-like fly is a Dance Fly (order Diptera, suborder Nematocera, family Empididae - maybe genus Toreus)? So many flies, and so many kinds of flies! See my [Previous] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2006)

File.Name: 6227a-6225-040.jpg
Notes: Duskywing Skippers (genus Erynnis) are hard to distinguish on the wing - rather than dead in a collection case! It helps to study the photos with my books. I believe this is a male Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristes) skipper butterfly. I think it's male because of the "bend" on the front "costal" edge of the wings. I love the brilliant white fringe on its hind wings. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2006)

File.Name: 6227b_6225-038.jpg
Notes: I believe this is a male Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristes) skipper butterfly resting in the canyon. See my [Next] photo. Look how it's hunkering down on the dead plant stem trying to hide as I hound it for another photo. Beautiful wings, like a fine white-fringed cloak. (San Marcos Pass, 25 February 2006)

File.Name: 6228a-6224-230.jpg
Notes: New spring leaves and catkins of Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) near Lake Cachuma. The weather has been swinging between warm and cold in the last few weeks. Most of our deciduous oaks are still bare, but this tree is dancing! (Santa Ynez Valley, 24 February 2006)

File.Name: 6228b-6223-158.jpg
Notes: New flowers of native Tauschia arguta in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family - umbels of umbels. (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2006)

File.Name: 6228c-6223-157.jpg
Notes: A true bug (order Hemiptera) on a sandstone outcrop in the mountains. There were many of these bugs on the ground, but they wouldn't stand still for a photo. I shot 30 photos and kept this one. I guess this is a Bordered Plant Bug (Euryophthalmus convivus) or kin in the Pyrrhocoridae family. It must be spring! (San Marcos Pass, 23 February 2006)

File.Name: 6301a-6224-222.jpg
