Notes: A flock of native Shooting Star (Dodecatheon clevelandii ssp. insulare) flowers in the woods near Lake Cachuma. "A flock"? - they remind me of little birds! These early flowers are in the Primrose (Primulaceae) family. (Santa Ynez Valley, 24 February 2006)

File.Name: 6302a-6302-071.jpg
Notes: New flower cluster of a native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the canyon. The flowers emerge from the buds like a bunch of middle school kids from a bus! This maple is common in Oregon, but scarce here. Like our native Madrones, Tanoak, Huckleberrys, and Thimbleberrys (and more) - it's a remnant from a previous wetter and cooler climate that still hangs on in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2006)

File.Name: 6302b_6302-075.jpg
Notes: New flower cluster on a native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the canyon. It looks like a catkin, but these are real flowers in a "pendent raceme". Some of the flowers are staminate with only pollen-bearing stamens, but most are bisexual with both male stamens and female pistils to make seed for the next generation. These new flower buds are packed FULL of life. It really is spring! (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2006)

File.Name: 6302c-6302-073.jpg
Notes: New flowers and new palmate leaves emerging from a bud of deciduous Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the canyon. It feels like spring! (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2006)

File.Name: 6303a-6225-034.jpg
Notes: Minimalist flowers on one of those grass-like plants that's not a real grass - maybe a Sedge (Carex) in the Cyperaceae family? It seems to have lots of pollen-bearing stamens - or are some of those gangly things female pistils? It's growing in the woods under the oaks, and it's fairly common. I thought it was something else, but I hit the books tonight to figure it out, and now I'm totally confused. Pretty though! (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2006)

File.Name: 6303b-6302-082.jpg
Notes: New flowers of native Chaparral Lotus (Lotus grandiflorus) in the Pea Family (Fabaceae). The flowers start yellow, but they develop red colors as they age. Most of our Lotus flowers do that. The lotus of dreams is something different. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2006)

File.Name: 6303c-6302-081.jpg
Notes: Looking at the "goodies" inside a native Giant-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) flower in the mountains - 5 male stamens and 2 female stigmas waiting for pollen. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2006)

File.Name: 6303d-6302-077.jpg
Notes: New fruit of native Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or California Manroot (Marah fabaceus) growing in the mountains. You wouldn't want to eat the spiny tangerine-size fruit - they taste terrible - but it's fun to shoot the slippery seeds inside by squeezing them between your thumb and forefinger! This impressive fruit developed from the small ovary of the flower in the [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2006)

File.Name: 6303e-6209-091.jpg
Notes:
New female flower of native Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or California Manroot (Marah fabaceus) growing on a recently burned area in the mountains. That fluffy-looking area behind the flower is the ovary where seeds will develop after the flower is pollinated. See my [Next] photo for the results! (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2006)

Compare my photo of the male flowers at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=12442839

I've been meaning to get a better photo of the female flowers, but they are scarce on this monoecious plant that has mostly male flowers with an occasional female. Now I'm puzzled - I need to look closer to understand the male-female thing.

File.Name: 6303f-6302-076.jpg
Notes: New flowers opening the buds of a native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the canyon. No flash - I caught the buds in a beam of afternoon sunlight with the dark canyon behind. See my [Previous] few photos for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2006)

File.Name: 6304a-6304-015.jpg
Notes:
Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) climbing over a twig in the woods. This is our largest terrestrial mollusk. We're finally having "Banana Slug weather" after recent rains, with more rain on the way. That's just what we need after a very dry new year. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2006)

It's always a photo challenge to capture these critters up close with those long eyestalks. This photo is 1/50 sec @ f/18 - still not enough depth of field.

File.Name: 6304b-6304-027.jpg
Notes: Little Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) hugging a fading Snowdrop (Galanthus nivalis) flower in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2006)

File.Name: 6304c-6304-024.jpg
Notes: I was looking at the new flowers of this native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) - then I shifted focus and noticed this single ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) tending its garden of Aphids (family Aphididae in the order Hemiptera) on the flower stalk. I guess it's our usual "piss-ant" - the California Harvester Ant (Pogonomyrmex californicus or kin). I've been watching these ants tend the aphids all winter. They form dense colonies, but now they seem to be spreading out on the shrub. I wonder if individual ants actually carry the aphids to colonize new places? (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2006)
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; ant; Aphids; California Harvester Ant
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Hemiptera; Pogonomyrmex californicus
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Hemiptera; Pogonomyrmex californicus

File.Name: 6304d-6304-022.jpg
Notes:
Male pollen-bearing catkins on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon - the first flowers I've seen. This is the same tree that flowered very early (in December!) last year - see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=9094410

Individual differences between members of the same species is the engine that powers evolution and adaptation. Last year - with all the rain - it didn't work. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2006)

File.Name: 6304e-6304-017.jpg
Notes: Sporophytes of tiny Liverworts (Marchantia or Asterella?) - related to mosses - on a sandstone outcrop still wet from recent rain. My first thought is surfing palm trees! The woods are full of surprises when you look close. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2006)

File.Name: 6304f-6304-019.jpg
Notes: Unknown mushroom on my trail in the woods with new grasses. This 'shroom is more than an inch across and it feels tough. My first thought was Clitocybe because of the decurrent gills, but I have second thoughts. We had good rain (and snow!) this week, but mushrooms are still scarce after the mostly dry weeks since the new year. I'm starting a vacation week from school with time to hunt for new mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2006)

File.Name: 6305a_6304-006.jpg
Notes: I believe this pretty little bird is an Orange-crowned Warbler (Vermivora celata) - Pacific race - hiding in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. See [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2006)

File.Name: 6305b_6304-007.jpg
Notes: I believe this pretty little bird is an Orange-crowned Warbler (Vermivora celata) - Pacific race - in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon. It does have a hint of an orange crown and a short dark eye-line. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2006)

File.Name: 6306a_6304-011.jpg
Notes:
This Rufous-sided Towhee (Pipilo erythrophthalmus) bird hiding in the California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) bramble at home sure looks like it's giving me the evil eye! Nah - these pretty birds just want to scratch in the leaves looking for grubs and other little critters for breakfast. Maybe those critters think it's an evil eye! (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2006)

It rained all day today - just an inch or two, but a good soaker to bring spring flowers and mushrooms. I'm on vacation this week - wahoo! It was a perfect day to do all those critical updates and backups on my computer that I've been meaning to do for weeks - and find some new music!


   http://www.archive.org/audio/
   http://www.emusic.com/

File.Name: 6306b_6304-002.jpg
Notes:
Plain little bird in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our porch. I guess it's an Oak Titmouse (Baeolophus inoratus) - aka "Plain Titmouse" - with its crest down. It's hard trying to see these little birds in my camera viewfinder with the long lens, when I can't actually see them come and go! (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2006)

Compare my Titmouse photo with crest at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=12565290

File.Name: 6306c_6304-010.jpg
Notes: This little bird in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees took wing just as I got the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2006)

File.Name: 6306d_6305-032.jpg
Notes: Native Southern California Black Walnut (Juglans californica) tree starting to flower with new catkins along the creek at my Dad's house in the Santa Monica Mountains. This tree is just a remnant of the native woods in my Dad's garden. It has real black walnuts - hard as a rock, you need a hammer to open them, but tasty if you get them before the worms. These native riparian trees have an interesting dirtribution. Back home, I mostly find them growing along creeks near old native American Chumash sites. They are being clobbered by development, but they are also used as rootstocks for new walnut orchards. Life goes on... (Santa Monica Mountains, 5 March 2006)

File.Name: 6306e_6304-026.jpg
Notes: New flower cluster of native Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata in the Berberidaceae family) getting ready to open. It's growing in a place where the sun never shines. This is a kind of "Oregon Grape" that really belongs further north - in Oregon - but here it is in the canyon. This remnant population is holding on in the north-facing remnant Oak/Madrone woodland where we live. I'm feeling more and more like a remnant myself! I've never seen a ripe fruit, but somehow it propagates. I took this photo in deep shade - 1/15 second @ f/8 with tripod and self-timer. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2006)

File.Name: 6306f_6302-078.jpg
Notes: First little flower of Common Eucrypta (Eucrypta chrysanthemifolia) - in the Hydrophyllaceae family - growing in a recently burned chaparral area in the mountains. This native plant has a distinctive strong smell - it stinks! (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2006)

File.Name: 6307a_6307-034.jpg
Notes:
Tiny mushroom growing from a decomposing Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) branch on the ground, with an oak leaf in front. Fungi are decomposing the leaf as well, making beautiful patterns. I guess this fragile mushroom is Bolbitius retuculatis or kin. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2006)

Rain yesterday, and the storm blew through today. It was really windy. Only the mushrooms were holding still for a photo, so today's photos are all mushrooms.

File.Name: 6307b_6307-051.jpg
Notes: Pretty little LBMs - "Little Brown Mushrooms" - growing from moss on the ground in a clearing in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2006)

File.Name: 6307c_6307-055.jpg
Notes: I guess this strange coral-like mushroom is "Crested White Coral" (Clavulina cristata), growing from the leaves under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon. It's not as colorful or as intricately branched as the true Coral Mushrooms (Ramaria), but it still looks like it belongs under the sea rather than in the woods. The dark tips of the tough stalks are the spore-bearing part. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2006)
CommonNames: Crested White Coral"; Coast Live Oak; Coral Mushrooms

File.Name: 6307d_6307-035.jpg
Notes: Strange Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum or kin) mushroom in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. Note the branching ribs on the outside of the cup. These mushrooms are common along trails in our canyon, but probably not edible. I'm not tempted. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2006)

File.Name: 6307e_6307-037.jpg
Notes: This has been a great year for delicious wild Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in our woods. These mushrooms bring $15-$20 a pound in local markets - more in restaurants - and a large specimen like this can weigh a pound or more. This makes me nervous - like putting little price tags and barcodes on the banana slugs and butterflies and native lilies in the woods. Be a naturalist and find your own! (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2006)

File.Name: 6307f_6218-027.jpg
Notes: I believe this mushroom is a Springtime Amanita (Amanita velosa) growing in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. It has white gills, a saclike volva or cup at the base, no ring to speak of, a cottony white patch on the cap, and striate lines along the edge of the cap. It's said to be edible and tasty, but I'm nervous about trying any mushroom with a "death cup" at the base. Why bother when there are still delicious chanterelles in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2006)

File.Name: 6308a_6307-063.jpg
Notes: Look close - this acrobatic little Bushtit (Psaltriparus minimus) bird has a beak full of web as it pulls apart an insect nest or coccoon in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our porch. See my [Previous] photo. I was pretty close with my big lens, but this is still a tight crop. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2006)

File.Name: 6308b_6307-057.jpg
Notes: Active little Bushtit (Psaltriparus minimus) looking for breakfast in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our porch. These small birds always seem to arrive in active chittering flocks that vanish as fast as they arrive. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2006)

File.Name: 6308c_6307-040.jpg
Notes: Unknown little fly (order Diptera) resting on a flowering cup of a Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the canyon. Something about flies' eyes makes these 7-sided reflections of my lens diaphragm - like the highlights in the corner. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2006)

File.Name: 6308d_6307-048.jpg
Notes: New foliage of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the canyon. Last year some little critters destroyed the buds of almost all our plants. I hope this will be a better year. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2006)

File.Name: 6308e_6307-046.jpg
Notes: Strange new frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) opening in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2006)

File.Name: 6308f_6307-053.jpg
Notes: Another strange "Crested White Coral" (Clavulina cristata) mushroom, growing from the leaves under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the canyon. The dark tips of the tough stalks are the spore-bearing part. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2006)
CommonNames: Crested White Coral"; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 6309a_6309-080.jpg
Notes:
Looking very close at a small California Slender Salamander (Batrachoseps attenuatus) that I exposed by lifting the bark from a decomposing Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) branch in the canyon. Check my [Previous] photo to see the whole critter. This native amphibian is just a few inches long and thinner than a pencil - almost a worm with those tiny legs. It jerked around like a fishing worm on a hook when I first uncovered it, but then it posed for a few photos before crawling away under the bark into the log. I reckon this is one of those "indicator species" of a healthy environment, like trout in the creek and the strange flies in the garden. How much would the world change if they all disappeared? (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2006)

Today was a terrible day for photos - gale force winds whipping the trees around as a new winter storm moves in. Everything is moving. I didn't expect much when I went for my walk, but I looked close at the ground for things that weren't moving so much and got some new photos. There's always something new to find in the woods!
CommonNames: California Slender Salamander; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 6309b_6309-081.jpg
Notes: This small California Slender Salamander (Batrachoseps attenuatus) is a worm-like amphibian with tiny legs. I found this one under the bark of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) branch in the canyon. It's just a few inches long and thinner than a pencil. When I first lifted the bark, it jerked back and forth like a fishing worm trying to get off the hook. Then it played dead and posed for a few photos until I went away. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2006)
CommonNames: California Slender Salamander; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 6309c_6309-096.jpg
Notes:
So strange - and hard to photograph. It's like like a crumpled-up piece of foil or one of those dang mylar birthday balloons that land in our canyon... This lime-sized object is the empty silk cocoon of a native Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus). I usually find the cocoons on Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus), but there were none of the shrubs to be found. There were native Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) nearby. Both shrubs are in the same Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family, so maybe that's good enough. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2006)

The cocoon feels and sounds like touching the paper cone of a stereo speaker. It's a magical sound. Native American Chumash shamans made rattles from these cocoons. The adult moths are scarce, and the cocoons are a rare find. I've been collecting them for years. I have a basket full, and I plan to make my own rattle.
CommonNames: Ceanothus Silk Moth; Greenbark Ceanothus; Coffee Berry; Buckthorn
ScientificName: Hyalophora euryalus). I usually find the cocoons on Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnus tomentella; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Hyalophora euryalus). I usually find the cocoons on Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnus tomentella; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: 6309d_6309-068.jpg
Notes:
I found this native Land Snail (Gastropoda) shell in the woods today. This snail has a flat shell that turns clockwise. It is NOT one of those nasty introduced Garden Snails with a vertical shell that are so common in town and at school. I hope it's too cold in the mountains for them ever to get established here. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2006)

I rarely see the live native snails in the woods. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=9497119

File.Name: 6309e_6309-076.jpg
Notes: Fallen Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) leaf in the woods, showing some color with the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2006)

File.Name: 6309f_6309-087.jpg
Notes: I'm pretty sure this mushroom in the woods is Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus or kin) under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees - the first I've seen this year. But you have to think like a detective to figure it out. The cap is shiny and dry, but the leaves stuck on top of the cap show that it was viscid and sticky after the last rain. It has reddish-brown gills, and you can see remnants of the "cortina" veil on the stem with the same color. It has dried "slime-boogers" on the stem, mostly hidden in this photo, but you can see a few. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2006)

File.Name: 6310a_6310-104.jpg
Notes: We had real snow this morning - followed by rain and hail! This Canyon Wren (Catherpes mexicanus) was one on the few birds I saw today. Where do the birds and butterflies go when it's this cold and wet? (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2006)

File.Name: 6310b_6309-070.jpg
Notes: New manzanita-like flowers of native Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum) in the shady north-slope Madrone and Tanoak woodland near home. This shrub is not common here. I rarely find fruit. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2006)

File.Name: 6310c_6309-090.jpg
Notes: A tiny forest of moss (Bryophyta) sporophytes in the canyon. It was very windy when I took this photo yesterday, but these little fruiting mosses close to the ground weren't moving much. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2006)

File.Name: 6310d_6309-091.jpg
Notes: A group of dark cup fungi in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees - maybe Peziza badia or kin? (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2006)

File.Name: 6311a_6311-048.jpg
Notes:
Pink flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the garden, wih snow. Look how the flowers give their colors to the snow! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2006)

It snowed all morning, but we never had 1/2 inch accumulate at one time, not enough to break the trees. I know most of you are used to this, but for us in Central California: what fun!

File.Name: 6311b_6311-049.jpg
Notes: A little pile of snow from today's storm on new berries of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) near the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2006)

File.Name: 6311c_6311-064.jpg
Notes: New Peach tree (Prunus persica) flower in the garden with a cap of ice from the snow storm today. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2006)

File.Name: 6311d_6311-043.jpg
Notes: Early flowers of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) with snow. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2006)

File.Name: 6311e_6311-058.jpg
Notes: Snow on the native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) flower buds getting ready to bloom in the garden. Look close on the middle-left below the stem - this single ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) is tending its colony of Aphids (family Aphididae in the order Hemiptera) on the flower stalk. Almost every flower cluster on this large shrub next to our house has an aphid colony with one or two ants in charge. There is a large mother-colony of ants and aphids that I can see out the window from the bathroom upstairs that has been there all winter. The ants must be carrying the aphids around to colonize this shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2006)
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Hemiptera

File.Name: 6311f_6311-040.jpg
Notes:
Snow day today - I guess it's too early to plant these marigolds in the garden! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2006)

It snowed all morning, but only 1/2 inch or so ever accumulated at one time, not enough to break the trees. I know most of you are used to this, but for us in Central California: what fun!

File.Name: 6312a_6311-044.jpg
Notes:
A new grass (family Poaceae) plant on the edge of the garden with snow. Snow builds up around the little plants and protects them, but it can pile up in the trees and bring them down. We only had half an inch or so in this late winter storm, no problems. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2006)

Today we had gloomy clouds, rain, some snow that didn't stick, and even some sunshine. Not a great photo day, and just as well as I have to get ready for school tomorrow after a vacation week. *Sigh*

File.Name: 6312b_6311-055.jpg
Notes: Our native Canyon Lupines (Lupinus latifolius) catch rain drops perfectly, but they don't do so well with snow! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2006)

File.Name: 6312c_6311-045.jpg
Notes: White snow on the native White Sage (Salvia apiana) on the edge of our garden. We had a snow day yesterday! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2006)

File.Name: 6312d_6311-083.jpg
Notes: Seedling Bedstraw (Galium aparine) posing with snow and hail and a colorful old leaf of California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) behind. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2006)

File.Name: 6312e_6311-079.jpg
Notes: Melting hail on a potted succulent on the porch yesterday afternoon. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2006)

File.Name: 6312f_6311-066.jpg
Notes: These new flowers on the Peach tree (Prunus persica) in the garden still have a cap of ice from the snow storm. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2006)

File.Name: 6313a_6311-082.jpg
Notes:
This photo is a forensic stumper - not a crime scene, but an accident to explain. You can see the base of the waist-high bookshelf on the left, two panels of a monochrome triptych painting by Julie, my old ice axe from many fine High Sierra backpack trips, and a spilled basket of Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) acorns scattered on top of everything else. Someone slammed the door too hard during the snow storm last weekend, and this is what I found on the floor. Originally the painting was against the wall on top of the bookshelf, with the basket in front, and the ice axe on the edge. I reckon the painting tipped first to knock everything else over the edge. But how did they end up in this particular order? (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2006)

It's a pretty cool photo too!

File.Name: 6314a_6310-109.jpg
Notes:
Little Canyon Wren (Catherpes mexicanus) bird perched on a cooler at home after a bit of snow last week. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2006)

This is one of my busy times - My son Ewan and Roz are visiting from Oregon this week, and my school Science Fair is next week, so I have to do MY project on local butterflies. No time for new photos - I'll be dipping into the archives.

File.Name: 6315a_6309-083.jpg
Notes: Yellow Coral Mushroom (Ramaria rasilispora or kin) growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees in the woods. Some critter has nibbled off the top of this strange mushroom that is said to be edible. It looks like it should be living under the sea rather than in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2006)
CommonNames: Yellow Coral Mushroom; Coast Live Oak; Madrone
ScientificName: Ramaria rasilispora; Quercus agrifolia; Arbutus menziesii
associatedTaxa: Ramaria rasilispora; Quercus agrifolia; Arbutus menziesii

File.Name: 6316a_6309-097.jpg
Notes: New unfolding "fiddleneck" frond of California Polypody (Polypodium californicum) fern in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2006)

File.Name: 6317a_6317-134.jpg
Notes:
Beautiful network-geometry of these native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum var. hoffmannii) flowers held together with raindrops after the storm today. You can see the spiny (and glandular) flower ovaries transforming into berries, swelling with ripening seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2006)

We haven't had a lot of rain this month (or year), but we've had many cold, rainy days. This must be good for the spring wildflowers to come!

File.Name: 6317b_6317-133.jpg
Notes:
Tiny flowers and seedpods of Bitter-cress (Cardamine oligosperma) - in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family. This little mustard is native, but it grows like a weed along trails and in disturbed places at home. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2006)

These tiny flowers are aphid-sized. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=12650072

File.Name: 6317c_6317-129.jpg
Notes: New spring leaves of deciduous native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis) in the woods near our home, with raindrops from the storm today. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2006)

File.Name: 6317d_6317-127.jpg
Notes:
New frond of native Goldback Fern (Pentagramma triangularis) with raindrops. There's a mature frond in the background. They fatten up as they grow. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2006)

It's been a busy week. I finally had some time after school today for photos, but it was raining. *Sigh* The sun finally came out for a short time, and I made the best of it. We haven't had a lot of rain this month (or year), but we've had many cold, rainy days. This must be good for the wildflowers to come!

File.Name: 6318a_6317-001.jpg
Notes:
Little Bushtit (Psaltriparus minimus) bird in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree closest to upstairs porch. There were no birds this morning until a flock of these tiny birds tore it up! Ten minutes later, they were gone. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2006)

It was very windy and cold today with passing clouds and some rain showers. Not a good photo day. Just as well. My school science fair is next Friday - I'm the teacher - and I still have my project to do. I'm making a database/calendar of my butterfly photos. It's been so cold (with snow) this week that butterflies seem a distant dream, but it's almost the vernal equinox. Spring is almost here!
Locality: with snow) this week that butterflies seem a distant dream

File.Name: 6318b_6318-005.jpg
Notes: Little Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) hugging non-native Salvia leaf in the garden. It's been very windy today as yesterday's storm blows through. It's interesting that these Syrphid flies are among the first and last large flies I find every year. It makes sense. They lay eggs that hatch into larvae that parasitize the larvae of other insects, and fall and spring are the busy times. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2006)

File.Name: 6318c_6317-137.jpg
Notes: Maybe these strange mushrooms are new White Elfin Saddles (Helvella crispa) or kin. The tiny spines of the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves are about 1 mm for a sense of scale. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2006)
CommonNames: White Elfin Saddles; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: 6319a_6319-010.jpg
Notes:
I was startled to see this shy little House Finch (Carpodacus mexicanus) hiding in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree closest to the upstairs porch early this morning. I haven't seen any finches since last summer. I guess this is a common bird in town, but not here in the mountains. Its bill looks slightly crossed in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2006)

Beautiful day - a bit windy - but I've been staring at my computer screen all day. My school science fair is NEXT Friday. I'm the teacher, but I always do my own project. This year I'm going through through my photos and making a slideshow and calendar of my butterfly photos.

File.Name: 6319b_6319-028.jpg
Notes: Perfect Coccora mushroom - Amanita calyptrata, aka A. calyptroderma - growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. Said to be "edible and unsurpassed" - but it looks something like the deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) and Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushrooms that I also find in our woods. That's good advice unless you really know your mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2006)
CommonNames: Coccora; Coast Live Oak; Death Cap; Destroying Angel
ScientificName: Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita phalloides; Amanita ocreata
associatedTaxa: Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita phalloides; Amanita ocreata

File.Name: 6319c_6319-026.jpg
Notes: Perfect Coccora mushroom - Amanita calyptrata, aka A. calyptroderma - growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods. "Coccora" is Italian for "coccoon" - a perfect description for the soft white "egg" these mushrooms emerge from. As the "coccoon" opens, it leaves the soft thick white patch on the cap and the large cup or "volva" at the base, along with the skirt-like ring on the stalk. This mushroom really is delicious, but it's good advice to avoid any mushroom with a "death cup" at the base unless you really know your 'shrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2006)
ScientificName: Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: 6320a_6319-013.jpg
Notes:
Early flowers of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) near home. These typical flowers in the Pea Family (Fabaceae) are called "Papilionaceous" - a fancy way of saying pea-like. They have five petals if you look close: the upright banner petal in the back to attract insects; two wing petals on the sides to make a landing platform; and the two fused keel petals that stick out from the tip of the wings in this photo. Sometimes you can tap the flowers with a stick and see the keel poke up to goose a visiting insect. The stamen and pistil "goodies" are hidden inside the keel. This is a very successful adaptation for pollination around the world! (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2006)

Today is the Vernal Equinox - the first day of spring! But it still feels like winter - it's cold and raining right now. And my school science fair is Friday. Yipes - no time for new photos this week!
ScientificName: Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; banner; wing; keel
associatedTaxa: Lupinus latifolius; Fabaceae; banner; wing; keel

File.Name: 6321a_6319-017.jpg
Notes: One day into spring after the equinox, but it doesn't feel like spring yet after yesterday's cold rain. Hah - it felt like spring in January! Here's one of the first Borage (Borago officinalis) flowers in our garden. It's an ancient herb that is namesake to the Borage (Boraginaceae plant family. It has naturalized in the garden and reseeds itself every year in different places. I like to add the pretty flowers to our salads. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2006)

File.Name: 6322a_6322-036.jpg
Notes: A little fly (order Diptera) belly-up inside a golden California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) flower in the school garden. A little spider had it, but ran off when I approached with my camera. I don't think this fly is going anywhere. (Santa Ynez Valley, 22 March 2006)

File.Name: 6323a.jpg
Notes: 13005469 Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) in a wood pile at school. Behind the visible eye, you can see its mysterious "third eye." The opening lower down on its neck is the tympanum of its left ear. It sure heard me coming, and took off when I tried to get closer. (Santa Ynez Valley, 23 March 2006)

File.Name: 6324a.jpg
Notes:
13005722 Here's my poster at the DMS science fair tonight. I'm the science teacher, but I always do my own project. (I'd rather worry about MY project than the kids'!) For my project, I went through all my posted Fotolog photos (and a few more) to make a digital butterfly collection organized by family and species. No butterflies were harmed by this collection! I was surprised to find I have captured photos of 49 different species of butterflies in our canyon on San Marcos Pass... so far! I organized my photos into a database by species and date, and made a DVD butterfly slideshow of 500+ photos. Julie gets the credit for assembling this wonderful poster showing every different species. (Santa Ynez Valley, 24 March 2006)

Whew - I'm glad it's over!

File.Name: 6325a.jpg
Notes: 13006404 This Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) isn't smiling because he's glad to see me. I tried to sneak up on him while he was croaking, but he saw me coming and stopped. His throat is still distended. A moment later he dived away. It's a good day here for frogs with overcast and drizzle. This is spring? (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2006)

File.Name: 6325b.jpg
Notes:
13006317 Ants in the books, ants in the bed, ants in my pants!

Our bedroom has been invaded by ants! The ants are totally in control. We began our counter-offensive today with vacuum cleaner and "Bar Keeper's Friend" cleanser. They don't like it. The war is on! Meanwhile, I'm sleeping on the sofa. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2006)

They sure look like our usual "piss-ants" - the California Harvester Ant (Pogonomyrmex californicus or kin), the same ants that tend aphids in the Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) near home. They are NOT the horrible tiny invasive Argentine Ants (Iridomyrmex humilis) that are taking over California.

The little ants bite and hang on. I got this photo through the window from outside. They drove me away when I tried to get close inside. There are big all-black winged ants and the usual smaller strong-smelling bicolored ants that recognize each other, but look nothing alike. I'm sure it's a mating swarm. Are the winged ants queens or males? Why are they in our bedroom??
Locality:
Iridomyrmex humilis) that are taking over California.

The little ants bite and hang on. I got this photo through the window from outside. They drove me away when I tried to get close inside. There are big all-black winged ants and the usual smaller strong-smelling bicolored ants that recognize each other

File.Name: 6325c.jpg
Notes: 13005968 Tiny Weevil (order Coleoptera, family Curculionidae) beetle with an elephant-like snout on a native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) leaf near home. It's bigger than a pinhead, but smaller than a pencil eraser. I saw several, and there is some leaf damage on the plants. I hope these little suckers (really chewers) don't become a problem for our lupines. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2006)

File.Name: 6325d.jpg
Notes: 13005899 Rufous-sided Towhee (Pipilo erythrophthalmus) bird eating the ripe berries of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the drizzle today. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2006)

File.Name: 6326a.jpg
Notes: 13021161 Male Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly resting on a leaf of Pitcher Sage (Lepechinia calycina) in the mountains. This butterfly is a "Blue" - one of the Gossamer-wings (family Lycaenidea), in subfamily Polyommatinae. I just finished my school science fair project on local butterflies, so I knew this pretty fellow right away - and now I have my own photo book! It's the first Spring Azure I've recognized this year, though I've seen other unknown Blues fly by. It's gray on the outside, but it really is blue when it flies. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2006)

File.Name: 6326b.jpg
Notes: 13021007 Little spider holding its prey in a pretty flower cluster of Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus). That's a terrible common name for the most beautiful Ceanothus shrub near home. It has deep blue flowers and the three-veined leaves. It's our last Ceanothus shrub to flower in the mountains here on San Marcos Pass. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2006)

File.Name: 6326c.jpg
Notes: 13020946 Pretty little "true bug" (order Hemiptera, suborder Heteroptera) on a new female "pistillate" catkin of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2006)

File.Name: 6326d.jpg
Notes: 13020756 Tiny mystery beetle (order Coleoptera) eating a slice from a single leaflet of a palmate Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) leaf near home. Big insects are still scarse after recent snow and cold weather, but I always find something new when I look even closer. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2006)

File.Name: 6326e.jpg
Notes:
13020698 I hit the books tonight, and I think this delicate little critter is a Stilt Bug (order Hemiptera, family Bertytidae). I thought "crane fly" at first, but it was sluggish and didn't try to fly away - but it did let me get a photo. Then I noticed it has four wings. (Flies are "Diptera" with only two functional wings.) Then I got the photo on my computer monitor and noticed the long proboscis extending back under its head. It's a "true bug" for sure. This Stilt Bug is perched on a stem of Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine) near the garden. It's less than 1/2 inch from tip to toe. My books say it feeds on plants. I like those little flags on its antennae! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2006)

This may be a relative in my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=10729404

File.Name: 6326f.jpg
Notes: 13020211 The same Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) in the [Previous] photo jumped into the pond when I got too close. Hard to hide with a brilliant color like that! Our "pond" is just a well-disguised 4'x4' fiberglass tub packed with water plants in pots. It works as a magnet for interesting native critters. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2006)

File.Name: 6327a.jpg
Notes: 13036164 First flower I've seen of Chaparral Zygadene aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus). The large green glands at the base of the petals are typical of the local variety in our mountains. It's a monocot in the Lily (Liliaceae) family. Even if it was delicious rather than toxic, you shouldn't eat this beautiful native lily! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2006)

File.Name: 6327b.jpg
Notes: 13036040 New flowers of native Cream-Flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca) growing in a place that burned last year. It's a strange bleeding-heart relative in the Poppy (Papaveraceae) family that only grows after wildfires and soon disappears. Only one of these flowers is fully open - lower middle-right. It looks joyous with arms raised to celebrate life! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2006)

File.Name: 6327c.jpg
Notes: 13035956 Unknown insect galls engulfing new leaves and buds of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the woods. These little oaks are "semi-deciduous" - they drop their leaves all at once, then get new leaves at about the same time. I guess these new galls are caused by the tiny Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus chrystallinus, in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera). I'll check back to see what hapens as the leaves mature. All insect galls are a strange genetic bond between insect and plant. I have many questions and no answers, but this is what I see. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2006)

File.Name: 6327d.jpg
Notes:
13035858 I found this strange growth that looks like a clump of green moss among the new flowers of native Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the woods. I believe it's a foliose bud gall caused by an insect swapping genetic material with the shrub to make a home for its young. But what insect? I didn't find this gall in my book on "Plant Galls of the California Region", but I discovered there is a "Ceanothus Gall Midge" (Asphondylia ceunothi, in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera). Maybe this is it. I find different-looking foliose galls in other Ceanothus shrubs. Gall Midge flies cause similar foliose galls on other natives like wild rose, creosote bush, saltbush, sagebrush, and more. The closer I look at the plants in our woods, the more confusing the web of nature gets! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2006)

I found a PDF monograph on the web about "The Gall Midges of California" by A. Earl Pritchard (1953) that mentions this critter, but there's not much info. Try a web search.

File.Name: 6328a.jpg
Notes:
13050289 Pretty new flower of native annual "Red Maids" (Calandrinia breweri) growing on a recently burned area in the mountains. It has 3 white female stigmas, 5 magenta petals, and 8 male stmens. Note that these numbers are all relatively prime, so the flower doesn't have easy symmetry. They are also all Fibonacci Numbers from the sequence 1 1 2 3 5 8 13... where each number is the sum of the two before it. These numbers show up often in nature. (Other numbers show up too!) (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2006)

For more interesting stuff on Fibonacci Numbers in nature, start with:
   http://www.mcs.surrey.ac.uk/Personal/R.Knott/Fibonacci/fibnat.html
   http://britton.disted.camosun.bc.ca/fibslide/jbfibslide.htm
   http://maven.smith.edu/~phyllo/About/

File.Name: 6328b.jpg
Notes: 13050175 Fresh morning flower of native Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata) with raindrops. The new flowers have these pretty pink colors, but they soon turn white. These natives are different from the garden flowers (genus Ipomoea) and the weeds (genus Convolvulus), but these flowers all share a Convolvulaceae family resemblance, and they all try to take over. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2006)

File.Name: 6328c.jpg
Notes: 13050029 New flower spike of Chaparral Zygadene aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus). See my [Previous] photo for a closer shot. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2006)

File.Name: 6329a.jpg
Notes: 13064315 A family photo of native Giant-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) flowers: new bud, emerging stamens, and the fading but ripe mature flower on top. This plant is a fire-follower growing in a place that burned year. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2006)

File.Name: 6329b.jpg
Notes: 13064260 First new flowers of native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) near home. It's a classic hummingbird flower, a bit late because of the cold weather this month. Our hummers have been waiting for them! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2006)

File.Name: 6329c.jpg
Notes: 13064209 There are still new flowers opening on the native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) trees in the canyon. They've been flowering all month. The cold and rain this month has slowed everything down. The yellow flowers attract bees and other insects. The bright red "petals" will become the wings of the ripe maple seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2006)

File.Name: 6330a.jpg
Notes:
13078145 Pure elegance... A perfect new flower of Blue-Eyed Grass (Sisyrinchium bellum) in the shady woods on a cloudy day. These natives are little Irises in the Iridaceae family. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 March 2006)

I took a walk in the woods near Lake Cachuma after school today. Another storm is approaching. What a strange year! It started dry and warm in January like summer, but March has been cold and wet. The plants are confused and there are still very few insects to photograph. I wonder how this effects pollination?

File.Name: 6330b.jpg
Notes: 13078026 First new flower I've seen of Baby Blue-Eyes (Nemophila menziesii var. integrifolia), a native annual in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family. They are such a perfect shade of blue. Did you spot the orange Mite on the top-left petal? (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 March 2006)

File.Name: 6330c.jpg
Notes: 13077922 Looking straight at a new flower of this native Larkspur in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family growing in shady woods on a cloudy day. I've been calling it Parry's Larkspur (Delphinium parryi ssp. maritimum), but maybe it's Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum. What remarkable veins leading to the glands on each petal-like sepal. I wonder what the insects see? (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 March 2006)

File.Name: 6330d.jpg
Notes: 13077874 First new flowers of this native Larkspur in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family. I've been calling it Parry's Larkspur (Delphinium parryi ssp. maritimum), but maybe it's Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum. Either way it's a remarkable flower, and not common. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 March 2006)

File.Name: 6331a.jpg
Notes:
13090824 Late-season Shooting Star (Dodecatheon clevelandii ssp. insulare) flower in the Primrose (Primulaceae) family near Lake Cachuma. It reminds me of a hungry nestling bird! (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 March 2006)

These beautiful natives have been flowering since December. It's been an unusual year with autimn rain, then warm and dry in January and February, now a cold wet March. This odd weather (for us) is keeping our first flowers going late and slowing down the spring flowers. I'm not complaining, but it doesn't feel like tomorrow is April!
Locality: for us) is keeping our first flowers going late and slowing down the spring flowers. I'm not complaining

File.Name: 6331b.jpg
Notes: 13090700 A flock of native Shooting Star (Dodecatheon clevelandii ssp. insulare) flowers in the woods near Lake Cachuma. I've been seeing these beautiful flowers in the Primrose (Primulaceae) family since December. They remind me of little birds. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 March 2006)

File.Name: 6331c.jpg
Notes: 13090600 "Wabbits Everywhere!" These pretty flowers of Henbit aka Giraffe's Head (Lamium amplexicaule) in the Mint (Lamiaceae) family sure look like cartoon rabbits to me. This non-native weed is pretty, though it does spread into the woods where it shouldn't be. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 March 2006)

File.Name: 6401a.jpg
Notes:
13103966 Green foliage of Big-leaved Mistletoe (Phoradendron macrophyllum) on bare branches of a white-barked Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree at the upper end of Lake Cachuma. A storm is coming. The tree is catching some sunlight as it starts to rain in the mountains beyond. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 March 2006)

No time for photos today. We're packing for a school backpack trip to the desert in Joshua Tree National Park leaving early Sunday morning. More rain is expected. I won't post any photos for a week, but I hope to get many in the desert!

File.Name: 6401b.jpg
Notes: 13103798 Watch out, some of those stamens have eyes! I was trying to get a photo of this yellow flower head of native Sanicula arguta in the Parsley (Apiaceae) family. Then I got the photo on my computer and noticed the "stamens" with eyes and antennae. I guess they are a herd of little Springtail (order Collembola) insects grazing on the flowers. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 March 2006)

File.Name: 6401c.jpg
Notes: 13103606 New flowers of native Johnny-Jump-Up (Viola pedunculata) - yellow violets - in the woods near Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 March 2006)

File.Name: 6401d.jpg
Notes: 13103568 This tiny native "flower" stumped me for years. It's a mininalist "buttercup" without petals - Ranumculus hebecarpus in the Buttercup (Ranunculaceae) family. That's all there is. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 March 2006)

File.Name: 6401e.jpg
Notes: 13103492 Strange White Elfin Saddle (Helvella crispa) mushroom with whitish cap and fluted stem. Note the unusual fly perched on the cap. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 March 2006)

File.Name: 6401f.jpg
Notes: 13103421 Native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) flowers are still going. Like the Shooting Stars in my [Previous] photos, this is one of our earliest blooming wildflowers that is still flowering due to our odd weather this year. We had autumn rain, then it turned warm and dry in January like summer, but March has been cold and wet, and April looks like more of the same. Here it is. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 March 2006)

File.Name: 6408a.jpg
Notes:
13199568 Beautiful Wild Bee (Apoidea) working a flower of native strong-smelling Turpentine Broom aka Mohave Desert Rue (Thamnosma montana) in the Pinto Basin at Joshua Tree National Park. (Joshua Tree, 3 April 2006)

We just got back from a school trip to Joshua Tree for the week. The weather was wild for April in Southern California. We had gale-force wind, rain, snow, frost, and sweet warm sunshine all within a 24 hour period! Despite the weather, I got some photos - I shot 1,097 photos and kept 678 last week! I'll post more tomorrow, but I'm also on Spring Break and there are new photos to get. We got 8+ inches of rain at home while we were gone, and more is expected. It's been an interesting rain-year with autumn rain, then warm and dry in January and February, now a very cold and wet March and April (so far).

Today's photos are all desert critters - including the last photo. Critters are scarce at Joshua Tree just like they are at home.

File.Name: 6408b.jpg
Notes:
13199197 Tent Caterpillar on its web in the Joshua Tree desert with the sun behind. Maybe it's the Western Tent Caterpillar (Malacosoma californicum), but other native butterflies like Chalcedon Checkerspot (Occidryas chalcedona) also make web tents, so I'm not sure of the ID. (Joshua Tree, 2 April 2006)

The dark pellets below the caterpillar are "frass" - insect poo. See my photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=9780279
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=7663666

File.Name: 6408c.jpg
Notes: 13199114 Bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (order Diptera, family Syrphidae) resting on a branch of California Juniper (Juniperus californica) with a Juniper berry behind. (Joshua Tree, 4 April 2006)

File.Name: 6408d.jpg
Notes:
13198994 Wild Honey Bees (Apis mellifera) in the Joshua Tree desert swarming around our water bags, even though it rained later that night. (Joshua Tree, 4 April 2006)

Look before you drink - or climb!

File.Name: 6408e.jpg
Notes:
13198869 Beautiful emerald-green Wild Bee (Apoidea) - maybe a Burrowing Bee (family Andrenidae)? It's working flowers of native Basket Bush aka "Squaw Bush" (Rhus trilobata) that also grows at home in the Santa Barbara mountains. (Joshua Tree, 3 April 2006)

It's interesting that the Joshua Tree desert is almost due east from our home. This desert is in the rain shadow behind the mountains, but many of our native plants (or kin) grow there as well. The flora is different, but familiar.
Locality: or kin) grow there as well. The flora is different

File.Name: 6408f.jpg
Notes:
13198683 Treebeard and entwife Julie ready to hike from our wilderness camp in Joshua Tree National Park last week on our annual school backpack trip. We're both desert rats at heart, and starting to show it. (Joshua Tree, 4 April 2006)

We've been doing this spring trip for years. Check out:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=9762461
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=7645400

File.Name: 6409a.jpg
Notes:
13211259 I spotted this native Phacelia in the Waterleaf (Hydrophyllaceae) family growing up through another desert shrub. Maybe it's Phacelia crenulata or Phacelia distans? It's still wet with raindrops. It rained all night and most of the day - even some hail or corn snow. That's no fun on a school backpack trip, but it's a treat to see the desert flowers with raindrops! (Joshua Tree, 5 April 2006)

Don't get the wrong idea from these photos. There were few flowers in Joshua Tree last week. I wonder if this late rain will produce later flowers?

File.Name: 6409b.jpg
Notes:
13210930 Strange "flowers" of Green Ephedra aka Mormon Tea (Ephedra viridis) in the Ephedra (Ephedraceae) family. This shrub is a Gymnosperm that is more closely related to pines than the usual Angiosperm flowers with ovaries. They have these soft cones ("strobili") rather than flowers. The only leaves are the dry scales barely visible under the cone on the right. This plant is male with stamens. The female cones are on separate plants. It's an ancient herb for making a stimulating tea. I've read that our North American shrubs do not contain the interesting alkaloid ephedrine, but they must contain something similar. (Joshua Tree, 5 April 2006)

What are those little yellow cups that sure look like flowers below the cone on the right?

File.Name: 6409c.jpg
Notes: 13210700 New flowers of Desert Lotus aka Shrubby Deervetch (Lotus rigidus) in the Pea Family (Fabaceae) still wet with raindrops from our desert storm last week. (Joshua Tree, 5 April 2006)

File.Name: 6409d.jpg
Notes: 13210591 Some kind of desert Popcorn Flower (Cryptantha or Plagiobothrys) in the Borage (Boraginaceae) family. The flowers are engulfed in a rain drop held by the long stiff hairs. It rained all night and most of the day. We even had some hail or corn snow. We were cold and wet on our backpack hike in Joshua Tree National Park last week, but it's a treat to see the desert flowers with raindrops! (Joshua Tree, 5 April 2006)

File.Name: 6409e.jpg
Notes:
13210404 Empty "paper bag" calyces of Bladder Sage aka Paper-bag Bush (Salazaria mexicana) with raindrops in Joshua Tree National Park last week. (Joshua Tree, 5 April 2006)

Too early for flowers, but I have a photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=7651599

File.Name: 6409f.jpg
Notes: 13210233 Strong-smelling Turpentine Broom aka Mohave Desert Rue (Thamnosma montana) in the Pinto Basin at Joshua Tree National Park. This is the same flower as the [Previous] photo with the bee. The two blue-green ovaries are swelling with seeds. They will soon lose their color as the other flower parts fall away. (Joshua Tree, 6 April 2006)

File.Name: 6410a.jpg
Notes:
13228385 Perfect flower of Desert Mallow (Sphaeralcea ambigua) in the Mallow (Malvaceae) family. It's a remarkable orange color like no other native wildflower. We had rain and even some hail or corn snow during our school backpack trip across Joshua Tree National Park last week. Flowers were scarce in the desert this trip, but I found a few to photograph despite the wind and rain. (Joshua Tree, 5 April 2006)

It's gloomy and drizzling today at home too. We're having an unusually cold and wet March/April after an unusually warm and dry January/February. I guess every year is different. The wildflowers I take for granted every year are the survivors who can take it!

File.Name: 6410b.jpg
Notes: 13228187 Native Desert Bluebells (Phacelia campanularia) growing in a rocky desert wash near Optimo Mountain in Joshua Tree National Park. It was cold and rainy on our school backpack hike last week. I only found a few of these beautiful natives in flower hiding in the rocks. (Joshua Tree, 5 April 2006)

File.Name: 6410c.jpg
Notes: 13228125 Tiny little Lotus (Lotus) flower in the Pea Family (Fabaceae). This little flower is almost touching the ground. Wildflowers were scarce on our desert backpack trip last week - but the closer I looked, the more I found. We had rain and even snow. Maybe the flower display is still to come. (Joshua Tree, 5 April 2006)

File.Name: 6410d.jpg
Notes: 13228032 Tiny little composite flowers and plants - look at the sand grains for scale! I call these Goldfields (Lasthenia sp.), but they are probably something else: too many composites, too little time! (Joshua Tree, 6 April 2006)

File.Name: 6410e.jpg
Notes: 13227648 I guess this flowering succulent is a Panamint Liveforever (Dudleya saxosa ssp. aloides) in the Stonecrop Crassulaceae family. It's growing in the rocks under a Singleleaf Pinyon Pine (Pinus monophylla) near the "Trash Cans" climbing site in Joshua Tree National Park. (Joshua Tree, 6 April 2006)

File.Name: 6410f.jpg
Notes: 13227486 New flowers of Desert Sand Verbena (Abronia villosa) growing on an arrested sand dune near the "Trash Cans" climbing site in Joshua Tree National Park. Similar species in the Four O'Clock (Nyctaginaceae) family grow on sand dunes on the beach near home in Santa Barbara. The Joshua Tree desert is a five hour drive almost due east of home. The flora is remarkably similar to home, though species' names change. (Joshua Tree, 6 April 2006)

File.Name: 6411a.jpg
Notes:
13242693 Female Nuttall's Woodpecker (Picoides nuttallii) bird chipping out a nest cavity in a Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the woods at home. Impressive - that wood is hard as a rock, but it makes great firewood. I hope she finds a mate and stays! (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2006)

The male woodpecker of the species has a red crown. This female has two white dots in the black area on top of her back that my bird books don't show.

File.Name: 6411b.jpg
Notes:
13242544 This European Starling bird is nesting in the old Willow (Salix sp.) tree at school on a cold day. These aggresive non-native invaders take over the cavities in the trees and drive away native birds like woodpeckers that depend on those holes for nesting. (Santa Ynez Valley, 30 March 2006)

Where are kids with pellet guns when we really need them?
Kids - learn the birds first!

File.Name: 6411c.jpg
Notes:
13242374 It's easy to recognize this crested Phainopepla (Phainopepla nitens) bird perched on a Catclaw (Acacia greggii) shrub in a desert wash at Joshua Tree National Park last week. It was working the ripe berries of Desert Mistletoe (Phoradendron californicum) nearby when I got too close and spooked it. It didn't fly far, and it's obvious from the mass of seeds stuck on the branches below it that this is a favorite perch. See my [Previous] few photos. (Joshua Tree, 6 April 2006)

I wonder if the Phainopepla birds only feed on this mistletoe? My birder co-worker Art says he's seen them several times in the canyon near Laguna Beach, so I guess not. But I only saw them near the mistletoe in Joshua Tree last week.

File.Name: 6411d.jpg
Notes:
13242079 These are the same ripe berries of Desert Mistletoe (Phoradendron californicum) growing as a parasite on a native Catclaw (Acacia greggii) shrub in a desert wash at Joshua Tree National Park last week. Native Phainopepla (Phainopepla nitens) birds love these berries and shrubs. The pretty birds wipe the sticky seeds from their bills on the branches, and excrete the seeds that have passed through. I think this photo show both methods of seed dispersal. Either way, new mistletoe plants grow on the Catclaws without ever touching the ground. See my [Next] and [Previous] photos. (Joshua Tree, 7 April 2006)

Catclaw is also known as the "Wait-a-minute Bush". The thorns snag my clothes and skin again and again. D'oh, wait a minute!

File.Name: 6411e.jpg
Notes: 13241902 Ripe berries and strange flowers of Desert Mistletoe (Phoradendron californicum) growing as a parasite on native Catclaw (Acacia greggii) in Joshua Tree National Park last week. (Joshua Tree, 2 April 2006)

File.Name: 6411f.jpg
Notes: 13241849 Strange clown-like "flowers" of Desert Mistletoe (Phoradendron californicum) growing as a parasite on native Catclaw (Acacia greggii) in Joshua Tree National Park last week. (Joshua Tree, 2 April 2006)

File.Name: 6412a.jpg
Notes:
13257621 Nature is so strange and disturbing! I'm sure this is a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes) growing from a fallen Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) branch (with lichen) in the woods. I squished one of the tiny "fruit" and it's full of goo - the spores of future slime molds that will "fly mother nature's silver seed to a new home in the sun" to colonize a new fallen branch in the woods. The lucky spores become microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists that will colonize another log until it's time to "flower" and move again. It's a perfect adaptation for a critter that uses up its dead-branch resource and has to move on. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2006)

For more info, see:
   http://www.fotolog.net/treebeard/?photo_id=8764834
and many more in the archive.

File.Name: 6412b.jpg
Notes: 13257615 Strange fruiting bodies of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes) growing from a fallen Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) branch in the woods. Look close and see that the litle wasp (order Hymenoptera) at the top is interested. See my [Next] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2006)

File.Name: 6412c.jpg
Notes: 13257607 A perfect line of Slime Mold (Myxomycophyta) fruiting bodies - maybe Lycogala epidendrum? - on a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. See my next two photos for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2006)

File.Name: 6412d.jpg
Notes: 13257598 Tiny mushroom coming up through the oak leaves - maybe Mycena acicula. It's smaller than a pencil erasor. I was suprised to find these mushrooms so late in the rain-year - our Central California summers are dry - but it's been unusually cold and wet this March/April, and everything is confused. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2006)

File.Name: 6412e.jpg
Notes: 13257521 Intimate dance of a native annual "Red Maids" (Calandrinia breweri in the Purslane (Portulacaceae) family) growing on a recently burned area in the mountains. Note how the 4-parted female pistil is delicately touching some of the ripe male stamens and receiving yellow pollen. I wonder if it rotates to touch the anthers it's missing. Despite all the buzz about birds and bees, I suspect most flowers self-pollinate in a pinch. I can't remember such a cold wet March-April. There are still few insects on the wing in our mountains. The flowers are doing what they have too to make seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2006)

File.Name: 6412f.jpg
Notes: 13257380 Strange new flowers of native Cream-Flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca) in the Poppy (Papaveraceae) family. They are starting to open in a place in the mountains that burned last year. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2006)

File.Name: 6413a.jpg
Notes:
13271058 Elegant native Common Linanthus aka Babystars (Linanthus parviflorus or L. androsaceus) flowers in the Phlox (Polemoniaceae) family. They seem to prefer the serpentine mineral soils which inhibit many other plants. Remarkable colors. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2006)

I explored Figueroa Mountain today looking for something new. I found beautiful flowers, but I had to hunt for them, and there are still very few insects on the wing. Most wildflowers are still below the old ranger station. The double-punch of dry December/February followed by cold wet (and snowy) March/April had an effect. But today it was spring with sweet new flowers!

The forecast is for more rain tonight and tomorrow. One of these storms will be our last until next fall. I'm ready for sun, but I still welcome the late rains for all they bring.
Locality:
and snowy) March/April had an effect. But today it was spring with sweet new flowers!

The forecast is for more rain tonight and tomorrow. One of these storms will be our last until next fall. I'm ready for sun

File.Name: 6413b.jpg
Notes: 13271050 New flowers of Chocolate Lily (Fritillaria biflora) in the Lily (Liliaceae) family. They are growing in the high saddle between Figueroa Mountain and Ranger Peak. The flowers look stressed for good reason - they've been pounded by heavy rain and snow for the last month of this unusual year. I found some plants with flowers still lying flat on the ground after the snow last week, but a few like this one are standing up. Many more are ready to flower. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2006)

File.Name: 6413c.jpg
Notes: 13271037 Elegant Catalina Mariposa Lily (Calochortus catalinae) growing along Figueroa Mountain Road between Midland and the old ranger station. This was the only one I found, but I hope many more will follow. Look close at the tip of the leaf to the right of the base of the flower - there's a tiny beetle enjoying the view. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2006)

File.Name: 6413d.jpg
Notes: 13271034 A wild onion - but what beautiful onion flowers! These are new flowers of native Crinkled Wild Onion (Allium crispum). It really does taste like onion, but I'd rather find it growing along my trail. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2006)

File.Name: 6413e.jpg
Notes: 13271030 Native Sky Lupine (Lupinus nanus) with the sky behind. I don't think these sweet-smelling lupines will cover the hills this year, but they are trying after a hard winter. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2006)

File.Name: 6413f.jpg
Notes: 13271011 Golden native California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) and Bigelow's Coreopsis (Coreopsis bigelovii) in a flower field on Figueroa Mountain. There are some little insects too if you look close. The flowers are just beginning after recent cold and snow. The best action is still below the old ranger station. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2006)

File.Name: 6414a.jpg
Notes:
13282839 Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly working little composite flowers of Goldfields) (Lasthenia californica). All insects - especially butterflies - are still scarce after our unusually cold and wet spring. I saw a few other early butterflies flutter by, but this was the only one that was common. (And the only one I could capture!) It has dull moth-like colors, but the clubs on its antennae prove that it really is a butterfly. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2006)

We had almost 1-1/2 inches of rain today. I never got out with my camera. Just as well. I'm ending "spring break" and I have school work to catch up on before Monday. One of these rains will be our last until autumn. Our plants and critters are confused that it's so wet and cold so late in the year. I am too.

File.Name: 6414b.jpg
Notes:
13282608 We have so many introduced Wild Mustards growing wild in California, so it's always a surprise and a treat to find a real native! I believe this native is Western Wallflower (Erysimum capitatum) in the Mustard (Brassicaceae) family. Several of these were growing in the shade on serpintine mineral soil that inhibits many plants. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2006)

Look close! Like all mustards it has 4 long stamens and two short ones. The little beetle in the lower-right likes it.

File.Name: 6414c.jpg
Notes: 13282486 I guess this is a flower of the South Coast Range Morning-glory (Calystegia collina ssp. venusta). It's an uncommon perennial with woolly leaves and irregularly toothed margins that grows close to the ground. This little Wild Bee (Apoidea) like it. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2006)

File.Name: 6414d.jpg
Notes: 13282305 New male pollen cones on a twig of Gray Pine aka Foothill Pine aka Digger Pine (Pinus sabiniana). They look like exotic tropical fruit up close. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2006)

File.Name: 6414e.jpg
Notes: 13282195 Strange little parasitic Gray Pine Dwarf Mistletoe (Arceuthobium occidentale) sprouts getting ready to flower. They are growing like mushrooms from a living branch of long-needled Gray Pine aka Foothill Pine (Pinus sabiniana). *Sigh* I'll always call this native tree Digger Pine. The old name is now shunned as derogatory towards native Americans in California, but for me it's a compliment. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2006)

File.Name: 6414f.jpg
Notes:
13282042 The inner heart - the sanctum sanctorum - of a Catalina Mariposa Lily (Calochortus catalinae) in the Lily (Liliaceae) family. These native lilies have three large outer petals, and three secret inner petals that hold alternate male stamens with nectar glands around the female column-like style with open stigmas on top. Steamy stuff. Note the two beetle in the lower left and right taking in the view. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2006)

This is the same flower I show three photos [Previous] at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=13271037
This is the only one I found flowering on Figueroa Mountain yesterday

File.Name: 6415a.jpg
Notes:
13295048 Pretty little House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) on the upstairs porch railing singing a blessing for the rising sun! Well, almost. This wren has claimed the birdhouse nearby (see my [Previous] photo) and it's letting the world know with sweet song. This is right outside our bedroom window. It's a fine way to wake up, whether you want to or not! The wrens nest here every year, usually several times. I guess this is a male advertising his snazzy house to attract a mate? (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2006)

I wonder if the Grateful Dead's "Bird Song" tune was inspired by these wrens? If you don't know it, head to the Internet Music Archive at:
   http://www.archive.org/audio/etree.php
and try a search!

File.Name: 6415b.jpg
Notes: 13294924 This House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bird has claimed the birdhouse on our porch and it's letting the world know with song! This is right outside our bedroom window. The wrens nest here every year, usually several times. I guess this is a male advertising his snazzy house to attract a mate? (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2006)

File.Name: 6415c.jpg
Notes: 13294800 White Cream Cups (Platystemon californicus) flower in the Poppy (Papaveraceae) family. This is a very different "California poppy". (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2006)

File.Name: 6415d.jpg
Notes: 13294653 Perfect new flowers of sweet-smelling native Sky Lupine (Lupinus nanus). (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2006)

File.Name: 6415e.jpg
Notes:
13294615 Bright new flower of Bigelow's Coreopsis (Coreopsis bigelovii) in a flower field on Figueroa Mountain. It's a composite flower in the Sunflower (Asteraceae) family. The small individual flowers in the head have "sepals" at the base. The whole flower head has an "involucre" at the base made of "phyllaries" that hold that hold the flower together. In this photo, you can see that this Coreopsis has two kinds of phyllaries - the lower ones thin and spreading, and the upper ones wider and forming a cup. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2006)

See my photo of many Coreopsis flowers at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=13271011
Can you distinguish the different phyllaries?

File.Name: 6415f.jpg
Notes: 13294447 Large Bumblebee (Bombus in the order Hymenoptera) working a flower of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius). Look close and you can see how the weight of the insect hanging on the lower petal of the flower brings the stamens and pistil up to its chest to transfer pollen to or from another flower. (Figueroa Mountain, 13 April 2006)

File.Name: 6416a.jpg
Notes:
13309218 A pair of tiny Weevil beetles (family Curculionidae in order Coleoptera) on a palmate leaf of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) near home. They look like little elephants marching in step. In fact, they were mating a moment ago, but I disturbed them with my camera when I got too close. That's how I feel going back to school tomorrow morning after a week of "Spring Break" vacation getting photos in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2006)

Nah, I'm ready for school - bring it on! But I will be busy building the school slide show in the weeks ahead. I'll post new photos as I can.

File.Name: 6417a.jpg
Notes: 13324693 Wildflowers were scarce on our hike in Joshua Tree National Park, but the Parry's Nolina (Nolina parryi, maybe aka N. wolfii?) are sending up new flower stalks. This is a yucca-like plant that is different from both local yuccas and Joshua Trees. You can see how windy it was! (Joshua Tree, 4 April 2006)

File.Name: 6418a.jpg
Notes: 13339763 Looking close at a Southern Alligator Lizard (Elgaria multicarinatus). It was curled up in a sunbeam in the woods for warmth, like our cat under the window. Neither critter likes being disturbed! What a remarkable eye. I think I see a little yellow Mite (Acari) riding the beast above and slightly left of the eye scross the head, and what is that bump just above its eye? (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2006)

File.Name: 6418b.jpg
Notes: 13339611 Ripe berries of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) near home, with April raindrops. This shrub started flowering in October - our first flowers of the new rain year rather than the last of the old. Now it has some new flowers after our unusually wet spring. I've never seen this shrub flower twice before. Is this one of those "global warming" things - or just another strange year? It seems the closer I look, every year is unique! (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2006)

File.Name: 6418c.jpg
Notes: 13339437 New flowers of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) with raindrops. It's been an unusually wet spring. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2006)

File.Name: 6418d.jpg
Notes: 13339402 Little Wild Bee (Apoidea) working a flower of Yellow-throated Phacelia (Phacelia brachyloba) in the mountains. This plant is a fire-follower growing in a place that burned year. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2006)

File.Name: 6418e.jpg
Notes: 13339341 Chocolate Marasmius (Marasmius fuscopurpureus or kin) mushrooms pushing up through the soggy leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the canyon. These little mushrooms can dry out and revive with new rain. They may have a connection with Lichens that also revive with rain. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2006)

File.Name: 6421a.jpg
Notes:
13348837 A single flower of native Owl's Clover (Castilleja densiflora) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family hiding among the colorful magenta-tipped leafy bracts that make up most of the flower head. (See [Previous] photo.) The flower is a ringer for "Alice the Goon" from EC Segar's classic Popeye comics! (Santa Ynez Valley, 19 April 2006)

I believe this flower is named for the small Burrowing Owls (Speotyto cunicularia?) that like to nest in abandoned gopher and ground squirrel burrows in the same grassy habitat. I used to see whole fields of these flowers on my way to school twenty years ago. Now I'm happy to find a few if I hike into the woods. What about the owls? I guess that's progress.

File.Name: 6421b.jpg
Notes: 13348448 New flowers of native Owl's Clover (Castilleja densiflora) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. The small "real" flowers hide among the colorful magenta-tipped leafy bracts that make up the flower head. (Santa Ynez Valley, 19 April 2006)

File.Name: 6421c.jpg
Notes: 13348266 This Narrow-leaved Owl's Clover (Castilleja attenuata aka Orthocarpus attenuata) was growing in the same meadow as its larger cousin in the [Next] photo. Both plants have small yellowish flowers that hide among the color-tipped leafy bracts, like a small store with a big billboard. (Santa Ynez Valley, 19 April 2006)

File.Name: 6421d.jpg
Notes: 13347849 New flowers of Checker Bloom (Sidalcea malviflora) in the Mallow (Malvaceae) family, growing in the woods near Lake Cachuma. Finding these familiar wildflowers for the first time each new year is like meeting old friends! (Santa Ynez Valley, 19 April 2006)

File.Name: 6421e.jpg
Notes:
13347699 Bristly "Woolly Bear" caterpillar on a grass stem. I believe these are the larvae of Tiger Moths (order Lepitoptera, family Arctiidae). (Santa Ynez Valley, 19 April 2006)

The obvious stumper is "Which end is up?"
(Think about legs and prolegs!)

File.Name: 6421f.jpg
Notes: 13347470 This bristly little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) looks like it's meditating. I'm sure it's keeping at least one of those eyes on me as I try to sneak close! It's hard to capture the metallic colors of this tiny spider. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2006)

File.Name: 6422a.jpg
Notes:
13362417 Northern Flicker aka Red-shafted Flicker (Colaptes auratus) bird perched on the cross-tie of our power pole. Look out little wing! I believe this pretty bird is female. I hear the "wocka-wocka" songs of these birds echoing across the canyon, but it's hard to find them in the woods and get close. In this photo, I'm looking down from the upstairs porch at home. Sometimes the males drum on our porch rail at dawn. I'll try to get that photo! (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2006)

It was gloomy and drizzly all day. That's unusual for late April here on the Pass. I can't remember such a cold and wet March and April. Not much chance for photos today. Just as well. I'm starting to buld my school slide show!

File.Name: 6422b.jpg
Notes: 13362227 I don't know - one of those small songbirds that visit our Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the morning. I guess this is a Hutton's Vireo (Vireo huttoni) with a little caterpillar for breakfast Without these fast little birds, the caterpillars would eat our forest! (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2006)

File.Name: 6422c.jpg
Notes: 13362097 The usual red flowers of native Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) flowering by our mailbox. Look close to see the small tubular flowers hiding in the big flower head of brightly colored bracts to attract pollinators and photographers. See my [Previous] photos for a few close relatives flowering at the same time. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2006)

File.Name: 6422d.jpg
Notes: 13362055 A white form of the usually red-flowered native Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) flowering by our mailbox. See my [Next] photo for the usual red form, and [Previous] photos for close relatives. They all have the same trick of hiding small tubular flowers in a big flower head of brightly colored bracts to attract pollinators. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2006)

File.Name: 6422e.jpg
Notes: 13361979 New flowers of Woolly Paint Brush (Castilleja foliosa) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. It's a close relative of the plants in the [Previous] few photos. It has the same trick of hiding its small yellow tubular flowers in a big flower head of brightly colored bracts to attract pollinators. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2006)

File.Name: 6423a.jpg
Notes:
13374668 Raindrops caught on hairy leaflets of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) and a blade of grass - like crystal spheres hovering in a magic act, with a whole world inside each one. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2006)

Our unusually cold and wet Spring continues with cold drizzle all weekend. One of these storms will be our last rain until Autumn, so I can't complain. But I'm ready for the sun! Today's photos are all native lupines with raindrops. Sure I've done that before, but Ansel Adams didn't just take one photo of Half Dome in Yosemite and say "Got that, what next?" Every photo is new and different!

File.Name: 6423b.jpg
Notes: 13374380 Raindrops on a new flower spike of Lindley's Lupine (Lupinus bicolor) in the canyon. This is our smallest native lupine. This annual is almost a weed that prefers disturbed places along the road. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2006)

File.Name: 6423c.jpg
Notes: 13374223 Flowers of Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the canyon. Many lupines have flowers in whorls - see my [Previous] photo - but the flowers of this annual are spread out one by one along the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2006)

File.Name: 6423d.jpg
Notes:
13374142 Looking down a flower spike of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the canyon after a drizzly weekend. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2006)

The top "banner" petals of these new flowers are folded like little burritos!

File.Name: 6423e.jpg
