Notes: 13374074 Raindrops on a palmate leaf of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the canyon. It was a drizzly weekend - perfect for lupine photos! (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2006)

File.Name: 6423f.jpg
Notes:
13374010 Tiny lupine in the desert, with raindrops. Maybe this is Bajada Lupine (Lupinus concinnus)? Flowers were scarce on our desert hike a few weeks ago, but I probably stepped over many of these tiny lupines before I noticed one with flowers. The whole plant is only an inch or two high. I layed flat on the ground to get this photo. (Joshua Tree, 5 April 2006)

Today's photos are all lupines with raindrops. This one is from the archives. The rest are from my hike in the canyon today after a drizzly weekend.

File.Name: 6424a.jpg
Notes:
13391184 Delicate white flowers of native Woodland Star (Lithophragma affine) in the Saxifrage (Saxifragaceae) family - with raindrops. The flowers are widely spaced on long stalks with a few alternate leaves that sway in the slightest breeze. It's a challenge to photograph. This shot is looking down the long stem with other flowers below. I held my breath! (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2006)

This is just another uncommon beautiful native plant with white flowers in the woods. I wonder what would happen if it disappeared after a few bad years with disturbance or no seeds. It's not a common plant, so I don't think the woodland food chain would collapse from just this casualty. I'm sure some critters would miss it - I know I would - but we'd all get by with other attractions. It's a real question: does biodiversity matter?

File.Name: 6424b.jpg
Notes: 13391105 New flowers of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) family growing in the canyon. This shrub is kin to our native Ceanothus and Coffeeberry shrubs in the same family. The spiny leaves look like just another scrub oak , but it has these real flowers instead of catkins, and it will produce bright red berries. The flowers seem to have four little yellow petals - but that's all it has, so they are really sepals if you care about the difference. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2006)

File.Name: 6425a.jpg
Notes: 13405028 A cluster of tiny dark-spored mushrooms in an unwatered field at school. The lawnmower man trims the fields every week - a "buzz cut" for sure - but some plants and mushrooms make do with a low ceiling. The plants are a Filaree (Erodium sp.) weed in the Geranium (Geraniaceae) family. I guess the mushrooms are Coprinus or maybe Panaeolus) - they don't seem to deliquesce. They look purplish in groups. (Santa Ynez Valley, 25 April 2006)

File.Name: 6426a.jpg
Notes: 13422131 Interesting native Fly (order Diptera) resting on a leaf in the woods. I have no time tonight to search my books. Its abdomen reminds me of those toy Star Wars light sabers that can collapse and extend! (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2006)

File.Name: 6427a.jpg
Notes:
13434117 There are still new mushrooms in the woods. I guess this is a Cortinarius in the subgenus Phlegmacium with slimy cap and dry stem. This should be the end of our wet season, but the rains keep coming. I can't complain - the hot dry buggy summer will be here soon enough! (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2006)

It's been an unusually cool and wet spring. When I got home from school this afternoon, a swallowtail butterfly fluttered by, the first I've seen this year. It didn't pose for a photo, but it's a sign of new life to come. I'm ready!

File.Name: 6427b.jpg
Notes: 13433916 Another unusual Fly (order Diptera) resting on a leaf in the woods. Compare my [Previous] photo. What big eyes you have! (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2006)

File.Name: 6428a.jpg
Notes:
13449101 Even the most common weeds hold surprises. This little yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is waiting for prey in a flower head of English Plantain (Plantago lanceolata) along the road. The male staminate flowers open first on the bottom with female pistillate flowers above. The ring of pollen-bearing stamens moves up as the flower matures. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2006)

Look close at the spider - it holds a great illusion. At first I see it reaching around the flower into the background. When I look close, I see a witch with red lips and a red-striped skirt reaching out for me!

File.Name: 6428b.jpg
Notes: 13449093 Beautiful lily-like flowers of Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis - aka F. lanceolata or F. affinis). This is an uncommon native in the Lily (Liliaceae) family that is endemic to our local mountains. (CNPS 1B.2) I want to crawl inside! (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2006)

File.Name: 6428c.jpg
Notes:
13449087 This is a Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly perched on a branch of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis). (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2006)

But it's stranger than that. I'm looking straight down with my camera mounted on tripod. The butterfly is laying sideways, parallel to the ground. When I looked around, I found five more butterflies in exactly the same pose. At first I thought they had all become prey for spiders, especially since the abdomens of all the butterflies were not visible. But I realized they all had their abdomens tucked up between their wings. So strange!

It was foggy with a bit of drizzle when I took this photo. I've been wondering what little butterflies do in bad weather, and I guess this is it - at least for Silvery Blues. But why do they lay sideways? Maybe so they look like dead leaves or bird droppings?

The closer I look, the more I don't understand!

File.Name: 6429a.jpg
Notes:
13462281 I'm looking straight down on this Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly hanging on to a Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) leaf in the canyon. It's hanging from the leaf sideways with wings parallel to the ground. Now I guess it's lying flat like a solar collector trying to warm up - hard on such a gloomy day as today. (Thanks Marion! ) (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2006)

It's been a strange weather year - but maybe they all are! I can't remember such a cold and wet March and April and now almost May. Flowers and butterflies (and other insects) are confused and out of sync. The banana slugs love it!

File.Name: 6429b.jpg
Notes: 13462041 Pretty mushroom with dark cap and upturned white gills poking up through the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the canyon today. Almost May, and there are still new mushrooms in the woods! It's been an unusually cool and wet spring. Flowers, insects, and mushrooms are all confused! (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2006)

File.Name: 6429c.jpg
Notes: 13461946 Ripe moss (Bryophyta) sporophytes bearing moss seeds. I can not tell a lie. They are growing on the concrete lid over an access point to our shared water system in the canyon. Maybe there's a small leek that keeps them happy. (San Marcos Pass, 29 April 2006)

File.Name: 6429d.jpg
Notes: 13461865 New flowers of native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) in the woods. It's a classic hummingbird flower. Flowers in many different plant families have converged to this form with long hanging floral tubes and extended sexual parts. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2006)

File.Name: 6429e.jpg
Notes: 13461769 New flowers of native Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus). The leaves have three veins on the underside. Terrible name for the most beautiful blue-flowered Ceanothus shrubs close to home. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2006)

File.Name: 6429f.jpg
Notes: 13461691 Flower head of weedy English Plantain (Plantago lanceolata) along the road. The male staminate flowers open first on the bottom with female pistillate flowers above. The ring of pollen-bearing stamens reminds me of a planetarium for a much busier solar system. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2006)

File.Name: 6430a.jpg
Notes:
13474905 Strange flowers of native Cream-flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca) in the flowers of native Cream-Flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca), with rain drops. This is a fire-follower growing in a place in the mountains that burned last year. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2006)

The sun came out today, and I saw some butterflies flutter by: Western Tiger Swallowtail, Sara Orange-tip, unknown Blues, and maybe a green Bramble Hairsteak. You know those mind-photos you get when you see a moment so clearly... I missed them all fiddling with my camera. Maybe next time.

File.Name: 6430b.jpg
Notes: 13474592 I think of this as the real "California Poppy" - native Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa), with fog drops under the petals. The official California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) is deep orange and has a large disk at the base of the flower. CalTrans and well-minding people have scattered seed to make it almost a weed. Away from the roads, I mostly find Eschscholzia caespitosa in our mountains.
Locality: Eschscholzia californica) is deep orange and has a large disk at the base of the flower. CalTrans and well-minding people have scattered seed to make it almost a weed. Away from the roads

File.Name: 6430c.jpg
Notes: 13474261 Pretty Suncups (Cammisonia sp., maybe C. intermedia or C. hirtella?). It's in the Evening Primrose (Onagraceae family, and the species are really hard to tell apart. The 8 stamens (4 long & 4 short), capitate pistle, and petal markings are like a clock face. It is a fire-follower growing in a place that burned last year. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2006)

File.Name: 6430d.jpg
Notes: 13474140 Yellow-throated Phacelia (Phacelia brachyloba) flowers in the mountains waiting for pollinators. How do the magenta lines in each flower know to brach the same intricate way? (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2006)

File.Name: 6430e.jpg
Notes: 13474089 New flower of native Giant-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora). It's a fire-follower growing on a fire break that was burned after clearing last year. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2006)

File.Name: 6501a.jpg
Notes:
13489997 A maypole for May Day deep inside a flower of native Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa). The genders are reversed from tradition. This "pole" is the famale style with four receptive stigmas reaching down to the male pollen-bearing stamens gathered around the base. We all learn about the birds and the bees in school, but I think most flowers can pollinate themselves when they have to. It's been an unusually cool and wet spring and insect pollinators are still scarce. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2006)

May Day is a workers' day and now an Immigrants' day. But it has deep roots as one of the cross-quarter days midway between vernal equinox and summer solstice. Interesting how the ancient holidays get recycled again and again. I have a stumper about this at:
   http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/05feb99a.html

File.Name: 6501b.jpg
Notes:
13489762 More of a mosh than a maypole, but this seems appropriate for May Day. This photo shows male stamens in a flower of native Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa). The official "California Poppy" (Eschscholzia californica) is more orange and has a large disk at the base of the flower. I mostly find E. caespitosa scattered in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2006)

The old name for this flower is maybe "copa de oro" or "cup of gold". Good name.

File.Name: 6501c.jpg
Notes:
13489731 Another natural maypole for May Day, though this "pole" is the female style and the "dancers" are the male pollen-bearing stamens inside this flower of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the chaparral. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2006)

Yellow is the hardest color to capture with a digital camera!

File.Name: 6501d.jpg
Notes:
13489577 Six male stamens doing their own May Day dance around the three-parted female pistil in a flower of native Chaparral Zygadene aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus). The juicy-looking green glands at the base of the petals are typical of the local variety in our mountains. It's a monocot in the Lily (Liliaceae) family. It's not common, so don't eat it - and it has toxic alkaloids. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2006)

To see how the pollination works, check out:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=7669357

File.Name: 6501e.jpg
Notes:
13489332 A maypole for May Day. This pole is a stalk of native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia) entwined with vines of native Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata) like a magic beanstalk. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2006)

May Day is a workers' day and an Immigrants' day. But it has deep roots as one of the cross-quarter days midway between vernal equinox and summer solstice. Interesting how the ancient holidays get recycled again and again.

File.Name: 6502a.jpg
Notes: 13506983 Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) on the wall along the garden on the first sunny day we've had for a while. What are those orange marks under its feet? (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2006)

File.Name: 6503a.jpg
Notes: 13507078 This male Wilson's Warbler (Wilsonia pusilla) bird has captured a male drone Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) for breakfast. We've been fighting the ants for weeks in our house this year. For some reason, the winged males collect in our bathtub every morning so I have to sweep them out before my shower. This black-capped warbler is my friend! (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2006)

File.Name: 6504a_6503-122.jpg
Notes:
Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly perched for the night on a blade of grass in the woodland near Lake Cachuma. After I noticed this one, I saw many more of these butterflies hugging different leaves as the sun went down. When it's colder, they lay flat like little solar panels and tuck their abdomens in between their wings. They really are blue-colored on top, but I only see it when they fly. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 May 2006)

Bad computer caca last night. I tried to prepare these same photos late last night when *something happened* and my home "fotolog" directory disappeared. I tried Norton Utilities and several more tricks and "undelete" programs with no luck. The original photos are good, and I had a backup of my fotolog photos from several weeks ago. I can retrieve all the other recent photos and comments from fotolog, one by one, and rebuild my database. But why didn't I back them up last week to save hours/days of work??

Stuff happens - ALWAYS HAVE A BACKUP (or two)!

File.Name: 6504b_6503-130.jpg
Notes:
A pair of little Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterflies settling in for the night on a leaf of Italian Thistle (Carduus pycnocephalus) near Lake Cachuma as the sun goes down. At least this is one good use of this horrible weed that invades our woodland. These two blues have very different backgroud colors. I don't know if that's normal variation or a gender thing. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 May 2006)

These butterflies are resting upright. When it's cold, they lay flat like solar collectors to catch the sun rays. Compare my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/?pid=13462281

File.Name: 6504c_6503-131.jpg
Notes: Little Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly perched for the night on a leaf of Italian Thistle (Carduus pycnocephalus) near Lake Cachuma as the sun goes down. These early butterflies really are blue-colored on the upper side of their wings. They show some color underneath too. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 May 2006)

File.Name: 6504d_6503-118.jpg
Notes:
Butts-up view of a little Wild Bee (Apoidea) snuggling into a native flower of Checker Bloom (Sidalcea malviflora) in the Mallow (Malvaceae) family. The flower will close for the night with the bee inside. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 May 2006)

Anyone else remember Robert Heinlein's classic book "Red Planet" when the boys survive the frigid Martian night inside the cabbage-like plant that closes up to protect them against the cold?

File.Name: 6504e_6430-074.jpg
Notes: I know this isn't quite in focus, but it's part of an on-going story. A little House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) bird has claimed the bird house on our upstairs porch. It sings all day outside our bedroom window, starting REALLY EARLY in the morning. This wren in the photo showed up with a beak-full of nesting materials, but the porch wren chased it away! They repeated this episode several times. I'm confused by House Wren gender relations. Is this a male with an offering being rejected (or teased) by the resident female? (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2006)

File.Name: 6504f_6430-080.jpg
Notes: The same little male Wilson's Warbler (Wilsonia pusilla) bird in the [Previous] photo after finishing his breakfast ant inside the canopy of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. A moment of peace. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2006)

File.Name: 6505a_6505-257.jpg
Notes:
Non-native Wild Mustard (Brassica sp. - mostly Black Mustard (Brassica nigra?) covering the hills along Santa Rosa Road between Buellton and Lompoc. The legend is that the mustard plants were introduced by Franciscan Father Serra to mark the road between the California missions. I guess he didn't have to do an "Environmental Impact Analysis" about spreading those alien seeds! The wild mustards are pretty, but they cover the hillsides like this and inhibit the natives. I prefer diversity. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 May 2006)

We had our annual DMS "Piece of Cake" school bike ride today from Buellton to Lompoc and back, 40+ miles on a perfect sunny day! I found time to notice the flowers and take a few photos along with 100+ biking photos - and I finished the ride!

File.Name: 6505b_6505-267.jpg
Notes: This orange-flowered Wallflower (Erysimim capitatum - maybe ssp. lompocense?) is the real native coastal mustard in our part of California. It's not common, unlike the non-native introduced mustards in the [Next] photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 May 2006)

File.Name: 6505c_6505-294.jpg
Notes: I guess this is Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) growing along the Santa Rosa Road approaching Lompoc. These flower heads seem bigger and brighter than our plants at home. The real flowers are the green tubes that stick out of the bright red leafy-bracts. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 May 2006)

File.Name: 6505d_6505-249.jpg
Notes: Some kind of pretty Evening Primrose (Oenothera sp.) growing along Santa Rosa Road on our school bike ride today. I'm not sure what it is - could it be Oenothera californica which is usually a desert plant? (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 May 2006)

File.Name: 6505e_6505-295.jpg
Notes: White-flowered "Whitewhorl Lupine" Lupinus densiflorus var. lacteus along the road. Now it's known as Lupinus microcarpus var. densiflorus. Whatever we call it, this lupine has lazy white or yellow whorled flower spikes that mostly grow sideways. I haven't found this lupine near home, but it's common along roads and trails in the valley. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 May 2006)
ScientificName: Lupinus densiflorus; lacteus; Lupinus microcarpus; densiflorus
associatedTaxa: Lupinus densiflorus; lacteus; Lupinus microcarpus; densiflorus

File.Name: 6505f_6505-277.jpg
Notes: Prayer flags in the Santa Rosa hills. Thanks Steve, there's lots to pray for. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 May 2006)

File.Name: 6506a_6505-256.jpg
Notes:
On the road with brilliant yellow Wild Mustards (Brassica sp. - mostly Black Mustard (Brassica nigra?) covering the hills along Santa Rosa Road between Buellton and Lompoc. I took this photo while pedaling on our annual DMS "Piece of Cake" school bike ride from Buellton to Lompoc and back, 40+ miles on a perfect sunny day! This is a step further back from the [Previous] two photos. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 May 2006)

The wild mustards are beautiful, but non-native. It's a stumper how these non-native plants can dominate the landscape when the natives have had millenia to adapt.

File.Name: 6506b_6505-256.jpg
Notes: Brilliant yellow Wild Mustard (Brassica sp. - mostly Black Mustard (Brassica nigra?) covering the hills along Santa Rosa Road between Buellton and Lompoc. This is a step back from the [Previous] photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 May 2006)

File.Name: 6507a_6507-001.jpg
Notes:
Rufous-sided Towhee (Pipilo erythrophthalmus) bird in the catkin-flowering Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) near our upstairs deck. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2006)

No time for photos today, I'm too busy sequencing photos for the DMS school slide show in a few weeks. It's a big job. Checking Exif info, I find I'm taken 17,047 photos since school started on Septrmber 6, 2005, and I've kept about 7,200 photos of school stuff and my nature stuff. Now it's it's time to make a slide show!

File.Name: 6507b_6507-004.jpg
Notes: Pin-size blue Damselfly (Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) resting on a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf. Small preditor for small prey. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2006)

File.Name: 6507c_6507-005.jpg
Notes: Pin-size blue Damselfly (Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) resting on a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf. "Them there eyes" - in photos at least, the compound eyes always have a dark "eyeball" that follows me. How does this work? (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2006)

File.Name: 6508a_6508-006.jpg
Notes: Elegant nodding buds of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) - a kind of Mariposa Lily - catching the last light of the day. No flowers yet, but it won't be long. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2006)

File.Name: 6508b_6508-011.jpg
Notes: Even the humble brambles - this is a flower of the native California Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) - look beautiful in the last light as the sun goes behind the trees. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2006)

File.Name: 6509a_6505-279.jpg
Notes: Plastic flowers (Narcissus artificialis etc.) on the wall of Steve's house in the Santa Rosa Hills. Note the tin can lid covering a knothole on the other side. There are artists in the local mountains as well as mushrooms and wildflowers. Thanks Steve! (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 May 2006)

File.Name: 6509b_6505-284.jpg
Notes: Another little outbuilding for the washing machine and water heater at Steve's house in the Santa Rosa Hills. There are artists in the local mountains along with the mushrooms and wildflowers. Thank's Steve! (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 May 2006)

File.Name: 6509c_6505-285.jpg
Notes: Beautiful little outbuilding at Steve's house in the Santa Rosa Hills. Of course there's an airplane on the roof and salamanders on the wall. You never know what you'll find in the local mountains! (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 May 2006)

File.Name: 6510a_6510-016.jpg
Notes:
Watch out, Amy - the poppy fields of Oz are right outside your door!

These flowers are California Poppies (Eschscholzia californica), planted at school. It's hard to tell what's native any more when so many of these plants are sown from seed packets. (Santa Ynez Valley, 10 May 2006)

File.Name: 6510b_6510-033.jpg
Notes: Apple blossom time! These new Apple (Malus pumila) flower buds are colored like roses, appropriate since it's in the Rosaceae family. This apple tree appeared 20 years ago when we built our home from a spit seed or tossed core. We always get a few tart green apples that the birds miss. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2006)

File.Name: 6510c_6510-037.jpg
Notes: Our native Canyon Sunflowers (Venegasia carpesioides) are finally starting to flower, but they are still waiting for butterflies - like me. This photo is from behind with the setting sun in front. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2006)

File.Name: 6511a_6511-060.jpg
Notes: Yin-yang Western Fence Lizards (Sceloporus occidentalis) on the concrete wall near the garden. I know I shouldn't anthropomorphize, but I guess the dark lizard on the bright top of the wall is male, and the lighter lizard on the dark face of the wall is female. "He" was definitely trying to approach "her". The darned photographer interrupted the intimate moment. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2006)

File.Name: 6511b_6511-058.jpg
Notes:
Little "True Bugs" (order Hemiptera) acting like "love bugs" on the small flower buds of native California Figwort aka Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Figwort (Scrophulariaceae) family. I've seen these little bugs before. They seem to be specific to this plant. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2006)

Insects are still scarce, but it's finally warming up.
CommonNames: True Bugs; California Figwort; Bee Plant (Scrophularia californica) in the Figwort

File.Name: 6511c_6511-044.jpg
Notes: I had a termite (order Isoptera) hatch in my classroom at school today. When I opened the back door, they streamed towards the light across the concrete floor and flew away over the curb. I wish I knew where they came from! (Santa Ynez Valley, 11 May 2006)

File.Name: 6512a_6512-098.jpg
Notes:
Apple blossom time! New Apple (Malus pumila) flower and rose-like buds in the garden. A pair of these apple trees appeared 20+ years ago when we built our home from a spit seed or tossed core. We always get a few of the tart green apples that the birds miss. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2006)
